text:
a stray cat often comes to our screen door and hisses at Sandal. 

but she's not mean: she's scared. she's had a tough life and Sandal is big. 

but yesterday, Sandal saw through it. he ignored the hissing. he sat there until they were friends. 

this orange boy will save the world.

I'd have to think that, for dogs, at least part of the appeal of dragging your ass on the ground after you take a shit is that it feels like the shit is getting on the ground, plus the shit is coming off of your ass. Win/win for dogs, & I gotta imagine the ground ain't too mad either. Hey, free shit

Dogs have quantum properties. Everyone says their dog is the 'best dog'. Even if they have more than one dog. And everyone is correct, simultaneously. 

A very similar thing also applies to cats. Just swap 'best dog' for 'biggest arsehole'

The cat and dog have a mutually beneficial arrangement where the dog barks his head off whenever the cat wants anything (food, to be let out/in) and then gets a snack as a finder's fee. I have no idea how this situation has developed but I can't say no to either of them.

people who come out as furry to their coworkers (or their parents) are wild to me. like why. why do they have to know. what possible benefit could it be to you, to have to explain something like that to Susan from Accounting

cats have no respect, or need, for our legal system. imagine a cat hiring a lawyer. you cannot. it is ridiculous. have you ever seen a cat in a law office? Nope. 

cats do not need laws. if they have a grievance, they have claws to take care of it. that is their law.

no, rachel, your dog does not want 1 million likes on your instagram page for their birthday. they want you to put your fucking phone down and play with them. goddamn. 

but i’m gonna like it anyway though

a sweet one-eared cat came by my porch last night as I was putting out trash and recycling. they were so friendly and cuddly, i ended up shredding some turkey for them. I picked them up after they ate (dicey i know) and they immediately just wanted to ride around on my shoulder like a parrot ; _ ;

After almost 48 hours in Istanbul the cat-count stands at NINETY-SIX. I’m not sure the count was always entirely accurate & scientific—I wouldn’t want Data Colada to peer too closely at my (non-existent) notes. But averaging two cats per hour (even when asleep) is very good. What an excellent city.

Got home yesterday to find that the partner had set off the fire alarm while cooking a veggie sausage, the dog freaked out and pooped on the floor. The alarm only stopped after being plunged into a bucket of water. The dog received half the sausage in compensation. And now we have an alarmaquarium.

OKAY OKAY the cat was on the desk, meowing (in the "I want to play!" register) and when I went to touch his head he gave me a play-gnaw on the forearm, and I said, "USE YOUR WORDS, BUDDY" and he REALLY SEEMED TO -- PERHAPS IT WAS AN ILLUSION -- press the PLAY button on purpose!

At least 95% of my communication with my dogs is non-verbal.  So much communication through taking/yielding space, body positioning/intent, head movement and eye monitoring.  Only when they're out of eye contact are verbal commands really more useful.

If I were God, even for just one day, one of the first things I'd do is give back everyone's old dogs, if they wanted them.

But they would be healthy, with youth restored and they'd remember all the love they'd been given... they'd remember You.

Wouldn't that be something?

Today's random memory: the time when I was home from school, so sick I could barely move, and the cat suddenly jumped up on me and gave birth to her kittens right there on my chest. This was not the first time I'd seen animal birth so I wasn't horrified, just confused why I was chosen for this.

if you have a mild allergy to your cat that you don't want getting any worse the best advice I can pass along is aggressively lint roll your underwear the second you take them out of the dryer. keeping cat hair out of your eyes and nose is obvious but most people dont even consider their other Holes

Nerd alert: Fresh dog poop cannot be used safely on crops that will come in contact with it. For things like fruit trees/canes, the pathogens in dog poop are not the concern (as far as I know) but it is a highly acidic fertilizer. Hot composting is the best way to use dog/cat poop as fertilizer.

Thanks all, the vet completely terrified me that Sebs had a clot and a heart condition & I was preparing for his final days but then she did X-rays & it’s more likely that he has osteoarthritis in his spine & an inflamed disc. He got a shot & painkillers & is now sequestered in my bathroom.

I was never “prescribed” one but my cat Noodle is basically mine. When I’m having a panic attack or crying, she runs in and lays on my chest, patting my face or neck with her paw while she purrs. She’s stopped so many anxiety attacks before they turned into full blown panics.

Went for haircut. There’s a dog there. “Is he getting a hair cut?” I say. Hairdresser laughs politely.

Another customer enters, points at the dog. “Is he getting a trim?” Hairdresser laughs politely.

3rd customer enters. “Is he getting his hair cut?” 

“No,” says hairdresser.

“Just we two” Postcard from my collection. Copyright 1907, mailed 1909. This photo is by C.E. Bullard - he's my favorite of the Victorian/Edwardian cat photographers because he showed cats being relaxed and naturally beautiful, not dressed up in costumes and forced into anthropomorphic poses.

Fun fact: the Kemetic word for cat is believed to be Miu, which is where a lot of scholars believe we get many of the cat noises that are popular in modern day language such as meow. Additionally the people of Kemet honored cats so much that it was illegal to bring harm to one.

#kemeticism #history

Yesterday one of our dog became lethargic.
We rushed to the emergency vet.
Diagnostic: Acute pericardial effusion. 
Today, after two procedures, she's stable.
The cause: someone poisoned her.
I'm so angry and so sad.

i had to say goodbye to my cat two weeks ago. the acute grief has passed, now i just get pangs. but i do still find myself doing things i used to do to accommodate her, like i just took a bunch of stuff off a chair she liked to sleep on while i worked. and i still watch for her in my periphery.

Bjork and Radiohead have had oddly similar careers:

1993—Decent but primitive debuts (Debut and Pablo Honey)

1995—Much better follow-ups (Post and The Bends)

1997—Landmark creative breakthroughs (Homogenic and OK Computer)

The Rubaiyat of Dorothy Ashby is my favorite jazz record. Basing lyrics on the poetry of Persian polymath Omar Khayyam, harp virtuoso Ashby plays the Japanese koto with equal talent and combines many musical influences for a truly novel listening experience with the feeling of divine revelation.

There was a Bee Gees documentary that talked about how record companies used to ship masters to Australia. Records were pressed locally and sold in plain white sleeves. The brothers learned the music they loved was by Black Americans when they arrived in England and saw the actual album covers.

In 2023, I sold a novel. Other People’s Husbands, expected in fall 2025.  It is a queer campus novel inspired in part by my visiting writer era — 2002-2016 — at nine colleges and universities, including my two alma maters. I started this novel to make myself laugh during a hard time and here we are.

What a charming little book! The characters were memorable and had great chemistry between the leads. Very cozy vibes, which was exactly what I was looking for. Had none of the gay angst so common in historical romance. I liked it more than We Could Be So Good. 5/5 ⭐️ 🌈📚

My third book of the year was Redshirts, by John Scalzi. It's a classic parody, but also critique of writers and the people who make money on them, an existential tome of philosophy, and an examination of interpersonal relationships.

what I’ve been reading: 

LEECH by Hiron Ennes. My god, what a perfectly executed Gothic horror - identity slippage, awful house, exquisitely tragic beautiful boy. Will buy anything Ennes writes from now on.

I absolutely devoured @user's 'Red Rabbit' on the flight home today. It reads like a horror-fueled 'The Sisters Brothers,' a fever dream of cannibalism, witchcraft, black magic, and lycanthropy mixed with the the best parts of the American West. It's an ASTONISHINGLY good read.

I've left Mars and am off to Venus in Edgar Rice Burroughs' PIRATES OF VENUS—the first in his Amptor series.

One thing's for sure about Burroughs' protagonists—they always learn the language of whatever alien culture they find themselves in laughable fast.

Three weeks to total fluency? Bullshit.

2023 Book #65) @user Wanderers 

YIKES: a fungal pandemic, fascist militia types, and a creative use of a supersentient AI. Tensely plotted, well researched medical stuff, great characters; sat home all afternoon to read the last 400 pages.  Loved

COLD, BLACK, & INFINITE is the new collection by @user and it's a fitting title. Dark forces permeate here, shaping fates with little concern for humanity. These stories contain cosmic chaos, but the REAL horrors reside within people's choices, realizations, and retributions.

Okay, I think i'm gonna wrap this thread up with a favorite of mine. It's arguably not horror, but deeply horrifying, so

It's Ken Russell's The Devils. I've never seen a better treatise on power and the church. It's scathing, it's gorgeous, and it's got hot priest Oliver Reed 🥵

THE MISCHIEVOUS LETTERS OF THE MARQUISE DE Q cover! Sunshiny rich girl who's actually full of rage + androgynous playboy novelist with secret broken heart + a man who needs a hug SO bad, set in magic 1820s Paris, told in letters, these bisexuals fall in love and do VENGEANCE books2read.com/u/3k6gX8

Just finished the Bradshaw Variations by Rachel Cusk, my favorite living novelist. While max witty, she so fully, subtly, beautifully integrates form and content, big ideas and small observations, that you can't rip out a few lines to exploit on social media without destroying the meaning. #reading

Finished reading this last night, based on the cover I wasn't expecting a deep dive into the complexities of friendship & grief. There's a difficult twist early in the book that really threw me & I almost abandoned it (I can't do grief plots right now) but glad I stuck with it, enjoyed it very much.

He Who Drowned The World by @user is brilliant, staggering, and hurtful. It, even more than SWBTS, is an incredibly nuanced exploration of gender in an excellent political fantasy. It is brutal, but it has a light shining through. Just fantastic.

Being overlooked & underestimated often helped women get the job done during the world wars. 
But we shouldn’t overlook them now. 
My 3 published books, + #AgentZo out in May, tell some of their stories. 
All great Christmas reads! 
Ps, one of those women tried to kill Hitler…

PROMISE is another SUPERB batch of strange, eerie, unearthly stories from @user. Weird futures, fantastical technologies, and unusual histories populate these worlds where Reality shimmers like a mirage of a dream: it may seem similar to ours, but it's never quite the same.

There are some stunning titles on Waterstones best nature and travel books of 2023 list but it’s wild that this by @user isn’t one of them. Warm, moving, not too precious, funny, approachable, absorbing and references Calvin and Hobbes. Put it on your Christmas list.

there may never be a more glorious review of Wrath Goddess Sing than this:

"you gotta understand that if the song of achilles is tap water then wrath goddess sing is mcdonalds sprite. if TSOA is a pleasant but watery iced tea then this book is gasoline laced with crack"

“A late entry for the year’s most interesting novel.” 

Me on Mike McCormack’s This Plague of Souls, a work of exceptional originality and beauty which prompted me to think, above all else: “imagine if all writers took this much trouble.”

So @user/Michelle West's latest book is just out. Hunter's Redoubt. Latest book in the long looong interlinked series of series of political fantasies stretching back to the 90s.

Never had the highest profile but just hugely good. Think "what if Robin Hobb wrote Song of Ice and Fire"

Finished MENEWOOD by @user this week, and it's such a wonderful follow-up to HILD! I love large books that I can just get lost in, not to mention historical fiction with characters who still come across as fresh and alive and *human* despite being from more than a thousand years ago.

If you're more in the mood for a languorous, atmospheric tone poem with excellent characters, and deeply unsettling imagery and themes, look no further than Na-Hong Jin's 2016 powerhouse, The Wailing. 

But be in the right mood for it, or it will not reward you.

You have to get your hands on this amazing book by @user. It is so fun, funny and weird - in the best possible ways - and somehow also manages to be an unflinching examination of trauma and possible ways of healing. I absolutely loved it!

Pleased to finish another A+ piece of #horrorlit - White Horse by @user. 

It is a story of generational trauma and bringing the ugly facts lost to history into the light. Beautifully written and emotional with a varied cast, unique plot, and a satisfying conclusion. Recommended📚😱

THE PALE HOUSE DEVIL is an excellent novella full of @user's token "heroes" with questionable morals and bleak senses of humor. It's a full-throttle horror story with a melting pot of folklore and unspeakable monsters. I'm hopeful we'll see more of these characters. 🕸️📚

Just finished @user's All The Hidden Paths, excellent followup to A Strange and Stubborn Endurance. Very good political fantasy, and an interesting meditation on what it means to be out of the closet when you never even imagined that might have been a possibility.

just finished AMERICAN RAPTURE by my bud CJ LEEDE and it is just so dang incredible. shocking and thoughtful and VERY smart. BIG TIME trigger warning for sexual violence, but if you can handle this and are an extreme horror buckaroo this should be on your list us.macmillan.com/books/978125...

Read and enjoyed "Zero Days" by Ruth Ware. It's a thriller with a pen tester as protagonist, set in the UK. Enjoyed it a lot, and consumed it in one hit on the plane ride from Nashville to Houston. Refreshingly free of "characters do stupid things to further the plot".

Justin Torres reviewed by @user: "The supreme pleasure of the book is its slow obliteration of any firm idea of reality — a perfect metaphor for the delirious disorientation that comes with learning queer history as an adult."

« My Name is Red » is the work of Orhan Pamuk. The novel is both a detective story and a love story. However, it‘s above all a 16th-century transposition of contemporary Turkish society, torn between its traditions and its aspiration to mimic the Western model.

This is a really wonderful collection of essays. Some are familiar, and some are new, and they amount to an excellent, accessible, beautifully designed snapshot of current critical thought on Fantasy. Firm recommend - maybe a lovely Christmas gift?

Well, just powered to the end of Fiona McFarlane’s *The Sun Walks Down*. One of the more exceptional novels I've read in a while. Beautiful writing and genuinely acute insight into characters … and also into the Australian desert landscapes … in a tense, missing child plot. Major recommendation.

Decided to try join others in keeping a thread of everything I’ve read this year. Book 1 of 2024! Amazing stuff here on Black activism in the Midwest and North at the state level before the Civil War, federalism, contested definitions of citizenship, and the struggle for civil rights.  Great read!

Periodic PSA: _Any_ HWA member can add works to the 2023 Stoker Recommended Reading List. A work needs 8 recs for a chance to be on the preliminary ballot (without the jury), so send in your rec even if they are already on the list.

Dolly Parton has probably done as much as any other living person to promote childhood literacy.

If you have or know kids who could use free books, you can check here to see if her Imagination Library program is available in your area. If it isn't yet available you can sign up for a notification.

BETWIXT from Viz was a great spooky read, right up my alley with the mix of surreal tales of doppelgangers & eerie spirits. Really liked the idea of having the book read in both directions with two covers by Junji Ito that themselves tie together & reference each other.

scott nicolay's most recent collection, 'and at my back i always hear,' is truly one of the best collections in recent years and i can think of few writers able to explore the way that regret and memory can deform consciousness (and, thus, the very notions of time, place, and the world.)

Emily Carroll’s horror can be seen to draw upon a longstanding tradition in Canadian literature called “the garrison mentality” in order to create impossible situations of tension, anxiety, and of a dangerous world that really wants to kill you. 1/9

My Friday read is “Breaking Water” by @user. I’ve read and reread this piece over the years, and each time I stumble upon something new to grapple with. Nuanced, horrifying, profane, delicate, and gorgeous in equal parts, I can’t begin to describe how astounding this story is.

Black Mischief is one of those ‘secondary’ Waugh novels—like Put Out More Flags—that I’ve never felt the urge to reread. But here Will Lloyd makes the case for it as the novel where “Waugh discovers that he is serious.”

My god, Zadie Smith is good at this. I don’t always feel that her novels fully work—Swing Time, for example, I think is ultimately pretty unconvincing—but sentence to sentence, paragraph to paragraph, she just PULLS you. Her novels are so, well, novel-y, in the best way.

Just finished Malcolm Lowry’s first novel, Ultramarine. A bit purple, and fascinating regarding how much a writer insists on getting out of the “will he cheat on his lady back home question” and whether or not he will get in a fight with the ship’s resident tough dude. Not a bad intro to Lowry.

Backlisted friends, I must alert you to Isobel English’s exquisite Every Eye, about an insecure woman finding her way in the world. The book, praised by Anita Brookner and Stevie Smith, reminded me of the incisive style of Elizabeth Taylor, & has a banger of an ending a la good friend Muriel Spark.

The greatest living ghost story writer is surely Reggie Oliver, who not only writes well, but also in a variety of styles including pastiches. He’s quite remarkable. Much of his work can be purchased through Tartarus Press.

A HAUNTING ON THE HILL is fine, as a "haunted house" book. It's competent & relatively engaging AS LONG AS YOU DON'T COMPARE IT TO THE HAUNTING OF HILL HOUSE.

The tone, plot, & writing is not at the same level- not even in the same neighborhood- as Jackson's. Hill House's horror still walks alone.
Black Cake by Charmaine Wilkerson has superb storytelling, beautifully woven together in a complex family history that crosses oceans and generations. It’s a warm and delicious embrace of a novel.

Next thing on my to-read list is Knight's & Magic, which finally has an EN release now thanks to J-Novel Club. I'm kinda interested in it cos it's one of the older Narou web novels, so it actually predates the Mushoku Tensei influences. Plus, it's a novel about mechs.

Stayed up too late last night reading more of WANDERERS by @user  (I know, I'm way behind). About halfway thru & thus far it's excellent, foreboding, & creepy. Characters have depth, plot has a strong bite of social consciousness. Irritated I have to go to work instead of reading.

When I started learning Portuguese last year, I didn't expect I would end up reading a Brazilian dark fantasy with a monstrous lesbian woman-spider, but here I am. Loved Bem Mal Me Quer by H Pueyo, loved Anatema, Dália and Casa Caprichosa 🕸

finished @user's Menewood this morning, what a fucking glorious novel. A world where the ability to pay attention and notice patterns results in possibility. A queer world full of beauty, grief, adventure, kindness, and possibility. Can't wait to read it again.

It’s been a year of comfort reads, with about 50 rereads under my belt and relatively few new-to-me books. Still, there are a few new reads I really enjoyed. Up first is LOST CARGO by P.A. Cornell. Great characters in a sf survival situation complicated by alien flora and megafauna. Great read!

My #bookreview of the new horror novel Whisperwood by Alex Woodroe. It's an intriguingly original take on cult & folkloric horror set in a small town hiding big secrets. Fresh take on cryptid lore & appealingly damaged but plucky heroine made this an enjoyable find for folk horror & fantasy lovers.

If a gorgeously written, New Orleans-set mystery novel where the sleuth is a deeply fucked up lesbian ex-punk rock nun (who was probably also an addict/alcoholic) sounds right up your alley, I cannot recommend Scorched Grace highly enough. 👍 Wow. What a read.

many books are amusing or entertaining or distracting or informative or lovely

a few have a shattering pearl of greatness inside them that changes you forever

been awhile since i sought out the latter; i have found it in isaac babel and some few others

During my recovery from February Covid, I read @user's The Book of Love, and it was So. Damn. Excellent. Obviously, I recommend it entirely. It's like Our Town, with dark parts visible, monsters articulated, poetry intact.

I cannot recommend "Why Does He Do That?" by Lundy Bancroft enough. It is a non-fiction book by a man who works with abusers to get them to act right. It's as readable as any novel I've read and has been  unspeakably useful in my life.

I finished reading A Man of Two Faces by Viet Thanh Nguyen today. With the way it deeply investigates the questions of identity, refugees, nations and war, I can’t imagine the 92nd St Y wanting him not to come.

But since they did, I hope lots of people go out and buy and read it.

Ray Nayler's The Mountain in the Sea is a beautiful debut, packed full of expansive ideas, an engaged plot, and soulful introspection about social connection and communication. The craft behind every single story choice is illuminous. Easily one of most brilliant books I've experienced all year.

Thank you to @user whose recommendation of Radio Red by Laura Groves has been an absolute gift from the gods.

If mid-70s Joni hooking up with Jane Siberry while getting high to Nerina Pallot's Year of the Wolf sounds like a very cool adventure, this is for you. 🖤

Yesterday I was in tears reading Tiffany Morris's indigenous swampcore novella GREEN FUSE BURNING coming from Stelliform Press in October. An astonishing work that processes personal & planetary grief, and leads the reader through a viscerally healing experience. Thank you for this witchery, Tiff.

Incidents Around The House marks the evolution of Josh Malerman. Adept prose and commanding storytelling that effortlessly draws the reader in for a deeply unsettling and genuinely frightening read. A masterclass in scares. Highly recommended!

Just finished 'Bang Bang Bodhisattva' by Aubrey Wood. Extremely meaty debut with two people trying their absolute best but getting pulverised into mince trying to solve— and clear their names of— a murder. Definitely recommend.

Today's book recommendation is Patrick Modiano's DORA BRUDER, a book about being haunted, and how an experience of haunting leads to obsession. It is about the doomed effort to salvage the pieces of a human life from the genocidal wreckage of history. Above all else, it is a book about loss.

Hench, by @user, is going to be the first book I finish reading in 2024. If you haven’t read it yet I highly recommend it. It’s charming and funny, but the heart of it is one of the *angriest* books I’ve ever read, a scathing examination of the idea of individualist power. So good!

I just re-read Madeline L'Engle's Time Quintet. I read A Wrinkle In Time approximately 1000 times as a kid. None are quite as good as that first one but An Acceptible Time has such an accurate portrait of a destructively selfish person who wants think of himself as a good guy it made my skin crawl

I finished reading The Book of the New Sun a couple days ago and I am still basking in its afterglow. The best fantasy I have ever read by a great distance. No offense to Tolkien or his infinite imitators I spent many afternoons of my youth with, but I wish I knew fantasy could be this good sooner.

Just finished @user 's MAD SISTERS OF ESI and it's magnificent, a tale of tales wrapped in iridescent spacetime, an infinitely nested creation myth, a portal and a ship and a campfire woven of prose. Reminded me of Calvino, and Clarke's PIRANESI (but spacefaring).

A book to look out for: Isabel Ibañez What The River Knows. Enjoyable example of recent novels drawing on Victorian popular fiction as a fantasy source. An Argentinian heroine, dastardly deeds in late 1800s Egypt, strange magic and other original touches make a fun read - 1st half of two book tale.

31 days of my Horror History. 

Day 27.

The first release from Dell Abyss, The Cipher by Kathe Koja, taught me horror doesn't need rules, it only needs to exist. This book fucked me up in ways that still reverberate today. If you haven't read it, get to it ... the funhole is always ready.

I loved STARS IN YOUR EYES by Kacen Callender! It was such a moving exploration of the impacts of trauma and shame, and it used some of my favorite romance tropes in really deep ways. 4.5⭐

#bookreview #bookblogger 💙📚👀🌈📚

🧵 : books I’ve read in 2024 (out of order)

#8- Tomorrow and Tomorrow and Tomorrow by Gabrielle Zevin. One of the most heartfelt pieces of fiction I’ve come across in years. Positive depiction of autistic and asexual character. Beautifully written. You don’t even have to like gaming.

Spent the day in the hammock reading The Blue Castle, then followed it up with its missing sexy edge piece: the Reylo Blue Castle AU (link to follow) because the only thing missing from the original (besides a sensibly-named romantic hero) is explicit sex scenes, 10/10 experience do recommend 🥰

I read this book. I liked it very much. There's an angel and a demon and they leave the Shtetl and go to New York City and they have adventures with lesbians along the way. It's a Jewish romp! I give it 5 black and white cookies.

One book from the competition that I want to make sure nobody misses, though, is _The Lady of Linshui Pacifies Demons_, a set of seventeenth-century cult stories about a female deity who is still worshiped today in Fujian. It is wildly magical, unapologetically proto-feminist, vivid and earthy. 1/3

Just finished Juni Ba's MOBILIS. 

I think this may be my favorite of Ba's work so far? It isn't as frenetic as Djeliya, and I'd say Monkey Meat is more formally experimental, but I think this is an evolution of his work overall. 

A book about the Apocalypse, Love, and Legacy.

Dog: *sniffing tree for a long time*

Me: What was that all about?

Dog: “Urine: A Novel,” by Spot. I enjoyed it. Well-paced, interesting plot, good character development.

Mantasy - Fantasy fiction for men, by men. Titles include:

The Bastarding - Rise of The Bastard
The Rigid Sword of Heteronormivity - A Septology
Darkfist McBloodsplatter - Book One of the Fistening of Broland

No e-books, no audio. Just thumping, rigid fantasy bound in beast flesh.

Toward Eternity might just be one of the best books I've ever read. It's speculative fiction that explores whether language is what makes beings humans...and it's beautiful. I just want to talk to someone about it! It'll be published this June - I got an ARC courtesy of NetGalley and HarperCollins.

Dear Bluesky. Please go read The Westing Game by Ellen Raskin. It’s a YA puzzle novel, a fast read, and utterly brilliant. Puzzles within puzzles. A brilliant grumpy girl sleuth who kicks anyone who gets in her way. So good you’ll want to read it again even knowing how it turns out.

Just wondering if anyone would be into a sapphic story set in a Welsh borders 1910s girls' school about a young butch lesbian field hockey captain with powerful poetry-magic falling for a fashionable debutante whose family want her to be the living vessel of an immortal Otherworldly dark queen
Finally burned my way through @user ‘s Wrath Goddess Sing and I loved it. Beautifully tragic trans reimagining of the Iliad and I’d strongly recommend it to anyone for what my opinion is worth.

The Saint of Bright Doors by @user is such a unique & great book about the son of a messiah he was raised to kill. I was sucked in by the complex & realistic post-colonial but still oppressive world, fleshed out characters finding their way in it, & really 🤯 use of the narrator’s POV. 🔥

This was a really good novel. About Libyan friends in exile. Like The Return, it is beautifully written (if you've not read The Return, do - a poignant non-fic about the author's search for his father). 💙📚

HEAVEN NO HELL by @user is the sort of book that fills you with the immensity of an artform. DeForge’s grasp on mode and tone is pitch-perfect, with some really stunning ideas on display, all at the exact point between articulation and abstraction that they need to be.

I’ve just finished this and it’s one of the most helpful things I’ve come across since my mum died. There’s a lot in here aimed at kids and teenagers but it was so reassuring and helpful to see my own experiences reflected back at me. Would highly recommend for anyone who has lost a parent.

Just finished @user’s Nettle & Bone, and I close the book with a big smile. While very different authors, TK has Diana Wynne Jones’ ability to infuse the ordinary with magic, deep empathy and a belief in the power of human decency which brings me joy. (More sex than DWJ though 😂)

If you’ve never heard of His Master’s Voice, by Stanisław Lem, it’s probably one of my favourite works of science fiction, mostly because it’s just a carefully-written work of philosophy of science with absolute bangers on every page. I’d call it epistemic cosmic horror

If you ever want to be on a trans Pacific flight reading the last chapter of a novel so emotionally affecting in an affirming way that it’s all you can do not to break out in sobs while your nose runs (fortunately under your mask) then let me recommend He Mele A Hilo (A Hilo Song) by Ryka Aoki

Two new novellas out from excellent SFF authors without much fanfare the last couple of weeks: latest in Lois McMaster Bujold's universally excellent Penric & Desdemona series, Demon Daughter, and a new Warchild entry from Karin Lowachee. Don't start here, but both series are low key 00s essentials.

I began my week with this wondrous book, a needed balm when the world hurts and I’m drowning in work. It is a masterful novella that packs a sophisticated and deeply affecting reflection on the historical layers of loss in a text of perfectly distilled economy. A most deserved Nobel.

I call this stack “So you want something weird?” Here’s a fun constellation of books I think can be enjoyed together, thinking about the relationship between the self & body, society & the alien. Divine sci-fi, clones & planet-sized AI, botanical science fantasy, the ethics of first contact. Enjoy!

Finished the great Zoey Ashe trilogy, which is the story of a dystopian city in future Utah so lawless and attention-obsessed that it’s a very useful satirical stand-in for the internet. The first book is called Futuristic Violence and Fancy Suits, which does what it says on the tin.

today's lifting weights, in case i need to bodyslam a 🐷, is brought to you by:

Nadifa Mohamed's incredible biographical novel 'Black Mamba Boy'

and

'Master Slave Husband Wife: An Epic Journey from Slavery to Freedom' (non-fiction) by Ilyon Woo

both are great and essential reading 💙📚

Read GRAVENEYE last night: the right time to curl up with a gothic(100%) Horror(100%) romance(YMMV) like this. Firmly towards the Hannibal end of the queer pop culture spectrum, Bowles draws up a bloodstorm and Leong's unusual choice of narrator and narration provides the the sepulchral drumbeat.

Today was able to look at proofs for the finished ENTER BOOGEYMAN anthology. Superb! I'm in it but more importantly you'll find Ramsey Campbell, Stephen King, Joe Lansdale, Linda Addison, RC Matheson, many others. Intro by Poppy Z Brite. Every story is beautifully illustrated--NOT by AI.

Really enjoyed North Woods by Daniel Mason. The novel charts the 400-year history of a house in Massachusetts.  Constantly surprising, wonderfully assured prose seemingly capable of anything. Vibrant characters, memorable scenes,  funny, emotional, surreal. Thanks for the rec @user

I read an excellent novel (The Round House by Louise Erdrich) cover to cover in one very long sitting yesterday and am still gently buzzing with it. It’s the very best way to read a novel, when you can make the time. I wish I could do it more often.

The new Nathan Hill novel is really fucking good, y'all. I adored THE NIX and wasn't sure what you'd follow that up with, and WELLNESS is just as human, messy, and wonderful as that was, and somehow folds together postmodern art, hypertext, placebos, and social media algorithms.

Rereading JESUS’ SON again—because I always am—and marveling again at the incredible efficiency of this opening. A single paragraph of setup to establish the ground situation, then a “but” to break it. I’d spend three pages doing this work, and to lesser effect.

Plans for Saturday totally derailed when I found @user’s No One Will Come Back For Us this morning. Now I’m nine stories in, it’s 2pm and I haven’t been this lost in a book of short stories since one of Clive Barker’s books of blood in the 80s. This is the good stuff.

there is a kiddo on the street that told her dad she only wants book recommendations from the Halloween Man Neighbor. 

that’s me, btw

middle grade horror is not something i’m well-versed in. so far she’s loved: The Graveyard Book (Gaiman), Scary Stories to Tell in the Dark (Schwartz)

i need recs

JR Drunk On All Your Strange New Words by Eddie Robson. Very good, extremely well-plotted and well-written, with memorable and vivid characters. I have to say it reminded me a lot of... nothing I have ever read before because it was so startlingly imaginative and ingenious. Highly recommended.

Books of 2023. E. Lily Yu is a virtuoso short story writer. From her early master work "The Cartographer Wasps and the Anarchist Bees" to more recent triumphs like "Small Monsters", her work sings. Debut collection Jewel Box: Stories is a triumph and easily one of the best books of 2023.

The past year or so has truly given us some wonderful books about fucked-up houses.

Just Like Home! Rose/House! What Moves the Dead! A House with Good Bones! The Wonder State (currently reading)! All phenomenal.

This timeline sucks in many ways, but the books about the houses are keeping me going.

This book is excellent. I didn’t think I was going to fall in love with a story about the tough mysterious badass loner (stop me if you’ve heard this one before), but they did it right. The art is really dynamic, great action. I also really like the production of the hardcover.

This book… it breaks almost every 1983 category romance rule, and you can tell that Sandra Brown reveled in that freedom. If editor Carolyn Nichols wanted to make a statement with Loveswept 1, mission accomplished!

Finished THE COMPLE(D) SAUCER COUNTRY. For my money, the best of the late classic period Vertigo books, finally wrapped up on Zoop after 2 publishers. X-files meets the West Wing is the easy pitch, but (like the best Vertigo) really a device for a team to dig deep into their obsessions. Strong stuff

I just devoured She Who Became the Sun by @user  it’s fantastic. It’s a fantasy epic set in a fictional China about a Mulan-like character claiming her destiny. Beautiful writing and powerful themes of gender, self-identity, and fate.

Yes, Persuasion was published OTD in 1817, but so was Northanger Abbey. Don’t underestimate it! It’s got:

🦇mystery
🦇 bookish bitches
🦇 gothic shit
🦇 a fangirl
🦇 discussion of fabric
🦇 a mansplainer

This is the time of year where I always feel I haven't gotten enough writing done, although I did have two books come out in 2023:
The Keeper's Six (January) (contemporary fantasy with a 60-ish protagonist)
&
Furious Heaven (April)(Sun Chronicles bk 2, gender swapped Alexander the Great space opera)

THE WISTERIA SOCIETY OF LADY SCOUNDRELS was Victorian era Monty Python for women, and... I adored it??

"The heroine is a polite pirate, the hero has a dozen different identities, proper old ladies take out assassination contracts on one another, and everyone carries far too many weapons."

Finished Anna Smith Spark's A SWORD OF BRONZE AND ASHES, which moves between the hearth and the forge poetically. I'll unpoetically paraphrase as "Brunhilde In Witness Protection post-Ragnarok gets hunted down by the horsemen of the apocalypse". Strikingly vivid both in its fantasy and mundanity.
We are living in a golden age of translated books.  In the past year I have read 13 books translated from 10 languages.

Book #114 of 2023: DAYSWORK by Chris Bachelder and Jennifer Habel. Simultaneously a smart, moving novel about literary obsession—in this case, Melville, and by extension, Hawthorne—and about marriage in the early days of the pandemic. Highly recommended.

NEW VIDEO ALERT! Today on Bar Cart Bookshelf we're talking about OTHER MINDS by @user. A thematically linked pair of near future cyberpunk novellas, this book is whip smart with a powerful vibe. Our drink, Facsimile, is a stirred cocktail on the cusp of summer and fall. LINK BELOW!

I've finally read Dr. Chuck Tingle's Complete Guide to the Void. Feeling pleased. This is the type of therapeutic literature both smart and delightfully absurd enough to work on me. I'm ready to prove love. I'm imagining the handsome whale. Let the good times roll. (Tag: @user)

Fusing the Gothic haunted house with the anxiety of climate catastrophe, Ashley Deng's new eco-horror novella DEHISCENT never spares the reader the discomfort of their own complicity.

This is a book I’m excited to read that came. It was on a list with mine where I heard of it. It’s a novel that fictionalizes Louise Little, the mother of Malcolm X who was an immigrant from Grenada who was a Garveyite revolutionary & was incarcerated in a psych hospital.

My father passed two weeks ago. Looking for healing, comfort reads and devoured Piranesi in three days by the tides of Puget Sound. Thanks Ballast Books for the staff recommendation.

A trip out to Cors Ddyga, a great wetland of reed, fen & wet grass. Created with Bitterns in mind, this is a conservation success: 3 booming Bitterns, loads of wildfowl including 16 Greenland W-f Geese, & all 3 spp of egrets. Fish survey finding abundant Rudd to keep Bitterns well-fed. #UKbirding

This Medium Egret was the last bird I photographed in Taiwan. I watched it hunting bugs in the parking strip while we left Guandu Nature Park in Taipei. Egrets are everywhere in Taiwan. "Medium" Egrets are one of three recently split from "Intermediate" Egrets #birding
A young female Pileated Woodpecker and her ruby eyes. Young Pileated Woodpeckers have brown eyes that will transition to yellow. In a few months, this Pileated will have the yellow eyes of an adult.

#BirdGlamour celebrates Scott’s Oriole! This oriole of the arid Southwest loves yucca and uses yucca fibers to weave nests and forage insects from yucca. Scott’s Oriole is the common name, which will be changed by the American Ornithological Society. How about the Yucca Oriole for a new name? 🐡🧪🐦

The subtle, elegant plumage of the gadwall is often overlooked, especially in comparison to "showy" ducks like the mallard drake. The gadwall is listed as "Vulnerable" on the Red List of Switzerland, so I was very pleased to see gadwalls at my favourite pond! 🪶 🌿

One of the things I always enjoy about winter birding in Scotland is all the sea ducks and the chance of finding something rare among them.  I didn't manage the latter but still enjoyed the common species such as these (Long-tailed Duck, Common Scoter, Red-breasted Merganser).  #UKBirding 🪶

🧪The Andean cock-of-the-rock, a clear example of sexual dimorphism! Males have a large crest and vibrant orange coloration, while females are more subdued in their brown plumage. Interestingly, even their iris color is sexually dimorphic, with only females exhibiting that striking blue 👀

Sometimes during migration, birds are so hungry they throw all caution to the wind in order to get food. This black-throated green warbler remained on the ground with people mere feet away while going to town on a cricket. 🪶#birds

Backyard ecosystems are soap opera dramas if you pay attention. This year a pair of aggressive, territorial robins decided they own my property. They attack & have driven off almost all other birds. I normally see 15 different types during my morning coffee. Now it’s just these 2 jerkwads.

🦉 Enjoyed mixed flock of elegant & royal terns on S. San Diego Bay this a.m. w/ @user - just met on Bluesky. The 2nd pic is for comparison: on left 2 large Royals w/ a balding appearance. On right:  small w/slender bill & shaggy crested Elegants. Saw Forster’s & Gull-billed terns also.

I love wrens. Love them. Nothing in the neighborhood is as fearless as this little rage nugget. Five foot Great Blue Herons fly away when you get within 20 yards; this guy will have babies in your bike helmet if you leave it outside and scream at everything that gets near. 🪶

Barred owls are native to the East Coast but have been expanding their range in the Northwest, threatening the Northern Spotted owl. Barred owls are relentless predators who eat anything that moves. Bigger, aggressive & more territorial than the ESL northern spotted owl.

phys.org/news/2023-12...

Back in 1902, a much wider range of birds could be seen in cities. Horned Larks were a common sight on Pennsylvania Avenue in Washington, DC, along with Tree Sparrows and Purple Finches - all of which would be incredibly rare to see on Pennsylvania Avenue today.🪶

Powerful Owl displaying a Brushtail Possum this afternoon. After sunset the pair met up, allopreened, flew around together & the male looked for over a minute into a tree hollow. Then they bonked! This is very early as the pairs in Brisbane don't usually nest until early May #birds #wildoz #brisbane

I’m having one of those incredible days y’all. Somehow I’ve managed to:

- Hit 200 on my birding life list with the Blackburnian and black-throated green warblers!!!
- Sell a bird photo print to a museum (!) for $375 (!!!)
- Get a full manuscript request from a new agent!!!
- Hike five miles!

Winds were favorable for a hawk movement over Chicago today. It wasn't busy, but I did have a Red-tailed Hawk, 2 American Kestrels that were escorted out of the area by my local…and to my surprise, he landed right over me! Also a few Sandhill Cranes…not bad for yard-settin'.

Borb No. 3: American Robin (Turdus migratorius)
Borbfact: Often considered a signal to the end of winter in the USA, many American Robins don't actually migrate, but simply change behavior so they are less likely to be seen in people's yards during colder months!

Here's a friendly male Allen's hummingbird I photographed last winter near Playa del Rey, Los Angeles, California. In recent decades the nonmigratory island subspecies of Allen's hummers from the channel islands have colonized the mainland and now live in LA yearround, making them a common sight 🪶📷

OK, this *never* happens. I found an I'iwi (2 of them, actually) foraging on the ground, in the open...since they're normally nectar feeders I have to assume they were looking for insects to feed to new hatchlings. I love these birds. :) 📷🌿🦉

According to eBird, Lapland Longspurs have arrived in NW Washington State! These songbirds breed on the arctic tundra and winter in most of the lower 48. What fall/winter visitors are you looking forward to seeing? 🪶🧪 #migration #conservation

A northern scorpion, Paruroctonus boreus, the northern-most scorpion species in the world. I found this one, who was quite chunky and so I assume was a pregnant female, on a rainy day in the Pryor Mountains of south central Montana. #Arachtober

Educational comparison between Western (L) and semipalmated (R) sandpipers, as pointed out by Gabriel Willow. The western has bill droop & unmolted chestnut scapular feathers from its breeding plumage while the semi has a straight conical bill & completely grayish back. (Jamaica Bay, July) #birds

From July 2014 and Jan 2015, Long-eared Owls in lean mode and floofy mode. Like most birds they will puff their feathers out to retain heat in the winter, and tuck them in during the summer, making for stark contrasts even though their body weight has barely changed. 🪶

#SuperbOwlSunday #owls

Too much fog to see the eclipse when I went outside, but I did find myself in the middle of an epic hummingbird battle between a male/female pair and a third bird (sex indeterminate). The pair held still long enough to have their portrait snapped together!

Size difference is a big cue in distinguishing hairy and downy woodpeckers, but they rarely pose side-by-side. Here's another tip! Check out the red on the back of the male's head. On a downy (L), it's one patch; on a hairy (R) it's two with black in between 🤓🪶

Welcome to the #BirdGlamour Glamager series, starting with the Western Tanager from western North America! The Western Tanager is extra and DOES NOT get his red coloration from a diet rich in carotenoids like many birds do: their red comes from rhodoxanthin from their insect-rich diet. 🐡🐦

I met about 25 new-to-me species of bird in Puerto Rico last week... here are a few of the niftiest ones I managed to capture! The nectar-sipping bananaquit (nearly ubiquitous, but hard to spot), the critically endangered Puerto Rican parrot, the scaly-naped pigeon, and the tiny Puerto Rican tody. 🪶

Barring a last minute owl, final stats for 2023 with the help of eBird: 

10 life birds.
272 species total for the year. 
226 for my state. 
213 for my county. 
113 species for the yard.
My eBird checklist streak is now at 1095 days. 

However you like to bird, I hope 2024 is good to you! ❤️🪶🎉

This immature Great Blue is growing up! Its dark crest is coming in nicely, and those long stringy feathers on its chest -- used both for protection from rough reeds and branches, and for intimidation of other herons -- are starting to appear. Lost Lagoon, Stanley Park, August 27 🪶

Excellent morning vismigging from SHB produced 4 Bewick’s Swans heading NE, my target species for March! Also 132+ Whitefronts NE and 29 Cranes N along with GBBG and two Ringed Plover as two overdue patch ticks. Biggest surprise was a RN Parakeet heading low west, very unexpected in Sweden! #birding

god i love watching a reddish egret just launch itself through the water, wings out, head swinging to and fro. It's a hilarious method of hunting AND IT WORKS. these dudes rustle up a ton of food running around like a maniac in the water.

The Black Guillemots showed an array of plumages.  Most of the adults were already in breeding plumage but a few were more wintery.  The first-winters showed little sign of advancing out of their winter-type plumage - not sure when they normally blacken up?  #UKBirding 🪶

#NationalBirdDay Here's the skull of a lovely white-tailed tropicbird specimen provided by USFW in Kauai, HI. The naturally downturned eyes of these pelagic birds make sense as they scan the sea for prey, including catching flying fish in-air! They also hover & plunge-dive! 📷: CC Wikimedia

Migratory swift roosts are one of my favorite natural spectacles! Here in Portland, hundreds of people of all ages & walks of life come out to watch 10K+ Vaux’s Swifts funneling into a chimney at dusk, cheering as the swifts evade a hungry hawk waiting for dinner. Incredible!

Powerful Owl this afternoon in Brisbane. Last night we saw the male of this pair investigating a nest hollow as the female looked on & hoo-ed! It seems wildly early for interest in nesting. Last year their 1st interest in a hollow was late April, and the female nesting early May. #birds #wildoz

Pacific Wren, Vancouver BC. 🪶🌿

The highest oz to db ratio of any bird I know. During breeding season, the male will build multiple nests in his territory, and as part of courtship, lead the female to visit them and let her pick her favourite.

Tufted Titmouse checking out a tree cavity for possible nest site - a variety of cavity nesting birds rely on pre-existing sites that require grasses sticks, fluffy stuff furnishings, but not actual excavation - keep your dead trees if you can! 🪶🌿

Today I saw this delightful mockingbird munching berries.

In the 1800s these birds were caged, sold, and referenced in music so often that they're still culturally ubiquitous, even though most Americans today wouldn't recognize them or their famous song.🪶

This palm warbler was one of a pair I saw zipping along near ground level, gleaning food. They eat primarily insects but will also eat seeds and berries. They also nest on the ground. Very un-warblery warbler! 🪶

The Grand Slam of winter woodpeckers! What a great afternoon 🪶
1. tap-tap-tap (Downy woodpecker) 
2. THonk ThONk (Hairy woodpecker) 
3. WHAM (Pileated woodpecker) 
4. Too cool for school (Northern flicker)

#marinelife 🧪🦈

A snowy egret landing in a shallow estuary at Baylands Nature Preserve. They're often described as elegant, but IMO they look pretty gangly in this situation. Decades ago, they were endangered — hunted for their fashionable feathers — but rebounded after the early conservation movement. #birds

I have the worst luck finding Merlins. Not only have I only ever seen like seven of them, I also tend to ID other things as them (young Sharp-shinned Hawks, broken tree branches, etc.). But yesterday I found #8! And what a cute lil murderer it was. #birds

A sea of shorebirds! I really hope I get to see this phenomenon again. Thousands gather along the Delaware Bay as they migrate north, eating horseshoe crab eggs to fuel their journey. Just a stupendous sight 

#birds 🦑 🌿

I’m the only one who had any fun on annual avian exam day. Caught & boxed 22 raptors, & my first bald eagle grabs (no pic bc we were very tired). A photographer followed me into the mews for this shot bc I was having a blast. It’s a total rush! I like big birds and I cannot lie🪶 
#redtailhawk

#BirdGlamour is back & starting 2024 with the House Finch! Once only calling western North America home, some were released in Long Island in 1940 after a failed attempt to sell them as caged birds. They made it to Oahu from CA before 1870 & were on all the major Hawaiian islands by 1901. 🐡🧪🐦

Only the 5th Long-tailed Duck I’ve seen at Inchgarth in over 20 years but today’s was the first with an actual long tail! 2nd of this winter here following one briefly on 29/10/23. Kingfisher in the snow was a year tick #ukbirding 🪶

The pine grosbeak is a beautiful winter bird. I'm always surprised at how big they are - robin-sized! They spend the winter eating tree buds, seeds and fruit, which they crush with their chonky beaks. If you look closely, you can see this one has a little evidence on her beak 🪶

Hey bud, you're dripping!
Didn't seem to be a concern for this 🪶 Florida Sandhill Crane

TIL there is a non-migratory sub-species of Sandhill cranes that just stick to Florida. They're diminutive compared to their migrating cousins, but just as gorgeous and dinosaur-y

📷📸 #Nature #Wildlife #Sandhill

So glad to have Merlin on my phone. Last night stayed up way too late editing, and around 3am a huge bird voice came hooting from the tree outside my window and kept going for about 15 minutes, long enough for me to find out what it was: a barred owl merlin.allaboutbirds.org/sound-id/

America had big dreams for imported ostriches in the 1890s - boosters expected them to become just as synonymous with California as raisins and oranges. These dreams went bust by 1915, but ostriches found a home in proto-amusement parks.🪶🗃️

birdhistory.substack.com/p/the-ostric...

Flies, fabulous flies. Estimates vary, but there are about 1 million fly species in the world and only around 14% of them have been described. Here are few gems from my little patch. 🧪

Black Scavenger Fly (Sepsis sp.), Marsh Fly (Dicta sp.) and Tachinid Fly.

Happy double lifer day to me! 🪶🪶

Mandarin Duck and Greater White-fronted Goose in front of a park trash can because, well, it’s a goose.

Urban birding in LA did not disappoint. Thankful for the tips of some fellow birders and the delightful weather of a SoCal November.

Down on the flood plain in search of a rare visitor to these lakes. A morning of mud-splatter and cracked ice; of snipe flashing off the wet meadows and grey partridges thundering from thickets. And then there it was, the sudden immensity of a white-tailed sea eagle watching the winter waters.

A new day, a new lifer! Seeing a hairy woodpecker IRL makes it VERY clear how big they are compared to their little downy counterparts. "Can I fit it in my pocket y/n?" Indeed! 🪶 The little downies aren't even as long as the suet feeder; this gal towers over it.

Keeping busy with illustrations for my forthcoming book on storm-petrels -these are Wedge-rumped Storm-petrels flighting over their Galápagos breeding colony during daylight (most storm-petrel populations are strictly nocturnal at their colonies), watched by a hungry Short-eared Owl…

White-breasted Nuthatch began singing this morning! He joins the Tufted Titmouse and House Finch in the dawn choir practice😂 Northern Cardinal should be kicking on any day now, then a tiny lull before Mourning Doves start. Oh, and February is usually when woodpeckers start drumming too. 🪶

A still and peaceful late afternoon at Abberton, though no signs of spring bar a few Chiffchaffs. Winter birds continue to hang on, including the flock of 5 Lesser Scaup, the ultra-mobile Black-throated Diver and a beautiful Short-eared Owl. Bullfinch and Turnstone were unusual here. #ukbirding

This morning in Brooklyn: Bright sun, 27 deg F, 24 MPH icy wind gusts, snow covering the ground and many plants. But these conditions didn't stop the super-rare Rufous Hummingbird from making an appearance and dazzling observers. This little guy should be wintering in Mexico right now. 🪶

I've just learnt that in Dutch 'waxwing' is 'pestvogel,' 'plague bird', a name given to it in the Netherlands and in Flanders because in the past its arrival sometimes coincided with outbreaks of plague.
I find this little fact too fascinating not to share.

Check out that leg bend for lift-off.  Snowy Egrets are quite common around here but I love their showy feathers during breeding season and those yellow feet! They are a success story of the Migratory Bird Treaty Act of 1918 - they'd almost been wiped out for their feathers used in hats.
🪶🌎
I love taking photos of birds because it slows you down. You need to look carefully, walk slowly, sit down sometimes and don’t move for a while. Then you see the world around you come to life. 🪶

A ridiculous birding story: I recognize birds by sound more often than sight. It's pretty rare, and very exciting, for me to hear a bird I've never heard before, and try to identify it. I'm out there every morning and this only happens once every few months. (1/x)

One of the best tricks I’ve learned is to fill the bird feeder at night so when I awaken, I get to visit with a riot of birds!

White-breasted nuthatches, tufted titmice, house sparrows, downy woodpeckers, northern cardinals, & black-capped chickadee-dee-dees.

A Blue-winged Mountain Tanager enjoys some fresh fruit. These birds are common and widespread in the Andean cloud forest. I believe the plant is a Clusia sp. Birds provide an important service to the forest by spreading fruit seeds. 
🧪🌿🪶
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

Check out this really incredible example of xanthochroism in a warbler. This is a condition of excess yellow color deposition in the feathers, but in this case the naturally melanic patterns seem to be overriding the excess yellow, making for a gorgeous bird 🪶

What was DC’s birdscape in 1900? Horned larks promenaded on Pennsylvania Avenue. Barn owls lived in the Smithsonian tower. Robins were sold as meat in Washington Market. Hunters shot ducks and bobolinks in the Anacostia marshes. Audubon officers raided hat stores for the illegal use of feathers.🪶🗃️

Watched two tiny mockingbirds drive off a raven in the park this morning. They were no more than the size of his head, but they buzzed at him and flew at him and made such a ruckus that he gave up and left.

While two pairs of robins fought over who owns my backyard, a Northern Flicker stopped briefly to check out the gardens. I'd never seen one before. Only got the back profile but that yellow stripe is great. 🌿🌱 #addBirder

Today in badass birds, the Kori Bustard 🪶

Male KB are the heaviest birds capable of flying. The picture taken in Kenya shows their elaborate mating display where males puff up and strut about to attract females.

apparently harris hawks (native to southern US/mexico) have exploded in popularity for falconry worldwide since the 80s because they're much easier to train because they are pack hunters in the wild (!)...

Bird Glamour’s Glamager celebrates the Summer Tanager! This southern US to Central American bright red bird is both beautiful and deadly. Summer Tanagers specialize in eating bees and wasps, which they catch mid-flight and then beat to death by whacking their catch against a branch! 🐦 🐡

So many Coops!
Cooper's hawks are about to take over top spot of our most common raptor patients this year for the first time. A west nile virus outbreak is the likely cause, they seem particularly susceptible and hard hit by it, at least locally.

never got around to posting this little asshole. they'd fly up high, drop the crustacean, and then fly down and try again. except it wasn't just the floor. the car they're perched on also had the thing dropped from a great height, making a mighty metallic clunking sound. #birds 🪶

Fall migration had started here in Minnesota, bringing with it nighthawks in need of help. Living most of their lives on the wing, nighthawks even drink while flying, swooping down over water to grab a sip. It makes them very difficult patients since they don't readily eat or drink without aid.

Last June at camp in Algoma, I saw a peculiar Phoebe; pinkish-red in colour. It turns out it was the first record of diet-induced aberrant plumage (erythrism) in this species. The editor of ABA's Birding magazine invited me to write an article, which was just published. #birds #birding

The final Bird Glamour Glamager look is the Flame-colored Tanager! This complex-colored tanager calls Mexico & Central America home & sometimes AZ home. They are a mountain bird, shifting their territory to higher elevations due to climate change, but mountains only go so high! 🐡🧪

Moult is such a critical activity for #birds that as the last story covered it may define the upper limit on the size of flying birds, but my favourite moult mystery concerns the tiny Willow Warbler & the mystery of why it does two complete moults a year. 1/N 🌿🌏 🧪

As banders, we use “molt limits” to help age birds. Molt limit = boundary between feathers of different ages. Second-year birds have them bc they don’t molt all their juvenile feathers during their hatch yr. E.g., this Scarlet Tanager with retained juv primary coverts, primaries, and secondaries.

#BirdGlamour celebrates the Bullock’s Oriole, an oriole the I have a chance of seeing in southern BC! These western North American orioles are acrobatic & hang upside down when foraging & weaving nests. They love insects but have a sweet beak and will visit hummingbird feeders. 🐡🧪🐦

[extreme David Attenborough voice]
Here we have the Alfred Hitchcock hummingbird. Note its familiar profile. When food or leading ladies are scarce, they enter torpor by slowing their metabolic rate, much as I do after my 5th gin & tonic

This is maybe a third of the group of tri-color blackbirds that filled the backyard and feeders this morning. They come through every year but this was the biggest number yet. Positively Hitchcockian. 👀 #BirdPorch

This has been a hilarious afternoon of desk-birding, as a parade of birds just landed in the tree outside my house, in direct view of where I'm sitting. I've looked up to find yellow-rumped warblers. Chestnut-backed chickadees. Oak titmice. 

And then... MF'ING WESTERN TANAGER. 🪶

My mum suggested that I draw an American goldfinch as they have been visiting her feeder. 🪶
Although this is closer to their summer color as males display sexual dichromatism with bright yellow plumage in the summer months and olive in the winter. Have you had any bird visitors today? #birds #art

Double the luck! First time at the sunflower feeder seeing the female red-winged blackbird join the male that showed up in the yard a week ago. Earlier in the day, a cardinal mating pair were at the same feeder. Now looking forward to two broods!😊🪶

🧪🐡The rufous hummingbird (S. rufus), an example of the challenges of species ID. Where they overlap, females/juvenile males are indistinguishable from Allen’s hummingbirds, & mature males only distinguishable by the subtlest feather notch. Often, reports don’t differentiate, stating “Rufous/Allen’s”

I love birds, I really do, but I'm not sure how excited I am for the geese to return 😆 
Most recent Svalbard summer population estimates are 80000 pink-footed geese, 35000 barnacle geese and 6000 brent geese.

That's 121000 geese, or almost 50 per person. That's too many geese.

Bull Point visible migration early a.m: 1st Swallow😍, 61 Sand Martin WNW from 07:30 (lifting of clag) to 09:30; 162 Mipit, 19 alba wag, first concerted passage of 43 Linnet & 47 Goldfinch. Also 5 Greenfinch (= mega spring count here!), 7 Common Scoter W 07:05 & 4 grounded male Wheatear. #UKbirding

Brown creeper photo would make a devilish jigsaw puzzle! These tiny birds stick close to the trunks of large loose-barked trees, probing for insects. The start at the bottom and work their way up, then move to the base of the next one 🪶

Favourite nature experience of 2023? This one springs to mind pretty immediately... April 15, snow almost gone. Came across a snowy owl who had found a little patch of snow. Both the owl and me were listening to a Western meadowlark singing on a nearby tree. Winter meets spring! 🪶

My only somewhat-successful long-exposure shot so far of one of the visiting long-eared owls in Central Park, hunting after flyout earlier this week. They are small and fast, and very hard to track at night! 🦉🌃

(5 second exposure, using only ambient light, taken from a respectful distance)

I do adore these little birds. Tufted Flycatcher (Mitrephanes phaeocercus) are small flycatchers that you find in the higher elevations of Costa Rica. They're fun to photograph because they'll use the same perch over and over. This was in the forest at Savegre Lodge in San Gerardo de Dota. 🪶

Every spring I serve as a part-time nanny to a Screech Owl family.  It takes a village during fledging!  This summer some honey bees moved into the nesting box for a few months, they have since moved on and we cleaned out the box in preparation for next breeding season.  Look at these honeycombs! 🧪🌎

I’ve been too busy to do any birding lately, but today the universe gifted me a merlin eating a bird on a telephone pole, on my street, as I was walking home from the train after work. It was the best valentine’s day gift, I’m being so serious 🪶
(rip to that little bird tho)

Went for a walk along the banks of the Nepean River, heard and eventually saw a heap of bellbirds 🪶- this is the only one that wasn't camera shy. Bellbirds mostly eat 'lerps' - small sugar-rich domes that certain insects make in gum leaves to protect themselves from predators. (1/2)

Responding to today's prompt to post #birds hanging out with other critters. This tiny pīwakawaka fantail is trying to gob down a big brown fly, but fails much to its frustration. (tried posting an animated GIF but sadly it didn't work on Blue Sky - but this photo still shows the battle was real)

Yesterday, within the ivy-clad garden fence, I found a blackbird’s nest. Made last spring was this marvellous & intricate grass-woven cup. Now still inside of me, a seemingly innate feeling of fortune & privilege. To have had their company—albeit unknowingly—is a beautiful thing.

A White-winged Crossbill I saw at Roger's Pass in British Columbia. Although a member of the Fringillidae (finch family), instead of the typical conical finch beak, crossbills have an adaptation that facilitates extracting seeds from conifer cones.

What do Northern Saw-whet Owls & Barbie have in common? PINK! Northern Saw-whet Owls are very nocturnal, making it hard to age them by flashlight. The trick? UV light! Newly molted feathers have fresh porphyrins, which glow bright pink under UV light. Year 1 owls are very pink! #BirdGlamour 🐡🧪

Thought my walk at my local birding patch this morning was going to consist of chilling with some wrens and sparrows and then found a LEWIS'S FREAKING WOODPECKER, right here in town. Migration is wild, y'all. 🪶

After a week of chasing Parakeets on my bike, I’ve finally located the East Oxford parakeet roost. The local flock of 35-40 birds all roost in conifers just off Church Cowley Road, opposite the cemetery. Now, is there a north Oxford roost too?

There were sooo many Purple Sandpipers at Barnegat! Love watching them feed in the crashing waves. The light was good enough I could even see the “purple” in the middle of their feathers (more in the potter’s pink-magenta realm, but whatever). If you stood still, they’d walk right under your feet.🪶

Black redstart (Phoenicurus ochruros) 🪶 Домашна червеноопашка

I love the face expression on these birds. Will never get tired of them. Curious but cautious, fragile but brave. They are not afraid of people but are shy and quiet and often remain unnoticed even at very close distances.

Banner day yesterday: 4 bald eagles, 2 sightings! Pair in pics also spotted 2 weeks ago but this time much closer to the road. Second pair spotted when 1 was flying across road to nest along Lake Ontario, while the other was perched on branch next to nest, but sadly no place to pull over for pic. 🪶

I was so excited watching a particularly showy male Black-throated Blue Warbler today-- I failed to notice that sometimes his throat was all-black, other times it was black-speckled.

It wasn't until both males hopped out onto the branch together that I realized there were two!!

Sketch vs final: the brood parasite card for Birdwatcher! Brood parasites are birds that lay their eggs in other birds' nests to raise. It's currently not known if there are brood parasites that lay eggs in bird of paradise nests, but if there are, White-crowned Cuckoo is a possible candidate. 🎨🪶🐡

Took this picture to show the difference in sizing. The bluebird is pretty true to 1/6 scale, and while magpies are larger than bluebirds, if this magpie was in the same scale it would only be twice as long as the bluebird. Good thing I wasn't trying to make them go together, it'd bother me forever.

The Common Kingfisher has to be one of the UK's most beautiful birds: it looks like it belongs in the tropics rather than fishing along a stream in a wee London park next to a children's playground, but that's where I found this handsome chap this morning in Colliers Wood. 🪶

White tern perched in an ironwood (Causuarina equisetifolia) tree, where the species "nests"... except they construct no nest to speak of! They precariously balance a bare egg within the ironwood's branches.

Photo from Saipan, CNMI 🪶

Took my new camera out for its inaugural spin today! For a first-time photographer I'm pretty pleased with getting at least one decent shot. #birds 🪶 

This female hooded merganser was alone among the mallards and canada geese, and obliged me by raising her pretty crest.

Today is another day of being paid (among other things) for looking at pictures of animals! Examples include the peacock butterfly and the hummingbird hawk moth, which sounds a lot less badass in German (Taubenschwänzchen aka "little dove tail") 8D
Pied-billed Grebes have lots of great nicknames, including Devil-diver and dabchick. They can slowly submerge like a submarine and swim long distances to avoid birders with cameras. #Birds

Have you ever seen a Purple-eyed Goby (Bryaninops natans)? This small (less than an inch long!) goby is known for its eye-catching bright pink to purple eyes and are associated with Acropora corals. I photographed this individual in Apo Reef Natural Park, Philippines.

Back with another raptor, this one spotted in Serengeti NP, Tanzania 🦅 I believe the ID on this one is a dark chanting goshawk (M. metabates). The chanting goshawk genus Melierax is named for their vocal behavior, particularly during breeding season 🧪 #SciArt

Here's a Nene, or Hawaiian goose, from my trip to Kaua'i one year ago. These excellent geese are only found on the Hawaiian islands and are one of the few endemics birds out there doing decently well, having rebounded in population after nearly going extinct in the 1950s 🪶

Borb No. 6: Cedar Waxwing (Bombycilla cedrorum)
Borbfact: Cedar Waxings are well known for getting tipsy on fermented berries but in 2018 in Gilbert, Minnesota there were so many drunk birds causing problems that residents began calling the police for help!

#borb #bird #birding

I live in the woods and am always stumped why there are so few owls here. We're about 500 feet from Gichigami/Lake Superior, which may be part of it? Maybe they don't like to be near the shore? In any case, I heard a great horned owl on the evening walk tonight and it made my fuckin week.

Some nice hybrids from my trip up north.  The Red-crested Pochard x Tufted Duck hybrid appears to be one of at least three birds that have visited the site, possibly including backcrosses with Tufted Duck.  The Mallard x Eider hybrid is also a long-staying bird.  #UKBirding 🪶

Eastern Bluebird, perched on a post at McMaster Forest, Hamilton, Ontario #HamOnt

Bluebirds love spots to perch like this so they can look down to hunt insects on the ground that they can swoop down and catch -- almost like being tiny hawks.

#birds #EasternBluebird

Of course I've done a Crossbill Bird Glamour! Here's the White-winged Crossbill! Chatty flocks travel among conifer tops, each bird eating up to 3000 conifer seeds per day! Bills can be right-crossed or left-crossed: right-crossed lower bills are 3x more common than left-crossed bills! 🪶 🧪 🐡

When it's 15° F / -9.4° C out, an uneasy détente develops around the suet. The Downy Woodpecker, despite being the smallest woodpecker on the continent, is still an insane jackhammer dinosaur, and generally everyone keeps their distance. This Bluebird decided to roll the dice. Tense. 🪶 #bird

How about Darwin’s famous finches of the Galápagos Islands? These birds actually do not belong to the Fringillidae family, but are in the Thraupidae (tanager) family instead. Common names can be very confusing! #FinchyFriday

Today's dinosaur-reminiscent bird is the crested caracara, a pair of which I saw in western Mexico a couple days ago. They look like hawks but they're actually hefty falcons that mostly eat carrion. This one, perhaps perturbed by my arrival, is making its rattling call. #birds #birding #Mexico

The last sighting of SF's Red-Headed Woodpecker was July 16th, meaning it was viewed on 17 consecutive days since I found it on the morning of June 30th. The bird was reported on 294 eBird checklists (easily over 1000 ppl given shared lists and non-eBird-users)

Swallow-tailed Cotinga in the Serra do Mar outside Rio de Janeiro last month - a #bird I hadn't expected to see as bad weather forced me to cancel a planned day of higher altitude birding, so a wonderful surprise when this one turned outside the lodge as I waited out rainstorms. 🪶 #BirdsOfBrazil

The bird🪶 glancing down at me here is a channel-billed cuckoo. Its foster parents were either crows or ravens; a brood parasite of this size needs a big nest. This one has just come back from its winter holiday in New Guinea or Indonesia.
Minnippi Park, Brisbane QLD

I was walking round a local park in #Extremadura, SW #Spain when this Barn Swallow (Hirundo rustica) landed on a bent reed stem about three metres away! It preened for about five minutes before going back to catching insects. #Birds
Gannet from Bempton Cliffs today. The black iris in the eye indicates that this bird has previously been infected with H5N1 Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) and survived. 🧪 #ukbirding

kirtland’s warblers are the rarest breeding warbler in the united states. they’re also tail-bobbing chonks with no fear of humans and a peculiar love of fire. this one has a pink mandible which means it was born just a few weeks ago!

Look closely at this! One of my more typical brown hare photos! Why? Though taken from directly behind, you can see both eyeballs, so the hare can see me behind with its 360° panoramic vision. And note both those huge ears rotated backwards to hear the slightest sound behind.🙄
#UKwildlife #mammals 🦊

A yellow warbler (Setophaga petechia) last weekend near Sayulita, Mexico. Relatively common across the US, too, but often hiding in the bushes. Males' song during breeding season sounds like "Sweet sweet sweet, I'm so sweet," but of course the message is "get the f*** away, this is my patch." #birds

Another look at the northern hawk owl I found on Sunday. It has been living in and around this tamarack swamp all winter. If prey availability is good, they maintain pretty small territories. An active little hunter, it's often atop a snag as I pass it on my way to and from my cottage. 🌿🪶

Another Chukar Partridge photographed in Shropshire. It is illegal to release this species for shooting in the UK. The last couple of years have seen an uptick in the number photographed around the country. A complete dereliction of duty by the industrial shooting industry! 🪶🌏

The Department of Ornithology here recently landed a grant to study the effects of Cannabis on seabirds.

They plan to leave no tern unstoned.

Back in Dec. we asked which IBP staffer would count the most species in a single Christmas Bird Count. And the winner is Peter Pyle w/ 108 spp. in both the San Francisco & the Point Reyes counts. The SF count included Barn Swallows which have lately been lingering along the CA coast in the winter. 🪶

#BirdGlamour’s Halloween-colored birds continues with Orchard Orioles! Related to blackbirds & North America’s smallest orioles, Orchard Orioles don’t mind neighbors & will build their hanging pouch nests in groups. They feed on nectar & pollen when wintering in Central & South America. 🐡🧪

Yellow-bellied Sapsuckers (who have a type of meowing call) are a welcome sight - the lines of small holes are a good sign you have them - they drink sap (and eat small insects that might be attracted to sap) after they make  the holes 🪶🌿

Today I fulfilled a birding dream and hung out with a hunting zone-tailed hawk. They look a lot like turkey vultures, hang out with turkey vultures, and may use vultures as cover to sneak up on prey 😮 This wasn't my lifer but it was the best view I'll probably ever get.

🪶 Birds in flight!
This was the moment I learned that Swainson’s Hawks hunt by tucking their wings and diving like falcons. Exhilarating to watch and photograph!

The Gold-ringed Tanager has the local common name Bangsia de Tatama for the very limited range where it is found. The tanager is the symbol of Tatama National Park and a species that has inspired conservation in Colombia. 🪶🌿

So I saw this baby squirrel in my back yard for weeks that didn't get bigger and I looked it up, turns out it's a red squirrel and they're just smaller than the "normal" gray squirrels which are smaller than the fox squirrels we also have and also yes I have adhd why'd you ask
A male Golden-browed Chlorophonia (Chlorophonia callophrys) enjoying fresh berries for breakfast at Paraiso Quetzal Lodge, Costa Rica.

The pyrrhuloxia or desert cardinal is a bird that inhabits the arid region from the southwestern United States to central Mexico.
It is similar to the northern cardinal in terms of song and behavior, and both coexist in many desert areas.

The Heist 🪶

Monk parakeets in an urban park in Buenos Aires were raiding the condiments at outdoor tables at a barbecue joint. They were always going for the mustard, bringing it up to where most of the flock was hanging out in a large tree, and making a huge mess

Greetings from Florida! Im thrilled to join the @user community! This account will focus heavily on wildlife and eco photography (mostly centered on FL biodiversity). To start, here’s one of my favs: Coluber contrictor priapus, the Southern black racer, in Lake county, Florida (18 August 2006).

Just to show how different echidnas from Western Australia and Tasmania are here are two young echidnas still being fed by their mothers and of about the same age. The WA baby has spines and no hair, Tas, hair and no spines, and there is no question about which one is cuter. 🧪

For #ArtAdventCalendar Day 16, the Tiniest Maui Honeycreeper, a Maui ʻalauahio (Paroreomyza montana) on ʻōhiʻa lehua (Metrosideros polymorpha). These little ones are hard to photograph, as they're true creepers, mostly stay in close to the tree trunks--I got lucky here. :) 📷🌿🦉

The great blue heron is a large wading bird in the heron family Ardeidae, common near the shores of open water and in wetlands over most of North and Central America, as well as far northwestern South America, the Caribbean and the Galápagos Islands
Along with the hybrid teal, we also have a Ross’ Goose visitor that’s uncommon in our suburbs! A Canada goose and white-faced ibis rounds out this winter walk! 🪶

I don't know if it's only in our building in Budapest (urban environment), but I stopped hearing blackbirds singing during/after COVID. I just heard a male blackbird intimidating another bird, and they do live around here. Has any of you noticed the same?
#birds

I don't know which kingfisher this is but can tell you that I've spotted it in Hong Kong and now also Japan. 😀

More good news! We've found a record number of the scarce Brown Hairstreak #butterfly (Thecla betulae) eggs at Rock Edge SSSI. Forty nine eggs around the site, including 2 triplets and 2 doubles. Only 3 eggs on Blackthorn, 36 on Cherry Plum and 10 on another Prunus species...
#UKWildlife #OX3

Golden Crowned Crane

What makes the African crowned crane special is its standout appearance, especially the golden crown of feathers on its head. It's also known for its fancy dances during mating.

For my wife’s birthday, she wanted to hike through Starved Rock State Park in north central IL. Had great weather for it. Saw some pelicans and a bald eagle, lots of squirrels and chipmunks. Alas, no Sasquatch. 

And check out the flights of stairs! 😵‍💫
More Barn Swallow fledglings from the wetlands because soon they'll be all grown up. What am I gonna do?!
