There are also white-throated dippers on the Pader.  The Pader is the shortest river in Germany.  Up to 5000 liters per second flow from around 200 springs and the first mill is operated after 400 m. #birds #scanjack #paderborn
Came across this lone Willet on Vilano Beach in Florida. I miss seeing them. Quite easy to find in my hometown San Diego. Not so easy (but not impossible) to see in Nashville. Feb 24, 2024. 🪶

Tang and his quailsteed Linda rest in the shade.

Birds can't pant to cool themselves but you've probably seen some with gaping beaks and bobbing throats, its looks like panting but its actually gular fluttering. Neat stuff.

The Apolinar's Wren is a small wren endemic to the Andes of central Colombia. Small and chunky, overall pale brown bird w some barring. Locally known as "Chirriador"(“creaker" in English) due to its song, which sounds like a creaky wagon wheel. birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/...

Meet the "red-bellied" woodpecker, one of five species common here in central NC. Everyone sees the red head, but there is a "red-headed" woodpecker, and frankly, all the mails have red in the head anyway. In this view you can see the faint amount of red on his tummy that earns him his name!

Northern parula, at Charles H. Rogers Wildlife Refuge—one of the more identifiable of the dark-backed, yellow-breasted, eyeringed, wing barred, small, hoppity birds.

First snake eagle of the year just seen banking over the hills beyond the motorway outside of Ioannina. The fingers on its massive wings riffling with wind; its pale feathers ghostly against the slanted sunlight. Travel well, beautiful bird.

During nesting season, the South Polar Skua swoops at penguin rookeries in an effort to dislodge an egg from a nest or find one unattended. The penguins make a joint effort to chase off the scavenger and protect their rookery.

It's National Pulled Pork Day. A great way to make a vegan, plant-based version of pulled pork is to use shredded jackfruit and bake it in a BBQ sauce to give it a more pork type texture. 🍽️🍜

Examples: vegan pulled pork Filipino Adobo, in Bao buns, and in a summer BBQ plate.

A little late night something for those who only have access to the cheap stuff.

Boil a pack of instant noodles, strain, and mix together in a bowl of hoisin sauce, Sriracha, scallions, and sesame oil.

Smoked ham hock stew. I cooked this for over six hours yesterday in a clay pot and then let it rest overnight. There was so much gelatin in the broth that when I took it out of the fridge in the morning it had set completely solid. Haha.

Dumped $5 of grocery store chicken breasts in a crock pot with a jar of cheap salsa (“New York City??”) 4 hours ago and just enjoyed a delicious tex-mex feast with plenty left for later. If you eat meat and own a crock pot I don’t know why you aren’t doing this weekly. Follow me for more sad tips!

Oyakodon, a traditional Japanese dish, is a beautiful representation of 'parent-child' love. Its name comes from 'oya' (parent) symbolized by chicken and 'ko' (child) represented by the egg, served over rice ('don'). They're simmered with onions in a sweet-savory soy-based broth. 🐓❤️🥚

I’ve been making a lunch this winter that’s tortellini in broth with spinach and a little tomato sauce stirred in at the last minute, but today I cooked black lentils in the broth first and it immediately became an all-time great lunch.

Ordering the Bucca di Beppo lasagna lovefeast for two to prove two things:

As food spoils quickly in Thavnair's heat and humidity, so have locals learned to preserve food with a plethora of spices. The ingredients are used to create their signature curry dishes, with many of them including hamsa meat and eggs.

Mirya speaks of the famous sweet/savory Hawaiian chex mix my friends dubbed "Hawaiian crack." In the islands, we make giant batches to give away during the holidays + during big sporting events. I suggest doubling the glaze, cooking in aluminum disposable pan, nori komi flavor. shorturl.at/bfpvO

My bean consumption has been transformed. I've been making chili, curry, etc. from grocery store dry beans for years and never been quite satisfied. These remarkable beans are from Rancho Gordo, so superior to store beans that they are like a completely different material. I will never go back.

Pasta alla gricia is an amazing old school dish in the "dry" class (ie dressed, not sauced). Very simple, it includes only the pasta, guanciale, pepper, water, and Pecorino Romano. It is rich, indulgent, and delicious.

Took Thursday’s jerk chicken and made jerk fried rice on Friday. Then took THOSE leftovers and made jerk chicken burritos on Saturday. Each iteration better than the one before.

All this and more in my forthcoming book, How To Leftovers. It is my superpower.

When I randomly see pizza recipes online, the quality of the dough nearly always makes me weep. If you want to improve your homemade pizza, please @ me any time. I will be patient and kind and helpful. Pizza made at home can be tremendous, and life is short. You can do this!

Hot hot oven… I get the oil (covering the base of the tin) smoking hot on the hob too

150/200 ml of batter. Back in the oven. Don’t touch for 25 mins 🤷🏻‍♂️

Make lahanosalata (Greek cabbage salad)! Finely slice cabbage, carrot and onion. Mix in 1/4 cup salt. Leave for 2 hours, kneading every 30 min. Rinse twice with water. Squeeze out as much liquid as poss. Store with a  few spoonfuls of lemon juice for max 5 days. Serve with a dribble of olive oil. 🥰

I make up honey butter every other weekend or so and I keep meaning to post this:

Stick of softened butter (or vegan butter)
1/3 whipped honey, or agave nectar, if vegan
Zest of one orange

Beat until fluffy and then store on counter for a day or so and then fridge it. good on all the usual carbs.

Lazy porridge recipe!

- 125g salted butter
- 250g oat groats
- 1L water
- 0.75L milk
- 1 tsp salt

1. Fry the oats in butter, salt
2. Pour in water, cook in rice cooker on "brown rice" setting
3. Pour in milk, leave on "keep warm" setting
4. Eat when chewy & delicious (~6hrs)

My new favorite way to make potatoes. Get the small variety.

Boil them for 15 minutes (start preheating yoir air fryer after 10).

Drain them and let the water steam off, then toss them in some butter or oil and salt.

Air fry them on 400 for 10 minutes. Toss them halfway through.

Serve and enjoy.

How to make Greek style roasted lemon chicken with potatoes!

1. Peel potatoes and mix with juice and zest of 4 lemons, olive oil, a spoonful of mustard, salt and pepper and half a cup of water. Lay on a bed of sliced onions and roast uncovered for 30 min at 200°C.

Malagasy vocabulary:

Laoka: a side dish that’s served with rice.

A “normal” Malagasy meal is supposed to be rice with laoka. Many Malagasy people are willing to accept a meal with very little laoka if necessary, but a meal with not enough rice is simply Not a True Meal.

🇲🇬

Pappardelle coccolare, or Coddle pasta in Hiberno English. You need a good few fatty rashers and sausages here. Make a normal carbonara sauce w two extra egg yolks for a thicker sauce and some sharp white Dubliner cheese grated in. give it a good mix. Parsley to garnish and white pepper to serve.

-3 carrots (i use carrot chips)
-1 head broccoli
-1/2 onion
-petite gold potatoes, quartered
-brussel sprouts, halved
-andoullie sausage, sliced
-garlic salt

roast on sheet pan @user, 40 minutes

Beef on Weck. Roast beef on a kummelweck roll, a roll that is topped with kosher salt and caraway seeds. The meat on the sandwich is traditionally served rare, thin cut, with the top bun getting a dip in jus and spread with horseradish.

Lentil and carrot soup with Lil Smokies. Not #vegetarian but easily can be. Just use vegetable stock (I'm using beef for a heartier flavor) and skip the smokies (or use a plant based sausage with a smoky profile). 🥣 🍚 🍜

Foodposting:

I slow cooked a pork loin in umami sauce and now I'm adding pulled umami pork to my 0.52 cent ramen cups. I've also tried the new egg trick where you save some of the broth at the bottom, crack an egg, whisk it in and microwave it to cook it. 10/10 would again.

when a lot of folks here "Icelandic food" they make think of shark, or ram's testicles, or hot dogs

but the best icelandic food what there is: rúgbrauð, a sweet dark cake-like bread made from rye and often baked with geothermal heat. and if you do get it, get the best, which is from Kökugerð HP.

the best caramelized onions you will ever make:

4-5 onions of various kinds, sliced 

5 tbsp salted butter

In a large saucepan, melt the butter over medium heat. Add the onions and mix. Turn heat to low. Cook for 4-5 hours, till onions are browned and sweet, with an almost jam-like texture.

Bifana from O Trevo. Two ingredients: pork and bread. It's ASTONISHINGLY good.

The pork is so tender and juicy, the bread is as light as air, and the combination is somehow more delicious than the sum of its parts.

And if that's not enough, you can add hot mustard and/or piri piri oil to taste.

I used the rest of the French bread yesterday making eggs in a canoe and took pics because it's a super easy meal with low effort if you have an air fryer. 1. Hollow out slice of bread. 2. Butter edges. 3. Drop in eggs. Season. 4. Microwave 2-3 mins. 5. Air fry 400 degrees 2-4 mins until brown.

One of my favourite things to find in the army archive is stuff about food & drink

Here are some details of mess supplies in Tripoli during the Italo-Ottoman War. Officers received mortadella, pecorino romano, preserved peas, fruit in syrup and a choice of no fewer than 6 types of mineral water!

Pot roast was very popular with the family! Did have enough for some good leftovers. Maybe hash? Or beef tacos? This is my MIL’s recipe she gave me 44 years ago. One can French onion soup, one can cream of mushroom. 350°F for about 3 hours. Veg go in after 1st hour.

Take a steaming bowl of hot rice. Crack a raw egg into it, add a little soy sauce, and mix it up. That's Tamago (Kake) Gohan, the breakfast of champions. To up your TKG game, separate the egg first, whip the whites, mix them in, then mix in the yolk. I once ate this for breakfast for 6m straight.

I had fun today! Beefsteak tomato topped with sautéed zucchini and garlic that I then puréed and mixed with balsamic, s&p, balsamic, and lemon juice. Roasted brussels and onions with salt, pepper, and garlic. Wish I had made a balsamic reduction to top the whole thing, but it was still lovely. 🍽️

Monday project. Reverse seared T-Bone recipe. Start with 1.5 to 2” cut. Dry brine in fridge with favorite rub 2-4 days. Pull and 1hr later place in 250 deg oven. Pull when inside temp is 100f. Sear on hot grill or pan to int. temp of 125-130f. Rest for 20 mins. Cut and serve with sides. Enjoy!

If you are someone who likes umami flavor, can I recommend toasted soy sunflower seeds? 

Heat sunflower seeds in skillet until toasted. Turn heat off. Add dashes of soy sauce / tamari to hot pan. Shake rattle roll. 

Add to salads, pasta, wherever you want a salty crunchy kick. Keeps for a while.

Every time I post about this someone finds it useful, so: brown butter pan fried tortellini. Needs: a few tablespoons of butter, fresh tortellini (if frozen or dried, boil for 2 minutes then drain and air dry a bit). Melt butter on medium low heat. Add tortellini. Stir until butter is browned.

The creek disappears into a dark tree-cave at the back of my property. Last year while clearing nearby brush I went in, shocked to find pieces of hydrangeas clinging to the sandy, pebbled banks. Their stems/roots were mostly petrified driftwood. I relocated 5. 4 have recovered. 2 have bloomed! 🌱 🌿

Fennel is both an amazing host plant and pollinator attractor. It grows best in the cooler months as it likes full Sun. After about 3 months of care the bulb should be large, ready to harvest and eat. If allows to fruit, it will reseed prolifically and is a great companion plant.

So pretty! And right on time. 3-4 weeks from agrobacterium rhizogenes innoc to visible RUBY roots. Could be a great addition to a biotech curriculum since it's extremely passive. Just refill the tap water and fertilize every weeks. Bleach the water used in exp to prevent release of microbe at end.

Pretty unruly, but that's because she almost died this year. I grew this Maple from seed 15 years. Last winter some mice decided to munch on her bark, killed her sibling, and almost killing her 🌱 . Light pruning only this year.

Have these in your garden? Cultivate responsibly, clip the seeds! Common Periwinkle, Bamboo, English Ivy, Daylilies, Tree of Heaven, Scots Pine, Kudzu, Creeping Jenny & Wisteria are garden faves but are invasive. Unchecked growth can outcompete native plants, altering ecosystems. Choose with care. 🌱

🌺 nasturtiums 🌸 flowers 🧑🏼‍🌾 gardening 🌱 plants 🥗 harvest 💦 irrigation

Harvested some aralia nudicaulis from the back forest, AKA sarsaparilla. There’s a large patch back there and I’m gonna make another batch of root beer this fall.  It’s prime time to harvest sarsaparilla — right before the leaves and stems fall.

Recovering from learning:
1) Pineapples grow from shrubs (not trees)
2) The plant is a perennial and can produce multiple fruits, each taking about a year to grow.
3) Wild pineapples are pollinated mostly by hummingbirds.
4) Of course, there's messed up colonial history of pineapple monoculture.

🌱 The wild columbine is blooming all over the garden. A native which is not planted so much as exuberantly spreads itself by seeds. Not hugely invasive and easy to pull up if it lands somewhere you really don't want it but a lovely early spring bloom to enjoy while waiting for other plants to bloom.

Found a big patch in a hyper-diverse little area I had been meaning to revisit 🌿

Fun fact about this plant: it is thermogenic, producing its own heat, both to help out the bugs that pollinate it and to melt any snow surrounding it

Garden tour continues: here are some closeup shots of the winter squash just starting to grow. The round one on the right is a butterkin. The hourglass shaped ones on the right hiding behind some leaves are the honeynut. They’ll both turn orangish brown as the weather cools. 🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: This mild winter has caused some roses here in RI to leaf early (and get damaged). Is this safe to prune? Yes. Normally you want to prune roses when the buds begin to swell, but before leaves. It's still early enough to prune plants like this. Do it soon though. 🌱

The family of Hawthorn (Crataegus spp) plants is large, but the members all have one thing in common - they’re pure magic! They grow almost anywhere and prolifically produce super healthy flowers and fruits. It’s prime berry season so let's get to know one of the oldest medicinal plants.

This bulb was an impulse buy around Christmas. At the time I was too depressed to even read the instructions, so I plunked it on this wine coaster I had left out and forgot all about it. Turns out that IS how you grow an Amaryllis. I think? Seems like bad luck to read the growing instructions now.

Sunchokes (Helianthus tuberosus) and sunflowers (H. annuum) can hybridize. The difficulty is that sunchokes bloom so late.

Fortunately, the giant sunflowers had a few secondary flowers that were shedding pollen. Now I just need to watch for seeds.

Menyanthes trifoliata L., Bogbean, flowers in late May. I know of 2 populations, one on the village pond, planted, & a wild pop. in the local fen/bog/mire (right). A monotypic genus, it has 2 morphs, like Primula, pin-eyed & thrum-eyed. The cultivated ones are pin-eyed, & the wild ones, thrum-eyed.
Perpetual spinach is the best to grow in small spaces when you live alone. Pick what you need, nothing goes to waste and it’s always fresh.  🌱

Succulents: Nature's "I've got your back" plants. Forget to water? No problem! With water-storing leaves, they're low-maintenance buddies. Perfect for window decor with minimal fuss. From the sassy Aloe to chic Echeverias, there's a succulent shade for everyone. Green thumb optional! 😉

Have you ever seen pink sunflowers? The Pink Pooh and Midnight Oil are just two that stand out. Sunflowers are heliotropic, which means they follow the sun's movement throughout the day. Native to North and Central America they have been cultivated since 3000 BC. Beautiful.

I prefer planting in autumn / end of summer as the soil is still warm, there is plenty of rain (hopefully) so the new plants often settle in nicely. I have planted several new Aquilegia vulgaris. A stunning, native species and popular with #bumblebees.

garden 🌱 report
jealous of @user's hellebores, I am inspired to report my #gardening mantra for this year:  "Gardening is making different mistakes every season." 

I am proud to say I have 9 blooms on my poor little azalea this year, that's up from 1 last year.

The precious Paperwhite Narcissus grows well in Florida, as long as you give it a cooler location to live in. Being one of the few varieties that doesn’t need chill to flower, they will bloom in clusters of showy, fragrant petals. Bulbs can be planted in either soil or a rock bed.

Despite the chilly cold front this week, new patches of toothleaf goldeneyes along the Salado Greenway went in for a fall bloom! These attractive asters grow in bunched colonies and can be up to 3-6 ft tall. I think I saw three or four patches in flower while out on my run yesterday. 🌼🌿
Lots of fab comfrey popping up. Its blooms will help early emerging pollinators & leaves shelter moths etc. After blooms are spent I’ll harvest the leaves for homemade plant food. 🌱

Planted more carrots today. 
... I'm using flat iron between the rows to keep the weeds down this time. Black polythene with slits in it did *not* perform well: in cyclonic winds it literally beat most of the carrot seedlings flat, so I've upgraded to solid steel mulch🦾🦾😂

TIL: Haas Avocado trees bloom March - May with each flower lasting only 2 days. The flower opens in the morning as a female, and then the following afternoon as a male. Seems wild and while they self pollinate the best harvests come from cross pollinating. 🌱

Spring is under way, the time we pay attention to flowers. But do we pay close attention? Look at the dogwood (L) with its big white bracts. That's not a flower, but an inflorescence - a cluster of many flowers. The individual flowers (R) are small, and not showy. Flowers deserve a close look.

These warm humid North Carolina days has done wonders to the garden. I tested out some crops to see what'll do well in my region & the tomatoes, peppers, cucumbers, mint, sunflowers (even after the deer demolished them) onions & carrots are doing well. The cabbage & beans are pest magnets though.

we're having early blossoms this year. it has also just started to snow. we almost never get snow. I'm standing in a graveyard watching peach and pink flowers gather snowflakes in their curves and dips. the poet in me is charmed. the scientist is alarmed

Previous owners filled this space with goutweed. Last year, finished lugging rocks up from the creek. I turned & plucked as much of the goutweed as I could, transplanted some shade plants & a mossy log from other parts of the garden, and hoped for the best. Quite happy with the transformation 🌱

The case for planting Allium: they are stunning and hardy. They are onion-family and a natural pest deterrent. They naturalize and multiply! They bring lovely height to a garden. Bees and butterflies adore them! 🌱

ZZ plants LOVE being overcrowded but this guy had stopped growing. 

Repotted him about a month ago in a slightly larger pot and he’s already teeming with life…4 new shoots! 🌱

What a difference an inch can make. 🙃

#Roomtogrow

Papalo in February in NH

Easily in my top 5 culinary herbs 😋🌱

Starting indoors in December to try and get one to seed has the delicious consequence of a leaf for me here and there until then

It's *very* strong and you only need a little - 1 leaf = 1/4c cilantro

Watermelon seedling finding a new home in thick organic mulch, a mixture of dried grass with cow and donkey manure.

We are doing a trial to determine whether the manure is broken down enough to support seedling growth.

If it is, and we get fruits, they going be delicious! 🌱

I bought 3 garlic bulbs today

with the sole purpose of letting them sit in the dark in a lower cupboard in my kitchen til they start to sprout and then I'm putting the cloves into the garden 🌱 bed and let the slugs feast on THAT fo' shizzle.  

Slugs - B - Gone

For fellow gardeners surrounded/ shaded out by black walnut trees 🌱: I sowed 3 tomato plants (sun gold) in my backyard, mere feet from the canopy edges of the dreaded juglone zone. Absolutely unaffected. Further context: my death-strip is the south canopy edges where squirrels drop the most husks.

the tiniest green shoots spring out on what I thought was a dead lemon verbena plant as bees buzz loudly all around. hard pic to take but a reminder that when you think there's nothing left to salvage there might be new life 🌱

Trying this winter sowing method at my community garden plot. I had success with this last year starting my breadseed poppies.

This year: MORE POPPIES, lettuce, mustard greens, broccoli raab. Experimental to see what they do: cucumber, delicata squash, and fennel. I had extra seeds so why not. 🤷‍♂️🌱

With higher temperatures and less rainfall, we are forced to change the way we plant food.

This image shows a row of black eyed peas growing between 10 year old date palm trees. The area was not tilled.

I kinda thing this is how it was done before we got smart. 🌱

I planted this Fuschia in the ground last summer because it got aphids and I just wanted it to live or die. It inexplicably overwintered (zone 6), regrew, and bloomed beautifully. Now that I know it can, I'm going to mulch it and cover it with snow and see if we can make it through another winter! 🌱

More work on allotment plot 2, digging a trench along the side to put in a vertical liner to stop weeds from spreading back in.
We did this with our first plot and it really helped keep it weed-free, I hadn’t actually planned on doing it again, and right now I'm regretting it!
#Allotment 🌱

Turk's Cap germinated in six days! Fourteen hour light cycle, 74 degrees. I put the trays under saran wrap to keep humidity up. Also scored the seed coats with a scalpel. Only one so far but damn I was expecting three or four weeks. 

If they all germinate well I'm starting two more trays! 🌱

I have repotted my monster orchid. I hope I didn’t damage it; getting the moss out from around all the roots was really hard! Now I’ve got it in a proper mix. Pro tip: do not repot your orchids in the bathroom. It SEEMS like a good idea until you have to clean it all up. 😬

This is the perfect season to grow Lemon Balm in Florida! Your plant will be flourishing with leaves, which should be harvested often. As the intense heat of Summer arrives, the plant will wilt and possibly die back. Cut it back and in the Fall it will likely come back to life! 

📷 Tanria Elementals

Rain = moving plants to the new garden areas built into the back hill along the creek. Too steep to mow, too hard to weed-whack. Not sure the Daylilies will have enough sun to flower, but the rest are appropriately shade-loving (hostas, phlox, Joe Pye, lungwort) 🌱

A known anti-fungal in the garden, Marigold assists with human ailments, too! It’s very good for skin issues and discolorations. It promotes the growth of healthy cell tissue of all types. Digestive and menstrual issues can be assisted with Marigold teas or culinary infusions!

Once the primary 🌱 agricultural focus of the region, #Meghri #Armenia has has shifted away from Soviet-enforced pomegranate orchards to the more profitable (and perishable) persimmon.

Deprioritized, pomegranates still get packed - but no new orchards get planted. Legacy trees straggle along.

Super stoked!! When I was growing up we had a lush passionfruit vine that would deliver fruit growing across the verandah. Someone pulled it out so I thought I’d reinstate it. Rapt to finally find flowers & fruit!! (Nice bit of alliteration there too! 😂)

Creeping phlox, muscari hyacinth, & tulips make a great combination either for pots or the ground as they all greet spring time in unison. They put on a spectacular color show to welcome warm days ahead…🌱

The winter rains turned my yard of mulch into a meadow, and the first California native plants are starting to bloom! Closeups: wartleaf ceanothus, pitcher sage, and blueblossom. (If you're just tuning in, this was a grass lawn 3 years ago)

Born in the tropical forests of Sri Lanka True (Ceylon) Cinnamon is well suited to grow in our zone 9b. This tree can grow up to 50 ft, or be pruned to live well in a container. It will not tolerate temperatures below 40 degrees, be sure to protect it from rare frosts. 🌱

Image via Tanria Elementals

The winter sun has emerged and my Haworthia cooperi is into it. This South African plant evolved translucent leaf tips because its native environment is so sunny & dry it prefers to grow mostly buried. It raises these clear leaf tips above the ground to catch light like little solar panels. 🌏🌱
$3.49 for a packet of lettuce seed, split in 3 lots and planted 2-3 weeks apart. After I’ve had my salads that’s about 50 small lettuces a week to share. 🌱

Helleborus x glandorfensis ‘Ice N’ Roses Red’.  I’m a little conflicted by the old lenten roses.  They are deer-proof and flower when color is desperately needed, but they are invasive if they escape into the woods.  The new, sterile hybrids like this one have the good without the bad.  🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: Lilium Red Velvet is a rich red lily. An older variety that's easy to grow. Flowers aren't as large as more modern types. Not rabbit resistant, although I've not seen the Lily Leaf Beetle on mine. Hemerocallis Hello Screamer in the background. 🌱

I noticed that Turk’s caps along the Salado Creek are going in for a fall-time bloom. They flower from late spring through late fall/early winter here. A good number of them flowered around June/July this year but the recent drought conditions kept them at bay for a bit. Glad to see them again! 🌿
It's -15°F outside and the days are only like 9 hours long but my Pilea peperomioides is flowering. I think these plants appreciate a good south-facing window.🌱

Unbelievable these tomatoes 🍅 were still growing in the veg patch a week ago, however, as green as apples. After a week in a paper bag in the cupboard under the stairs, they are now perfectly ripe and ready to enjoy.

I threw a few collard starts in the ground 🌱 in early summer, and I wasn’t sure how they would do around/under my peppers. Well… now I have collards for dayyyyyys. This plant is almost up to my waist. I’m making saag paneer tomorrow to go with aloo gobi from our own potatoes.

The new soil works! My tomatoes went from shrivelled to lush, sparse to abundant, fleeing this mortal coil to smothering my neighbourhood’s native flora. 

More than I can store. Family & friends won’t accept them. Cupboards are full. They’re in my bed. I dream tomatoes.

The vine is in charge.

Good evening. Post #3. Our allotment journey has really been centred around #nodig approach. We find it very productive and easy to garden as a result. With one plot, we've been able to keep the beds in use for most of the year and haven't bought any fruit or veg for at least the last 2 years.

Today's Gardening Advice: Certain wildflowers deserve to stay right in the woods.  Various orchids and trillium are difficult to establish in the average garden.  Orchids like the common lady slipper have symbiotic relationships with fungus.  Gardeners are too often tempted to try them and fail. 🌱

No 5 I am especially fond of the Goldilocks Buttercup, Ranunculus auricomus, on account of its dishevelled appearance, petals frequently imperfect, some of them absent altogether. Forgive anthropomorphism, they always look as though they got out of bed late and had to rush to meet the day

🌱🧪 Apple Watch circa day 30. 

The damp paper towel in the refrigerator for germination was the trick! 

8/8 Macintosh apples are doing well. 2/4 Johnny Appleseed Apple are doing well. The are just growing at their own pace. Transplant time soon! As I have a mix, I will get hybrids with these. 👍

I’m super impressed with these Indian Summer black eyed susans. They’ve been blooming since June and are not only still going on Halloween, but are mostly unfazed by the hard freezes we’ve had. Definitely something I’m adding to the 2024 seed lineup! 🌱

The rains from Hurricane Hillary in August have created a sort of second spring here in the Mojave Desert. Plants that typically bloom in spring and summer, like creosote and sand verbena, are flowering in October. It's beautiful but also a bit unsettling tbh

🚀🌱WE HAVE LIFTOFF!9 days since sowing, nearly half of these Canna seedlings are up. Pic 2 is a seed after scarifying on coarse sandpaper. Pic 3 is 'Cannova® F1 Bronze Peach' & as predicted, F2 seedlings show foliage variation - flowers will likely vary as well. Cannaceae

As long as there's good drainage, Borage grows in most soil types even in containers! Known to be a great companion plant in the garden as a decoy and is said to improve the flavor of plants it's grown with. Pruning flowers will encourage more blooming plus they’re delicious!

📷:Tanria Elementals

I nursed this bush back from death over the summer. The whole thing was brown sticks. I didn't even know it could flower. 

I'm bad at growing things and I have trouble maintaining the yard with my disability. And somehow, it blossomed in the fall anyway ❤️

Today's Gardening Advice: Be wary of the new "compostable" pots appearing at garden centers.  In my experience, they don't biodegrade and/or snap into extremely sharp blades under pressure.  If you find peat/coir or fabric pots be aware they dry out quickly as moisture wicks through the soil.  🌱

This spring, exhausted with work stress, I chose to take a break and grow things. I grew 8 leaves, 12 herbs, and 2 tomato varieties, all from heirloom (pre-WWII) seeds. Everything was grown in chemical fertilizer and pesticide free soil, with a little vermicompost. It has been amazing.

This Mandevilla used to climb a trellis over the old pond that is now empty. Had been neglected but now fed, watered and in a semi shade spot it has come back to life.
Still lots of warm weather to come so growth and flowering should keep improving.
Certainly worth saving.
🌱🇳🇿

And up past that slate was... Diplacus kelloggii! I love Phrymaceae and the tiny species with flowers bigger than the rest of the plant (look closely—most of that isn’t even a stem; it’s a corolla tube with a calyx at the base!) are my favs. This one was ~50mm tall and they don’t get any bigger!
🌱📷📸

This cool rainy summer must have been ideal for raspberries because they started the first week of July and haven't stopped except for a week or so in August. TBH the freezer is full and I'm getting tired of them. I may leave the rest for the yellow jackets. 🌱

Another native wildflower post! This is tenpetal anemone, a rhizomatous perennial native to the southeastern USA + northern Mexico. These cute little flowers bloom in the late winter and early spring, generally from February through May. They like sunny open spaces best, but do well in woodland. 🌿

One of the 'easy Sunday' plans is to plant some banana and plantain suckers on the farm.
I will plant this one as well, silk banana or some call it golden banana.
It is not the best type of sucker to plant. This is a water sucker, the better ones are sword suckers but it will still grow. 🌱

Missed y'all while @user and I were planning/planting a bunch of new gardens! Here's a small taste 🌱 tbh, new plantings are always a bit anticlimactic - many of the bushes pictured will reach 10-20 meters tall within a few years. Fall planting is all about structure & ground cover.

I'm sorry to tell you again, but fire increases the germination of Cistaceae seeds in contrast to summer-type heat (which reduces germination!)
Paper: fireecology.springeropen.com/articles/10.... Blog: jgpausas.blogs.uv.es/2024/02/18/s...

🧪🌍🔥🌿🌾 wildfire botany #PlantBiology #PlantScience

Two of our favourite allotment crops, Florence fennel and celeriac. The fennel was sown early to get a good start to harvest bulbs in June before it bolts, celeriac was sown early because it takes until October for the tiny seedlings to finally produce 1-2 kg roots.
#Allotment 🌱

🌱 In #syracuse #ny this forsythia bush is blooming in my neighbor’s yard. It should bloom in the spring, so this is wicked early.   Flowers blooming out of season is a bad omen. For example, spring flowers blooming in the winter means the temperatures are too warm & that has lots of ramifications.

Today's Gardening Advice: Thalictrum pubescens (also listed as Polygamum--same plant?) is a part shade loving perennial that tolerates moist soils.  This clump was found growing alongside a road in Newfane Vermont in a roadside ditch.  Attracts pollinators and easy to grow. 🌱

Maybe these 🌲 would have been bigger in bigger pots with added fertilizer and lots of work and care. But they are just fine by my estimates. They spent their lives in these same pots full of cheapo potting soil and received only normal tap water. Are all of the fancy soils and equipment necessary?

My nightshade seeds (peppers, eggplant, tomatoes) are coming up in earnest. I pre-sprouted them in a wet paper towel, which I find shaved days off the germination process and eliminates guesswork about seed viability. 

Aren’t they cute? 🌱

Last year’s dwarf irises—among my very favourite plants—were a treasure. This year we learn whether they’ll perennialize in the spots I picked for them, but as insurance, we planted another fifty. There can never be too many! They take up no space; their post-bloom foliage is narrow and subtle 🌱

🌱 Advice from gardeners appreciated. I know I should have planted my daffodil bulbs in autumn, but will it do any harm to them if I plant them now? Will they have a shot at blooming next spring? (I’m in NC, southeastern US, and daffodils are already in bloom here.) #plants #flowers #gardening

Multiplier onions are a win! Bought these after learning about them from SkillCult. Did not disappoint. Started from sets 2 months ago. Harvested sustainably for gronions as they grew, and each single set tripled or quadrupled.

Anyone else growing multipliers / potato onions?
🌱

For me, sometimes happiness is just having the luxury of time to spend hours photograping poppies😂 

2 different days, different light, different species, Papaver rhoeas and Papaver somniferum, growing as a food crop.

Erythonium americanum | Yellow trout lily 🌿
Erythronium umbilicatum | Dimpled trout lily

To my mind trout lilies are the definitive spring ephemeral, erupting in huge abundance before the trees leaf out and finishing their life cycle just as the darkness closes back in.

Want to see something super cool? I bought this succulent plant from Home Depot on clearance because it was on the verge of dying. I read that to propagate succulents, just leave the petal on top of soil and a new succulent will grow. LOOK AT THIS!

if you like pretty variegated plants (white and green speckled leaves) but you don't have the budget for a monstera albo, go look at variegated syngoniums. they grow super fast and make amazing leaves! 🌱

Sunflowers may be the symbol of Ukraine, but they're native to North America, where they were first domesticated in the Late Woodland period.

Seeds found in Mexico are all pre-contact, suggesting Spanish priests suppressed them to block their importance in Sun-worship cults.

bean of the week! Hyacinth (Lablab purpureus) is a perennial vine that produces striking somewhat flat purple pods reminiscent of snow peas. Edible if boiled well with several changes of the water. Used culinarily in East, Southeast, and South Asia, as well as East Africa. Plants are cold hardy too.

Went off to the Kawakawa seed, crop and knowledge swap this morning. 
Swapped a carob tree seedling for a pine nut seedling. 
Dragon fruit plant for a tiny pineapple plant. 
Met a lady who lives across the valley from us in a bus. 
Found out you can grow brussel sprouts up here. 
Win win win.

drying & collecting tomato seeds is very simple. u just take them out of the fruit, put them on a plate & leave them on the side for a day or so to dry. after that u scrape them off with a blunt knife or a fingernail, & tip them into lil envelopes & u have seeds ready for the next few years!

I just love the way a Monstera leaf grows. It starts fully formed, invisible inside the stem. And then the plant decides it is time and the vine lengthens and just shoves this giant, tightly rolled burrito out! You watch it unroll, going from nothing to a huge, intricate leaf in just a few days. 😍🌱

Potatoes were cultivated by indigenous people across the Americas, but likely 1st domesticated in the Andes region of Peru 7-10,000 years ago from wild species Solanum brevicaule.

Cultivated potatoes are all tetraploid, while wild potatoes can be diploid, tetraploid, or hexaploid.

Hello, friends! Zinnias certainly give you one of the best returns on investment in the garden. A packet of seeds and you have color and blooms all summer long and the birds and bees and butterflies go wild over them too. 5 out of 5 stars 🌱

Last few days above freezing. Pruned the calmondin back by almost half 🍊🌱(was growing weirdly anyway), shook all blossom bits down, gradually getting her ready to live indoors again.

Smells dreamy, I made enfleurage with calmondin blossoms in spring. Blooms every 4-5 months.

#FlowerReport: The one early daffodil is looking a little peaked, but another cluster on the other side of the house is blooming! Also I saw 3 Georgia blue speedwell flowers. Is that enough to mark it down as “in bloom” on my bloom tracker? I wrestle with this question every year. 🌱

These photos mark the first real life contact I’ve had directly due to Bluesky. I was lucky enough to connect with @user, a gardener local to me, who offered me some hellebore. Yesterday we met in person while my husband dug up her volunteers, and today I planted them! 💗💗🌱

I get a lot of people overwhelmed by the number of plants in my garden- I wish people understood that the more plants you have (and as closely spaced as is healthy) the easier gardening becomes! Living mulch FTW. 🌱🌱

This weekend, with ~five lbs of cherry tomatoes from the plant I did not plant, I made pickled tomatoes and pasta sauce. Tomorrow when I go outside, the plant will have magically regenerated at least another pound of tomatoes. I can’t keep up 😭🌱🍽️🍜

Many daisies in Aotearoa (New Zealand) are shrubs or small trees. Heketara (Olearia rani) is flowering now. This is a cluster of tiny florets: 12 outer white ray florets each have a bilobed stigma; 5 inner yellow disk florets, each with 5 stamens joined in a tube & a stigma that pushes out pollen.

We forgot to bring in one of last year’s hanging baskets until after the first several hard frosts and big snows. Since it contained violas, a few were still happy and healthy—and now the pot is filling up with seedlings! Hope that means seedlings in the garden in spring, too 🌱

Chondrus crispus, a red algae in which the little pustular fruiting bodies in winter glow with a rich violet-blue iridescence. It's often seen with the pink coralline companion, Corallina officinalis. In case you're wondering, rock pooling with your children never grows old. She was 36, I was 62.
Apocalypse seed banks are great, but have you tried the the soil seed bank? It holds life for generations. Care for the soil, allow the soil to thrive, and seeds germinate.

Yay, all my E. coli strains can't eat sucrose natively! Lots of options to choose from in terms of a house strain for my Flowers For Everyone project. I'll avoid the commercial strains and modify the genomes of my final choice. Top 2 rows are M9-Glucose. Bottom are M9-Sucrose. 🪄🍬🦠

Decided to plant potatoes on a whim in an overgrown area of the property surrounded by hedges. Had no idea if it got enough light or had good enough soil. Of all 3 areas I planted them, it’s flourishing best here. Sometimes rolling the dice pays off. 🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: Try some gardening adjacent activities in the new year.  Make a brush pile, put that half rotten log into the garden, add large rocks, start a compost pile, remove a patch of lawn, paint those reclaimed boards wild colors and put them in the garden.  Make an environment. 🌱

First wildflower of…winter??? Texas baby-blue-eyes are native annuals distributed from Texas, Oklahoma, Louisiana, to Alabama. They generally have an early bloom season, starting from around mid/late February where winter temps are moderate, through the end of spring, and dying back by summer. 🌿

5 months ago, I got the local horse stable to drop off a giant pile of manure & bedding. I sowed a bunch of seeds into it, mostly winter squash, which dominated the actively composting pile. I did almost nothing to it for the whole summer

Just harvested 436.7 lbs of squash

I love this time of year and watching our rose bushes and their stubborn, dogged persistence in the face of impending winter. (Please ignore the blackspot; we keep trying to find varietals that aren't as susceptible to it, but the ones that most thrive here are weirdly the ones that get it worst.)

Did you know saffron crocuses - which grow in your ordinary twenty-first-century garden - are basically clones of plants that grew in Minoan Crete, hundreds of years before Homer? Blows my mind. vertigrow.substack.com/p/saffron-cr...

Scrambled eggs! Scrambled eggs! 🍳 This cute native wildflower is often called “scrambled eggs” or “yellow Corydalis.” Across their native range you can find them blooming in and around rocky areas, creek beds, and hillsides from late winter through early fall as temperatures and conditions allow! 🌿

🌱 I bought this cactus when the 2020 winter lockdown was announced, and everyone was madly trying to finish Christmas shopping before the portcullis dropped. Took it home, all the buds fell off. Since then I give it the simplest care, an austere winter and a summer in the garden. This is my reward.

Added 3 #nativeplants (PNW) to habitat garden today, which will provide lovely blooms for humans and 🐝 and 🦋 next Summer & Fall.

Rose checkermallow (grown from seed!), sneezeweed (not a weed!), & blanket flower (my new favorite!). The latter 2 are native to most of US and great garden additions!

Today’s projects include sawing a fallen tree limb into 5-6 ft lengths to create a new vegetable garden bed for next year. Sod is turned. Just need to get some compostable goodies in there. I keep ostrich ferns at the fence line so in the fall, I can cut them down & use as mulch. 🌱

It’s weird to have plants from clippings from a friend you stepped away from. It feels weird to have this living thing from that person but it doesn’t know the frame from the day it was clipped till now. It’s just sitting there growing inside where it shouldn’t be and looking cute.

We had a lemon tree on the balcony that outgrew its pot. We gave it to the caretaker, who planted it in the garden of our block of flats. This year it’s groaning under the weight of dozens of lemons so I’ve just been down to relieve it of part of its burden. The smell in our kitchen is unbelievable.

My heritage tomato, a Cœur de Bœuf Rouge has finally ripened in the window & was ready for seed harvesting. The seeds were like orange segments in each bulge. I paid around $5 for this tomato. It came from seeds discovered in an attic that had been there for over 60 years & the cultivar was revived.

No dig gardening is my favourite way to garden. We planted these 3 Rhododendrons and 1 hydrangea in the lawn a few weeks ago.
Today I covered the lawn with cardboard and wood chip and made an edge with re-used bricks.
New border done in two hours without digging, for free. 🌱

"Fairy Pepper" is the name I came to for this new miniature variety. Most plants grow to under six inches, but this one is a little smaller.

The pods are very hot, much like the wild pepper ancestor (Capsicum annuum var glabriusculum) that was a parent in the original cross leading to this variety.

I succumbed to Black Friday and got myself more Greenstalk self-watering planters. Highly recommended if you are short on space or have mobility issues. I grew a ton of tomatoes, beans and herbs in mine and want to expand.

Not sponsored or anything, I just love them
Friendly suggestion, if you're not already, to harvest the moment your tomatoes start to get some colour. Especially now because the critters are getting extra hungry with the weather cooling down 🌱🥲
These white & pink flowers are Ice Wine Coreopsis (aka Tickseed) which is a fall blooming perennial that is a good alternative to mums because it doesn’t spread as aggressively. 🌱

I haven't weeded in my first year of my native plant bed and this is why. Every plant ID app told me in the early stages that this was horse weed, an annual that I hadn't planted. But now that it's more mature, it's easily identified as the goldenrod seeds I did plant.

Just gotta give it time 🌱
Every year I plant a few less tomatoes (last year I had 700 cherry tomato plants, this year it's 650), every year my yield goes up faster than I planned and I end up with lots of surplus.

Thyme is exceptionally easy to grow. It needs well draining soil and full sun, and loves to be trimmed. Waiting until just before flowering ensures the leaves have the most oil available. Our rainy season can damage the plant, but moving it indoors or away from the elements can help. 🌱

I am in the A period of gardening.

As in A zucchini, A red pepper, An orange pepper.

Lots of green fruit on the plants, so the avalanche should begin late August, I think. (We have late last frost, late early frost, I expect to harvest summer veg into October.)

I love Joe Pye Weed. Since last spring, I’ve been swerving a small handful as I weed-whack. Can’t keep all the natural flowers/plants along the creek’s edge (bad spot for ticks) but I encourage a few for helping slow erosion & just general prettiness 🙂 They are spreading nicely. 🌱

Hello, friends! One of the highlights of the garden at the moment is a huge stand of these swamp sunflowers. They always bloom so late that we think they aren't going to bloom at all but then they go wild! Nice late pollinator and food source for wildlife. 🌱

Primroses also kicking off in Rakesden, along with yesterday's hellebores. Spring is definitely starting to spring, and with buds showing green on this and that, pruning has already started--a bunch of St John's worts getting hard pruned today to clear off all last year's dead leaves and berries. 🌱

When we moved in the front garden was a sort of boggy grass that never thrived. Last year I dug it all out, sank some old log slices that were free off FB market place, and planted up the whole thing. It's quite small this year, but I'm hoping next year will be amazing. You really don't need grass 🌱

I don't buy seeds. I harvest them from veggies that I like. Now I'm ripening a heritage Cœur de bœuf rouge tomato. It's grown in a greenhouse in Canada from 60 yr old seeds discovered in an attic. This tomato cost me almost $5. I don't use imports. They irradiate them at the border for pest control.

Next up: the Texas yellowstar, native to Texas; Oklahoma; Arkansas; through to Coahuila. This pretty annual herb is commonly found in prairie-like habitats across north-central and southern east Texas, specifically along the Edwards Plateau. It does best in well-draining sand and limestone. 🌿⭐️

💙🌱TOO SOON? Well, this Spring is nearer than we think, and last Spring is in the rear view mirror - reason enough to celebrate the true electric blue of Gentiana acaulis 'Maxima'. 😃 This ground-hugger can begin blooming here in Chicagoland late March. Gentianaceae

❤️🌺🌱DECEMBER TREAT! Give tropical Hibiscus rosa-sinsensis like this 'Glamour Gal™' sun & warmth indoors & they'll respond with abundance, even in cold Winter short-day climates. Though they tend to defoliate & drop buds 'til days lengthen, keep 'em moving & you'll be pleasantly surprised.😃 Malvaceae

I am going to brag a little. Poast? This is alocasia melo. Rare, and very finicky. I moved it from Maryland to NY, and it is thriving. It gets a heating pad under the rocks, and lots of humidity but not much water. 🥰

Today's Gardening Advice: A number of perennials are easy to start from seed.  If you're willing to wait, it's a great way to go.  Echinacea, Lupine, Penstemon, Columbine, and Rudbeckia are all excellent choices.  Alcea (Hollyhocks), are super easy biennials to start. 🌱

My heath aster is blooming! I’m so excited about this stuff. I planted 3-4 babies last fall and it has spread beautifully. Keeps down weeds, flowers in fall when the pollinators need food, and is native to much of N America. Seems hardy af so far. Why don’t I see this everywhere as ground cover? 🌱

🌱 So.. I am having roaring success with orchids ,  these days.. 
Not in direct sunlight, plunge them in rainwater +feed, for 20 mins, every 2 weeks or so. Then let them drain. 
The end.

Harvested my Crapaudine beets today. It was an experiment...I tend not to have a ton of luck with beets because we have heavy soil. I had half and half luck with these-- maybe six or seven good sized beets and a bunch of small ones. I have a ton of greens, though, so that's exciting!

There are a lot more than four seasons. We have just begun Purple Haze, the season when our woodlands and stream banks are lined with riots of redbuds, Cercis canadensis. Redbuds exhibit cauliflory - flowering on the main stem - and ramiflory - flowering on branches. What seasons are you seeing?

Today's Gardening Advice: This is Gaultheria procumbens, known as American Wintergreen. It's an evergreen groundcover that likes woodsy, composty soil. Tolerates dry when established. Has small white flowers and red berries. Part shade to shade. Smells minty. Birds enjoy berries.🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: Harry Lauder's Walking Stick is a neat shrub for winter interest. All twisty branches and early catkins. Japanese Beetles and sucker growth can be issues. Like full sun and average garden soil. 🌱

Rhododendron ‘Heart of Pineapple’ in the greenhouse this morning.  The cross is [R. (macgregoriae x laetum) x saxifragoides] F3, hybridized by the late Bill Moyles. All three parent species are from New Guinea. Has shown surprising heat tolerance, given its high-altitude R. saxifragoides ancestry. 🌱

Paul thinks we’re piling ramial wood chips here so he has a comfy nap place.

But this is our favorite way to improve soil on a slope. The mycelium will kick in and keep them from washing down.

Next year, we may plant true tea or a pollinator strip in this 10’ wide band between garden tiers. 🌱

Peas! These tasty legumes are excellent for freezing and canning, though they’re delectable fresh from the pod (I couldn’t resist eating a few every time I checked on them).
They're also a spring garden staple. In some folklore, peas represent love and fertility. 🌱💚

My window sills will never be stylish, tidy or uncluttered, because there's always something green growing. This is mint (so easy to grow, indoors & out, indestructible & prolific) & spring onions cut-offs. They're now planted to use their upcoming green in a salad or for cooking.

Year 4. My theory of orchids is controversial to my mother. Her: light stands, watering/fertilizer schedules, struggles. Me: theory of benign neglect: north-facing window with reflected snow-light, irregular/forgetful watering, remember fertilizer once or twice per year. Bloom like clockwork. 🌱

When I miscarried, I buried my embryo in a houseplant. I didn't know what else to do. As part of my ongoing healing, I clone the hibiscus every time she needs a trim. Most have not survived. One is very healthy. These are her very first clones.  She is ready for a bigger pot.🌱

Tomato seedlings for high tunnel production. This is Kakao and Caiman.
I do not recommend these varieties for home gardens bc they both require protection to produce to their potential.
🌱
Kakao is similar to Kumato but a little better flavor and higher productivity in the hoop house.
Rose of Sharon sure is pretty but the fact that this one is growing from the center of a dense hedge should tell you to think real hard before planting one 🌱

So this cigar plant (cuphea ignea) has survived the mild winter so far despite being a tropical species, and this Californian nemophila menziesii all came up way too soon. Now we're about to have very freezing temps and I'm going to try and see if tenting them in plastic might help them make it 🙏🌱

Cosmos is the flower for the birth month of October and symbolizes balance and harmony.It attracts butterflies and is easily used as an attractive border or backdrop plant.Cosmos is simple and easy to grow from seed sown in the spring, readily reseeds itself,and tolerates a wide range of soil types.

Today's Gardening Advice: If you're looking for an extra tall, wispy perennial I highly recommend Thalictrum rochebrueanum. Native to Japan, gets at least 8' in my yard. Foliage stays low. Prefers full to part sun. Rich soil. No real pests. Foliage dies back on mine after flowering. 🌱

First year this baby will flower!🌺

Red flowering currant is a wonderful west coast native (US), which provides nectar for hummingbirds and habitat for numerous butterflies. Give it full sun and it will give you gorgeous early Spring blossoms 🌺🌺🌺

Here’s the very fast-growing pittosporum undulata. It pops up from fruit dropped by (transferred through) birds and it’s suddenly there, a whole tree. Once a year, its fragrance is astonishing at night. The rest of the time, it’s dull but provides deep shade. 🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: Magnolia virginiana, or Sweetbay Magnolia, is an excellent native small tree/shrub.  It produces creamy white, heavily scented flowers throughout summer.  May retain some foliage in winter.  Tolerates moist soil.  Tends to be multi-stemmed. 🌱

Man, these take me back. 

This is how we developed rolls of film before digital cameras. The film would go on those spools, then into a metal cannister. Pour in developer, agiate slowly, then stop bath, fixer, then a water bath. All in a pitch black darkroom.

Hang film strips on a line to dry.

This is my DIY darkroom setup in our laundry room where I make prints from my digital images. I use inkjet printed digital negatives to contact print traditional silver gelatin photo paper or do alternative processes in my UV exposure unit.

Question: How do you photograph a scene while keeping your kayak steady?

Answer: Suction cups my friend. Lots of suction cups! 🪠

Seriously though, I used a gecko mount to suction my DSLR to the kayak deck. I set a 10 second delay timer, and used both hands on the paddle to stabilize the kayak. 📸🛶

In case anyone want the recipe for taking a photo on photographic paper (normally it creates a negative) but making it positive
Normal paper dev
13.5 grams of Sodium Bisulphate in 250 ml of water mixed into 1g Potassium Permanganate to 250ml of water
Dev
Wash
Bleach
Wash
Dev (light on)
wash/fix

Today's Elliptical Cinema: The Frighteners, which I'd seen back in the day (as a mailed disc from Netflix). It's held up pretty well. I'd argue a charm in the relatively unsophisticated effects, by today's standards. Fox plays against type (sorta) and Combs is delightfully unhinged.

If Yakuza was a 1980’s franchise that got an early 90’s American movie adaptation it would be about the Italian Mafia in New York, Sylvester Stallone would be Kiryu, and Nic Cage would be Majima. It would be a cult hit that made no money in theatres.

There are two types of hetero relationships: one where Jimmy Stewart succumbs to a paranoid delusion and (maybe) pushes Kim Novak off a bell tower a la Vertigo (1958), the other is Kim Novak giving up witchcraft to be with Jimmy Stewart as seen in Bell, Book and Candle (1958).

Poster for THE MASKED BRIDE (1925), a lost silent drama starring Mae Murray and Francis X. Bushman

Josef von Sternberg was the film's original director, but he walked out after two weeks because he couldn't stand the sight of Murray any longer.

The Guru (1969). Michael York and Rita Tushingham team up again after Smashing Time for pop star travelling to Bombay to learn the sitar. Expected a light hearted cringefest but surprisingly interesting and earnest look at contrasting cultures. Filmed entirely in India.

Apropos of DAZED AND CONFUSED, what belongs with it on the Mount Rushmore of “launched the most individual successful acting careers” movies? THE OUTSIDERS? FAST TIMES AT RIDGEMONT HIGH? THE LAST PICTURE SHOW? DO THE RIGHT THING?

Nathalie Richard - 📽️ Irma Vep (Olivier Assayas, 1996)

Zoé is a warm and smirking costume designer with a crush on Maggie Cheung. Richard is so easy to like that she transforms that tertiary role into a major one that rises above all the other subplots in Assayas’ wonderful showbiz dramedy.

Movie 23: "The Raven" (Dir. Louis Friedlander, 1935)
Bela Lugosi's Edgar Allen Poe obsessed neurosurgeon seems to be taking revenge on Boris Karloff for his character's sins against Lugosi's in "The Black Cat." Granted, his character is also a sadist.
#UniversalHorrorChronologically

My last four watched on Letterboxd

GIALLO IN VENICE (1979) is a super scuzzy mix of video nasty and softcore porn.  It stands out for featuring the most egg-obsessed character this side of Edith Massey in PINK FLAMINGOS, a detective who eats at least 3 or 4 hardboiled eggs in every scene he's in.
A superb documentary feature full of real life. Varda explores a wasteland of discarded objects, wasted food and outcasts. Poetic and magical. Highly recommend.

There is a straight line from THE STARFIGHTERS (1964) to TOP GUN (1986) to this.

As in "There Has Never Been A Movie More Commissioned By The D.O.D."

Catherine O’Hara - 📽️ After Hours (Martin Scorsese, 1985)

The funniest Scorsese is a parade of hilarious women popping in to roast Griffin Dunne. O’Hara basically ties Teri Garr as the funniest one. As Gail, she’s the final boss: the driver of a Mister Softee truck who thinks Dunne is a burglar.

My Noirvember film #2, THE HUNTER’S DIARY (1964). Salary man’s double life as a cynical womaniser comes unstuck when his conquests start turning up murdered. Proto Lynch/Verhoevan overtones in this lurid, pitch black noir that pivots plot wise half way though but loses none of its tension

i got to talk to John Woo, which is a real thing i can't believe i get to type. we talked about his new movie Silent Night, his return to Hollywood, trying something new at 77, and his problems with many American action movies

#90 Phase IV (1974) - The only movie Saul Bass directed, the message he wanted to send out into this world, was, "We'd better obey these dang ants!" Totally unique with tons of macro photography of ants doing acting. A few great ant horror sequences and lots of trippy crossfades. Wonderful.

Protea (1913) played by Josette Andriot. (Probably) the first femme fatale / super-criminal in cinema. A master of disguise doing heists in a black acrobat suit, two whole years before Feuillade's Les Vampires.

I Love Maria (1988). Adrenalized mash-up of RoboCop, Metropolis, Weird Science and tokusatsu TV. Supposedly ghost-directed by Tsui Hark who also stars, and full of his gift for huge spectacle on minimal resources. A feast of wacky comedy, inspired action, and beautifully chunky '80s cyber-tech.

Jharrel Jerome - 📽️ Moonlight (Barry Jenkins, 2016)

Besides the small kids, the most underappreciated Moonlight performance is Jerome (now an Emmy winner) as Teen Kevin. His tenderness in that magnificent beach moment with Chiron (Ashton Sanders) makes for a brutal contrast with the bullying scenes.

Thinking what 10 pre-1970 movies the average idly attentive current moviegoer would be most likely to be familiar with.

Wizard of Oz
Gone with the Wind
Psycho
Breakfast at Tiffany’s 
The Birds
Casablanca
Rebel without a Cause (though they’ve not seen it)
Citizen Kane (same)

That’s 8. What else?

WHEN EVIL LURKS features my favorite line of dialogue of 2023: "Evil loves children, and children love evil."  The rest of the film is rad as well, reminiscent of Cronenberg's THE BROOD.  As with THE BROOD, this is a horror movie about divorce, specifically fears of your kids turning against you.

four years running, there has been a best picture nominee with a trans actor in a significant role:

2020: PROMISING YOUNG WOMAN (Laverne Cox)
2021: WEST SIDE STORY (iris menas)
2022: WOMEN TALKING (August Winter)
2023: BARBIE (Hari Nef)

Bushwick (2017). Everybody dunking on the Civil War trailer brought this one to my attention. Dave Bautista and Brittany Snow try to get to safety as Civil War 2 breaks out. A lean, mean, no filler 93 minute chase of the kind I thought they didn't make any more. And yes, it's on Tubi.

#82 Burnt Offerings (1976) - Oliver Reed, Karen Black, and Bette Davis in a Shining-like haunted house tale which came out before King even wrote the book. Bleak. Creepy flowers that multiply as the haunted house eats away at the family. Lost souls sucked into photos. Great performances all around.

MOSS ROSE is deeply, deliciously weird. Love the absurd logic of Cockney chorus girl Peggy Cummins blackmailing Victor Mature and compelling him to take her to his country estate so that she can learn to be a lady.  Love even more that we are supposed to root for her and want her scheme to succeed.

Diana Rigg passed away three years ago today. My favorite film of hers is THE ASSASSINATION BUREAU (1969, Basil Dearden). And it's comforting that her final film was also one of her best: LAST NIGHT IN SOHO (2021, Edgar Wright). Her timing and line delivery remained priceless.

And that final standoff.

Sergio Leone, Ennio Morricone, and the editing/cinematography combo of Nino Baragli, Eugenio Alabiso, and Tonino Delli Colli at the height of their powers.

Impossibly badass with an energy and a tension that stands unrivaled.

THAT is how you stage a climax for an epic.

Kelly Lynch - 📽️ Drugstore Cowboy (Gus Van Sant, 1989)

Lynch radiates so much cool in this film that it’s hard to dislike her no matter where the story goes. Her relationship with a poetic, superstitious Matt Dillon becomes a pained, raw, real dynamic of struggles between addiction and sobriety.

MST3K's eviscerations of 1950s industrial shorts/social hygiene films ("Last Clear Chance," "A Date with Your Family," "The Days of our Years," etc.) may be some of the best examples of cultural criticism ever produced.

Red Road (2006 dir. Andrea Arnold). A twisted erotic thriller as filtered through (now-global) UK panopticon culture. Kate Dickie is a taciturn CCTV operator on a ruthless #problematic revenge through a realm of gloomy Glaswegian techno-grime. Weird, nasty, unforgettable.

Idle intrusive thought: the Presidency was the hottest movie genre of the late 90s. From buddy comedy (My Fellow Americans) to action (Air Force One) to kid flick (First Kid) to romcom (The American President) to gentle satire (Dick, Dave) to biting satire (Wag the Dog, Bulworth, Primary Colors)

The Babadook, Jennifer Kent's debut film (based on the short, Monster) is a great film. I want that kids book that was in the movie.

Most of us have seen it. But she made another film in 2018 called The Nightingale. The movie is quite amazing. My wife was transfixed to the screen. Highly recommend!

In 2004, mathematicians at King's College London conducted a study commissioned by Sky Movies which concluded that THE SHINING was the 'perfect scary movie' due to a proper balance of elements including shock value, suspense, escalating music and isolated setting.

Movie people 📽️, what are sequels you think are better than the original? I've seen some "sequels bad" talk lately but some are really amazing! It's close but I prefer Godfather 2 over the original. Bride of Frankenstein over the classic. Maybe Exorcist 3 over the original but they're so different!

It’s that time of year for watching random horror movies on Tubi. Had never heard of The Ghoul (1933) and now I know why! It wastes Karloff and a good premise with a super slow plot. But it has good atmospheric cinematography and one shot in particular stood out. Remind you of anything?

Hagszissa (2017) is a deeply unsettling meditation on trauma. Set in the Alps in the 15th century, a woman who has been abused, isolated, and shunned starts to lash out. Is it witchcraft or madness? Beautifully filmed, at times opaque, and difficult to watch due to disturbing content

#265moviereview Filmsky 📽️
MINNAL MURALI
A tailor from a small village gets struck by lightning & develops superpowers & must fight an unexpected foe. This Indian superhero film delivers everything the MCU does not. It’s heartfelt, funny & serious. All of the actors give 110%
WATCH IT on Netflix 
4/5

Highly recommend this 1991 shot-on-video horror movie from Hawaii if you have a fondness for shoestring DIY movies made by a high schooler who you can hear yell “go” at the beginning of every other scene.

Edward Yang's YI YI (2000) is monumental: a cinematic expression of urban alienation on par with Hopper's body of work in its haunting, indelible beauty, following a Taiwanese family adrift, as we perhaps all are, in a sea of humanity whose currents remain cyclical yet unpredictable, unfathomable.
watching doom (2005) and realizing this works as prequel to karl urban's dredd (2012) the same way demolition man (1993) works as a prequel to stallone's judge dredd (1995)

Here’s a challenge for the horror lovers of Bluesky. If you’re up for it, I don’t want to demand labor from you or anything. Do you have any horror film suggestions where there is no fire involved? I love horror, but after being trapped in a fire so many are hard to watch. Would love suggestions 💛

Lisa Frankenstein: Kathryn Newton falls for Cole Sprouse's reanimated corpse and starts murdering people to fix him up, in Zelda Williams' macabre rom-com, that shoots for a Tim Burton meets Heathers vibe, but Diablo Cody's script feels weak, and the PG-13 rating blunts the much-needed edge.

Nicolas Cage did 10+ years of movies that sound awesome until you find out they were filmed in Romania. And they're all titled like

Hard Guns
The Night Accountant
Punched to the Bone
The Icarus Protocol
Hard Guns 2: Bullet for a Requiem
The Icarus Protocol: Phoenix Override: The Termination

Finally watched Talk To Me and can confirm from personal experience that the part where everyone is joyfully and repeatedly fucking with scary bad stuff like it's a hot new sleepover game -- which I've seen criticized as ridiculous -- is actually the most realistic part!

random Charles Grodin movie of the day:

Just Me and You (1978)

it’s a brilliant and hilarious made for tv road trip love story co-starring Louise Lasser (she wrote the script too)… it’s not available on dvd or blu, and i can’t find it streaming anywhere right now. but it is worth tracking down.

SEVEN-Ups premiered on 3/1/74 in NY! Other films you could see that day in NYC: Zardoz, The Sting, Man on a Swing, Busting, Papillon, Blazing Saddles, The Exorcist, Sugar Hill, Black Belt Jones, McQ, Day For Night, American Graffit, Serpico, The Last Detail, Day of the Dolphin.

Weekend counter-programming recs: EARTH MAMA and THEY CLONED TYRONE. 

Both great directorial debuts (Savanah Leaf, Juel Taylor), very different in style and tone, but at their core each features multifaceted black characters making their way through a fucked up system.

#AllOfUsStrangers is utterly devastating and won’t leave me. Growing up gay in a homophobic world has damaged us and attacked our self worth, this film speaks so much about that. It’s so relevant to the journey I’ve been on this past few months in learning to love myself. Thank you to all involved❤️

Watched The Circle (1925), a funny little comedy of manners by Borzage. Very short appearance by a very young Joan Crawford at the beginning. Always great to see Eleanor Boardman in anything, such a shame she retired so soon into the sound era. I could easily see her in Irene Dunne parts.

My #Noirvember film #12, Sean Penn’s THE CROSSING GUARD (1995). Spiritual successor of THE KILLING OF A CHINESE BOOKIE, Nicholson’s sleazy jeweller obsesses over killing drunk driver who took his daughter’s life. Peak 90s neo noir, what made crime films in that decade so aesthetically ugly?

Another one I love so very much that, like Ravenous, has finally started to get the love and attention it so richly deserves is Pontypool. It's such an odd little film. Shot almost entirely in a single location, with much if not all of the action taking place outside, offscreen. But it is so *TENSE*

Obscure film of the day: The legendary Abel Ferrara's only comedy to date, THE GO GO TALES, about the goings-on at a strip club! Willem Dafoe stars! Asia Argento! Matthew Modine! Played the NYFF, never got a U.S. release or dvd or streaming! ok.ru/video/256675...

DAVID HOLMES: THE BOY WHO LIVED (2023, dir: Hartley) a genuinely moving portrait of Daniel Radcliffe’s stunt double, who was paralyzed by an on-set accident gets perverted by the filmmakers insistance on making the story uplifting and redemptive despite what the interviewees actually say.

It's a great night to watch Lifeforce (1985), dir. by Tobe Hooper (Texas Chainsaw), written by Dan O'Bannon (Alien), based on the book The Space Vampires, has an unforgettable performance from Patrick Stewart, is a zombie apocalypse film and a love story, and happens to be perfect.

#Watched Marked for Death (1990) - Seagal, per usual, is a total dick. BUT then the film turns into a non-stop sleazy parade of wild car chases, cartoonish Jamaican accents, gratuitous nudity, squibs, Seagal’s girlish run, crumpled forearms, and Keith mf’in David. Gleeful trash. #filmsky

#Shocktober 2023 - Horror Rises from the Tomb (1973) - You know how Nosferatu is Dracula with the Bram Stoker serial numbers filed off? This is a vampire story with the vampire serial numbers filed off. Fun sleazy Paul Naschy Spanish gothic full of nudity and gore, like a trashy late Hammer film.
There’s more than one Road House, for instance the 1948 noir with Ida Lupino as a femme fatale who plays piano in a bowling alley while getting unwanted advances from Richard Widmark. It’s great!

#NowWatching Morgiana (1972) - High surrealist gothic from Juraj Herz (his Cremator and Ferat Vampir are essential). Two sisters (both Iva Janzurova), one sweetness and light, one simmering with resentment and jealousy. Borders on camp, Poe vibes, wild faces, glorious costuming, a delight. #filmsky

I’ve been watching all the Bond movies in chronological order on letterboxed VHS cassettes with a 9” TV/VCR combo set and here is my ranking so far: 

1) From Russia With Love 
2) Goldfinger
3) You Only Live Twice
4) On Her Majesty’s Secret Service
5) Thunderball
6) Dr. No

It's so funny when people say "Umm, this doesn't feel like a Criterion Collection release..."

My man, when they first got into DVDs, they released two straight John Woo action flicks in their first 10 titles. #12 on the spine numbers was This is Spinal Tap. #23 was Robocop.

I'm watching While You Were Sleeping for the first time and wow this guy's whole family is perfectly cast and directed. Everybody needs 1.5 seconds to be believably distinct and simultaneously part of an overwhelming family unit.

You gotta appreciate Halloween 3 for having the chutzpah to make a Halloween movie that had nothing to do with the previous two movies and didn't even feature Michael Myers. No popular series would even dream of doing something like that now.

Bill Gunn - 📹🎥📽️ Ganja & Hess (Bill Gunn, 1973)

In his low-budget vampire movie, former playwright Gunn took on the villain role. It’s a stabby, homicidal B-plot often about him skulking alone; his presence sells it, and his direction of a deeply strange and radical film makes it work even better.

I keep thinking about THE DISCARNATES (1988). I wish it was more wildly known. 

A lonely man meets his young parents in their prime, long after they've died, and starts visiting them for dinner. It's not long before he realizes that dwelling in memory has its price. Sweet and sad, until it's...Not.

#NowWatching Soul Brothers of Kung Fu (1977) - so-so Bruceploitation about Bruce Li and Lo Meng teaming up with foreign dockworker Carl Scott is dull until the last 20m of non-stop brutal fighting, including some shots that could have been in Story of Ricky. And Scott has chops! #filmsky

Is Last House on the Left the only horror film that defies nostalgia—where it’s still as gross and depressing and transgressive now as it was back then? 

At the time Friday the 13th was considered extreme ultra-v horror not for the faint hearted but no one thinks of it in those terms anymore.

I’m glad I went into Carnival of Souls (1962) without knowing its reputation as a low-budget cult film that used to get the RiffTrax/MST3K treatment. Instead, I took the movie seriously. And I loved it! Aside from the eeriness of the visuals, the rapey neighbor made me SO UNCOMFORTABLE

Hubert Koundé - 📽️ La Haine (Mathieu Kassovitz, 1995)

This gorgeous indie sensation made the world aware of Paris’ racial violence and riots. Koundé plays the level-headed pacifist of the three leads, a boxer reaching a breaking point about police brutality. It’s hard to take your eyes off of him.

IT’S A WONDERFUL KNIFE: A warm and fuzzy Christmas slasher that feels like a Hallmark special gone haywire. That’s a good thing! It’s as much a celebration of the holiday as it is a bloody killing spree. Charming, deadly, and with that same FREAKY or HAPPY DEATH DAY attitude.

Today in Things We Like: The Seventh Seal (1957), directed by Ingmar Bergman. One of the most beautiful and enduring pieces of cinema ever shot; its alternatingly grim and humorous depictions of medieval Europe inspired in many ways the creation of Old Moon!

Looking for a dose of horror comedy from New Zealand that *isn't* What We Do in the Shadows? Check out Housebound! it's very charming, very funny, and leans into the actual horror harder than WWDinS does. Rima Te Wiata is so wonderful as the mom, she's so sweet and annoying in the perfect mom way

Rewatching The Boys & god casting Aya Cash (& the writing) was so brilliant. She has this incredible delivery, mean yet disarming & charming, an effortlessly cool bitch you want to like you. The perfect person to seem like she's speaking truth to power at first, then actually be a nazi sociopath.

The Silencer (dir. Amy Goldstein, 1992) can best be described as "Naked Killer for white people" and it's close to being as good. A sleazy, ultra-fetish triangulation of grindhouse, arthouse and comic book. Foremost it is a movie about smoking and sick fits.

Clint Eastwood's HIGH PLAINS DRIFTER (1973) and John Carpenter's THE THING (1982).  Both are apocalyptic fables about mysterious, demonic figures coming to small communities and bringing discord and death.

A film that’s haunted me since it’s release in 1990 is Jacob's Ladder. Vietnam vet Tim Robbins sees demons wherever he goes & has no idea why. The film's all practical special effects have had an outsized impact on pop culture, influencing everything from other movies to video games, eg Silent Hill

we saw THEY CLONED TYRONE last night and it rules

there’s this old pulp novel called “The Homing” that does a version of this plot and I’ve read it half a dozen times, after a childhood of ignoring Dean Koontz’s awful writing because all that hypnotizing mind control stuff was so cool

Catherine Keener - 📽️ Lovely & Amazing (Nicole Holofcener, 2002)

The Holofcener/Keener duo has been one of the most reliable in indie cinema. Brenda Blethyn and Emily Mortimer dazzle, but Keener feels like a movie star here. Her flirtations with her employee (Jake Gyllenhaal) are awkward hilarity.

Oliver Reed - 📽️ The Brood (David Cronenberg, 1979)

The late great Reed played Dr. Hal Raglan, the controversial psychotherapist who may be central to a web of murders. Simultaneously the film’s mysterious dilemma character and its true starring role, this is a contender for his most gripping work.

Rewatched All The President’s Men (1976) tonight, it’s so great. Every journalism film in the last 40 years has been chasing this one. I think the key is a laser-tight focus on the investigation. No bullshit love story, no subplots, no whatever. It never stops moving, and it’s flooded with details.

Angel Heart [1987] dir: Alan Parker
A slow burn with an obvious spoiler right at the start but still an enjoyable 'thriller'. Parker excels here with the Trevor Jones score and Courtney Pine's saxophone adding texture to the lush cinematography. Still consider this Mickey Rourke's best performance.

re-watched A Goofy Movie after probably 23 years. wild to watch it with adult eyes and realize that both parties to the central conflict are essentially right and it resolves with them realizing that. also, a breezy 68 minutes long.

One thing that Spielberg does so well is put us face to face with primordial terror from dangers greater than ourselves. Like in Duel where man faces off with it's ultimate predator, a really big truck

You *will* be moved by a horror comedy featuring a very-much-alive-yet-elderly Elvis defending his nursing home from a soul sucking mummy with the help of JFK (who is also still alive -- and black!). Can The King regain his long buried mojo and save the residents of Shady Rest? Don't bet against it.

Date night with the hubs. Went to see BOTTOMS at the Drafthouse. It was good. Really skewers movies like SUPER BAD and the like by positioning it from the point of view of high school ladies and playing on the absurdity and bad behavior you accept from men but will balk at from women. Loved it.
Nobody is a perfect 90 min movie. Man on a vengeance tour, no filler, Bob Odenkirk, RZA, & Christopher Lloyd gun fight scene. I can think of nothing this film is missing.

Watched "Singles" today for the first time since it was released (gulp) 31 years ago. About what I remember, mildly amusing fluff. Except now it's a time capsule, capturing the Gen X Seattle heyday, albeit superficially. Matt Dillon steals the film, as the meathead no-talent grunge rocker.

Excellent lesser known gay movies if you're looking for something to watch

Days (Tsai)
End of the Century (Castro)
O Fantasma (Rodrigues)
Hawaii (Berger)

The last two really speak to two very distinct ways to be horny that I painfully relate to

Speaking of The Thing, I saw the other The Thing a few months ago. The Thing prequel. No movie is necessary, but some movies are really unnecessary. It was so bland. Like they were testing A.I. scripts out in secret. Whatever people imagined re the Norwegian camp's demise had to be more interesting.

Just watched The Sky’s the Limit (1943), loved it. Kind of a trifle, plot wise, but Fred gets to do some dramatic acting, and he does it well. Joan Leslie is fine but this is almost like a solo Astaire movie. The scene where he’s drunk and dancing through a bar destroying things is just marvelous.
