The French Connection plays on BBC1. A cracking print. An utterly fabulous movie. Perhaps the greatest New York film of them all? The sense of time and place is remarkable.

Twister, mission impossible, dragonheart, independence day, the rock, the cable guy, Michael Collins, Swingers.

Summer of 1996 may have been the most stacked movie summer of all time. 

All originals, wall to wall bangers.

Shock Treatment (1981) might not be as iconic as it's predecessor Rocky Horror but this almost forgotten sequel sure beats it in set and costume design.
Worth a watch just for the homophobia song that rhymes faggots with maggots.

Miller’s Girl (2024) Instant Classic. Simmering. 𝘯𝘰𝘵 𝘰𝘯𝘭𝘺 tension; but a classical plot line many are failing to grasp. Layering woven thru spoken word, maturation of longing & framing like Minnelli. Literature was vehicle for stealth Gothic Romance. If u don’t like books, you’ll hate this #filmsky

A friend & I have been working through notorious/reviled films. Last night was CANNIBAL FEROX (1981). These Italian cannibal films are fascinating. They follow virtually no narrative rules & stories exist in a pre-globalised international zero setting. The violence is full on, esp against animals

If you grew up with a consuming passion for ninja magazines, throwing stars, and samurai films like I did, you're likely to go nuts over PATH OF BLOOD.  Eric Power's movie oozes with adoration for the chanbara genre and it's truly a marvel of DIY filmmaking.  Ridiculously violent and gory, though.

watched LOLA (2023) and it was pretty dang great, especially for a found footage movie. 

two sisters in 1940 England build a machine that can pick up broadcasts from the future and instead of just listening to David Bowie and Bob Dylan, they decide to help in the war effort and everything goes awry
EVENT HORIZON is proof positive that a lot of individual pieces of a movie can be great - the concept, the cast, the production design - and the movie as a whole can still STINK.

I feel like I’m the only person who likes Abel Ferrara’s Body Snatchers (1993). For me Don Siegel, Philip Kaufman and Ferrara all made really good movies. I’ve never seen the 2000s version but three out of four interesting movies is a very good track record.

Can't remember the last time I had a film sneak up on me and hit me as hard in its final minutes as THE BIRD PEOPLE IN CHINA. Literally bumped the film up a full star for me - what seemed to be pointlessly diffuse in the way that Miike can be instead now reads as finding beauty in the ephemeral.

hey draculas.  today’s rec is CANDYMAN (1992).  a supernatural slasher/haunted house that is very CHICAGO.  it explores the legacy of racism, systematic and interpersonal, past and contemporary in a really sophisticated way through horror.  also not a funny one this time.  and TONY TODD is the lead
A perfect fantasy film. Fantasy before it became a genre -- a pulp hallucination of eldritch myth, held together with fishing line, glue and imagination.

Thinking about The Archers (as every film buff does) I just realized that THE RED SHOES and BLACK NARCISSUS are fundamentally horror films that happen to be family-friendly. Especially BLACK NARCISSUS. What a weird, astonishing Technicolour nightmare!

If you like con artist movies, catch Nine Queens (2000) before it leaves Criterion Channel at the end of the month. It’s a ton of fun, and has great details—like when they’re trying to swindle a businessman who’s being deported & throughout the scene a shredder is humming along in the background.

I finally saw Past Lives, which is lovely, and I appreciate that John Magaro resolved the whole "Jews can play Italians and Italians can play Jews" thing simply by being both. (He and Greta Lee and Teo Yoo are all excellent and the movie actually managed to make me want to take the Circle Line.) 👍

YOWZA YOWZA YOWZA! To De Cinema to see THEY SHOOT HORSES, DON'T THEY? (1969) which I could have sworn I'd seen on TV (probably pan & scan), though none of it seemed familiar. Solid 1970s social issue drama, not unlike THE HUNGER GAMES of its day, but with grown-ups.

"The Fly" (1986) was one of my favorites as a kid. Seth Brundell is such a dorky, harmless, and likeable character in the beginning. Watching him slowly transform into an actual monster as a result of a very human weakness (jealousy) is heart-rending and disgusting. 

8.5/10 snowballs ⚪️

My last four watched 

ARREBATO (1979) blew my mind.  I can't think of another film that gets into the addict's warped headspace so terrifyingly well.

Fifty years today, arguably Ray Harryhausen’s magnum opus, and certainly one of my seminal texts, received its London release. A truly astonishing cast, a hugely evocative Miklós Rózsa score, an inventive screenplay, and some of cinema’s most monstrously beautiful creatures!

Bringing Out The Dead doesn't get its due, both as a Scorsese film and as a film capturing the spirit of the late 90s, with possibly Nicholas Cage's best performance. 

It's surprisingly gothic in atmosphere and manages to capture Scorsese's twin passions of New York grime and human spirituality.

watched that new hulu original movie No One Will Save You because I'm a sucker for alien abduction shit and GOD DAMN it just kept impressing/surprising me at every turn. VERY good film, the lead actress sells every scene and the choice to have no dialogue at all really elevated the whole experience.

Jesus Christ, I forgot how amazing Hitchcock's "Lifeboat" is. I remembered it being a technical achievement but the questions the script asks about Nazism, eugenics, and evil are just as relevant in 2024 as they were in 1944. In a way, it's Hitchcock's most frightening film! And Tallulah is sublime.

Quentin Dupieux’s Smoking Causes Coughing gave me some of my hardest laughs of the year. Deeply silly, kinda lovely, best environmental message movie this year. Power Rangers via Tales From the Crypt. Great stuff.

Lady Bloodfight (2016). Remnant of 2008 in regards to hyperactive editing and Maxim male gaze, and you could predict every plot beat in your sleep. But earnestness and great visuals (from the cinematographer of Taken) make this rise above. Makes most other flicks at this budget look positively lazy.

“Oh, he does card tricks!”

Tonight’s movie: Continuing Stanwyck week with the tops, The Lady Eve. It never gets old. It was my first Sturges, 25+ years ago, and I distinctly remember it felt like a revelation—so lively & funny & heartfelt. The cast, to the smallest role, is perfect. What a delight.

Revisited Lake Placid (1999) this evening. Hot damn, that is a very 90’s movie. The dialogue belongs in a 90’s museum. Bill Pullman gives absolutely nothing to his role. But the CGI holds up surprisingly well, the crocodile still looks great! And of course very large. 🐊

Avenging Force (1986)

Bonkers Louisiana-set action flick in which a martial arts dude goes to war with a racist secret society called the Pentacle. Originally conceived of as a sequel to Invasion USA until Chuck Norris got cold feet.  A great watch for Mardi Gras!

Directed by Sam Firstenberg

Wish I could see a better print of THE SPIDER.  The version on YouTube is pretty murky. Even through the fog, the film's a hidden gem, highlighted by the splendidly staged and shot scenes of Edmund Lowe performing his magic act. Coulda done without El Brendel's hacky idiot foreigner schtick, though.

Deathtrap is on TCM now; a funny, clever Agatha Christie-esque whodunnit with Michael Caine & Christopher Reeve & directed by the late, great Sidney Lumet, who also directed the 1974 glamorous all-star version of Murder on the Orient Express; highly recommended!

Aside from the other benefits of a really good and tight monster movie script, one of the strengths of TREMORS (1990) is that it is a remarkably accurate portrayal of several Types Of Rural Guy in that era, doubtlessly contributing to it standing the test of time as a cinema experience.

#83 Phantasm (1979) - A film of the late 80s, copied heavily in the 2010s, was somehow released in 1979. Haphazard plot can't slow the exuberance of this weirdo warp-travel freak out. Silver blade balls, the Tall Man, BOY!, a guitar playing ice cream man, and goop dwarves from space. LOVE IT!!

Rewatched GLENGARRY GLEN ROSS last night. It’s a truly a prime example of how you can drop an audience in the middle of something, give them almost no exposition, and not only will they figure things out—but it will be damn compelling.

My #Noirvember film #13, Elio Petri’s INVESTIGATION OF A CITIZEN ABOVE SUSPICION (1970). A rewatch but I adore Petri & Volonte & think this stunning film has gone full circle & is, once again, a frighteningly relevant dissection of the neo-fascist mind set

Last night's watch, the Criterion Collection's restored  ISLAND OF LOST SOULS (1932). I've only seen the butchered, sanitized version that was on tv. 

HG Wells hated it, but it's a disturbing film, and Charles Laughton is magnificent as the malevolent Dr. Moreau.

In case you don't know, I thought I should mention that Tubi is packed with awesome horror movies. There's Horror in the High Desert 1 and 2, GonJiam Haunted Asylum, A Dark Song, The Invitation, Lake Mungo, The Descent. It's a cornucopia of dark delights.

I finally watched Footloose for the first time and I must admit I thought I was getting a corny teen romance and instead I got a surprisingly sober-minded and articulate film about how grief warps our sense of joy and risk and also I would fucking die for this guy and their wonderful bromance???

Not a great film, by any means, but for my money, the set up intro of I AM LEGEND with the cancer cure news program piece, followed by a shot of ruined New York THREE YEARS LATER is one of the most effective horror openings of all time.

Demon Queen (1987, Donald Farmer). Hypnotic, near-plotless SOV full of wicked vibes and droney synthscapes. Incredible banger soundtrack by Jan Haflin that sounds like Patrick Cowley (in both his italo/NRG and porn soundtrack modes). 50 minutes of magic. Hell yeah hell yeah

PASSAGES (streaming on Mubi) is a really well-done, excruciating slow burn film about the messiness of relationships — especially when they begin falling apart. 

It’s 90 mins of beautiful people having sex and/or behaving terribly. Just know that you’ll never look at Paddington the same way again

Last night's movie was Noomi Rapace Speed Skating War Movie BLACK CRAB. Pretty good high concept, Rapace is excellent as always, almost a comically Swedish movie but also dour as hell and a little direction less. Still, pretty good.

#TheQueenofMyDreams is a beautiful, heartrending exploration of the messiness of generational trauma and the fraught relationship between a queer woman and her mother - crafted in a unique love letter to Bollywood and all designed expertly to make me cry 😂 #TIFF23

such a beautiful & elegant minute-plus long crane shot in the funhouse. tracks the little brother making his way towards the exit as stragglers clear out. captures unspoken confusion of this kid by considering space around him. this kid's experience on this night is the best stuff in the film imo

Your _Bottoms_ movie review:

Stone cold instaclassic, Sennott and Edebiri heading up a perfect cast delivering brilliantly, Seligman clearly a genius as a comic director, the _Mean Girls_ of its time though probably even better, jokes stop to start, everything pays off outrageously. See it now.
I watched LORD OF MISRULE (Prime) last night and really enjoyed it. It reminded me of both Ben Wheatley and Robin Hardy's modern Brit folk horror, but from a mother's POV. Recommended.

I've finished rewatching The Texas Chainsaw Massacre (2003) in my full series marathon and... I still love and prefer it to the original. It has some beautiful shots, and though it can't match the first film's gross atmosphere, the constant feeling of dread is more than enough.

Watching:

An American Werewolf In London
Director: John Landis
(1981)

One of the best werewolf movies, perfectly crafted from beginning to end. I can talk about this movie for hours but what keeps me rewatching it is the level of quality executed on so many different levels.

Just rewatched The Train (1964). A badass action movie of course, but I’d be hard pressed to think of another action movie that looks this good. Fantastic B&W shots of locomotives, and of big burly Burt Lancaster, who moves like a locomotive throughout.

Watched Švankmajer's FAUST (1994) again. Probably my favourite of his Surrealist delights. Rabbit hole layers of wonderful strangeness and endless tactile invention. The lifesize puppets trotting around are never not very funny. One of the best screen devils. Proper magic of cinema, baby...

Finally watching what is, with the death of Michael Apted, almost certainly the last film in the remarkable, one-of-a-kind Up series (aside from maybe a reunion of some sort now that he's gone). These are very special movies -- deceptively simple but richly complex in their humanity. I love them so.

I watched Insidious last night because it's one of the few ~ good ~ horror films from the last 20 years that I hadn't seen yet and I simply can't stop thinking about how they landed on Cirque du Soleil Darth Maul for a bad guy when there was literally any other option on earth available

Still absolutely one of my favorite horror movies, not to mention all time movies. a queer coded movie about finding your weird tribe by allowing the version of you that denied who you are to die & be reborn? feat. Cronenburg effects & the man himself as the best Serial killer in the genre? hell yes

The Art of Self-Defense (2019) Jesse Eisenberg is 200% in his element in this. A movie that is labeled a dark comedy that is actually that! This is an enthralling satire of toxic masculinity gift wrapped in a wonderfully plucky, deadpan & morbid wrapping paper. I HIGHLY recommend! #filmsky

learning that Konaka wrote Evil Dead Trap 2 is v. unsurprising, "what if the lain guy wrote a giallo" is the films whole deal. televised grotesquerie, memetic paranoia, predatory cults, the lurking industrial under the sheen of the misogynist capitalist neon-soaked no-future- *the* Lost Decade movie
Day 2 #SilentFilmHorror Dr. Jekyll & Mr. Hyde 1920 A chilling, violent & strange film. John Barrymore transforms not only his body but his very soul in a virtuoso performance of degradation and evil.

The cinematic equivalent getting sucker punched with a roll of quarters.  Sure, the score's ludicrously melodramatic, the dialogue rife with moralizing.  Overall, though, the film's a satisfying slice of nasty goodness, with Lawrence Tierney at his Lawrence Tierney-est as the rottenest of thugs.

Thinking about a 10 film watching challenge where you take a movie and then watch it plus 3 movies from the same director, 3 movies from one of the lead actors, and 3 movies from the same year. 

So if someone recommended Halloween it might be 3 John Carpenter, 3 Jamie Lee Curtis, and 3 from 1978. 📽️

Just watched Dog Day Afternoon again. Amazing how relevant this movie still is today — gender, LGBTQ issues, celebrity, fame, class, police interactions. And it was made in 1974! No major studios would be brave enough to tackle something like this now.

Watched a gem of a movie last night: The Damned from 1962 directed by Joseph Losey, a Hammer films production. Oliver Reed stars as a gang leader who is a little obsessed with his own sister. Throw in a hidden government experiment and radioactive children and you've got a movie!

psycho iii (1986, perkins) 

loved this so much. disorienting use sound design & burwell's score. diana scarwid's performance is so molecular it doesn't even feel like acting, making her plight that much more uncomfortable against the cruel eccentricities perkins deploys throughout

Can't even disagree even a little bit. Jordan Peele showed us how a very intimate understanding of alienation and having your autonomy up for debate translates into writing real terror and putting it in the big screen

I really admire Gareth Edwards’ THE CREATOR. It’s kinda shocking to watch an original sci-fi concept in a theater. Also, it looks gorgeous. It’s incredible on a modest budget they pulled off something that looks real and not like a CGI cartoon.

40 minutes into "Final Destination," a movie I remembered as pants-shittingly terrifying, I have clearly suppressed all memory of (a) a teenager literally yelling "I'm NEVER going to die" and (b) Tony Todd saying, of Death, "you don't want to fuck with that mack daddy!" A delight, a lark, five stars

I've never heard anyone say MAD MAX (1979) is the best MAD MAX film, and it's not, but it warrants re-evaluation. It's scarily relevant.

MAD MAX isn't post-apocalyptic. It's about how the apocalypse sets in, as society gradually dies around us. It's about giving up on the fantasy of civilization. 📽️

Today's Elliptical Cinema: Walter Hill's Last Man Standing from 1996. A veritable merry-go-round of brutality, a western with gangsters, a remake of Yojimbo where the absence of nods to Red Harvest scream from the dusty desert. Still on Tubi for a couple weeks. Worth the visit.

Now watching a film I still wish had gotten a proper theatrical release. An upcoming release reminded me of it & made me want to revisit. It not only absolutely rips, but Amber Midthunder  kills it. If we're going to get sequels to classic films of the past, one can only hope they're all like this 📽️

Yuen Qiu - 📽️ Kung Fu Hustle (Stephen Chow, 2004)

Need I elaborate? The Landlady of Pigsty Alley is the greatest image and character of any Chow movie, a strong-willed snarker who steals the movie and fires sonic screams that destroy all matter. It’s one of the peak comedy performances.

🎥 filmsky

THE ABYSS: Still my favorite Cameron. The 4K restoration is astounding. It’s never looked this good, never. Ed Harris and Mary Elizabeth Mastrantonio give such great performances . It’s a shame they won’t talk about it. Masterpiece.

House on Haunted Hill (1999) Movies that are exclusively in one location, can either fail miserably or succeed triumphantly. This ensemble cast hit all the right notes in this classic. I enjoy a good unpredictable script. Chris Kattan; a wonderful buffer. High marks all around. #horrorsky #filmsky

Recommended: The Pigeon Tunnel, based on the memoir by John le Carré. It's Errol Morris and therefore dripping with style and Philip Glass, but it works. Reality is the greatest illusion. Cornwell could have been a movie star, by the way.

Ten favorite found-footage films I love:

Incantation
Host
Paranormal Activity
As Above So Below
The Borderlands aka Final Prayer
The Outwaters
Hell House Origins
REC
Cloverfield
The Blair Witch Project

Special mention: Once Cut of The Dead (because I can't decide if it counts or not)

Spooktober Movie night 2 complete! Watched Gaia and it was alright. Not the horror movie it was hyped to be but it did have a pretty cool idea around fungi infections. I thought the inclusion a religious worship of he earth was interesting as well. If you like suspenseful dramas I'd say go for it.

Fritz Lang’s HUMAN DESIRE (1954) is my #Noirvember film #8. Lang’s misanthropy is well used in this tale of a small town femme fatale, Gloria Grahame, who attempts to manipulate recently returned Korean War vet, Glen Ford, into murdering her abusive husband. Grahame absolutely devours this film

Angel Dust (1994). Subway murders, New Age cults, impenetrable mysteries. Sogo Ishii trades his wild punk filmmaking style for a surgically controlled, brooding murder thriller. A prophecy for nineties-style doom, predating Cure (which draws from this) and the real-life Aum Shinrikyo attacks. Eerie.

The 1987 Dragnet movie with Dan Aykroyd and Tom Hanks is a forgotten banger and was a really smart/funny way to adapt that old series. Wild they did it 25 years before 21 Jump Street and everyone acted like that was such a fresh concept when it came out. Everything is cyclical, I guess. Or we are.

#ItCameFromTubi Street Trash. Holy fuck this movie rules. If you like Robocop, this movie would make a fine pairing. Contaminated hooch causes bums to melt is very surface level reading of film that also feels like post 60s America contending w/ failures of that period.

I don't know what took me so long, but I finally watched Park Chan-wook's STOKER (2013). It's a beautifully shot, quietly intense thriller with rich visuals and outstanding performances from Nicole Kidman, Mia Wasikowska, and Matthew Goode. Blew me away. Available on Hulu. 📽️#filmsky #horrorsky

A strong recommendation for 'Poker Face', Rian 'Knives Out' Johnson's TV series which partially answers that question about whether Natasha Lyonne would make a good Columbo. Each episode is a compact movie and episode 9, 'Escape from Shit Mountain', is especially cinematic. Not bleak! Not grey! 📺📽

I had such a blast with Naked Killer. Delightfully weird, surprisingly subversive and as usual with Clarence Yiu-leung Fok, there’s a hazy dreaminess permeating each frame. His films, especially this one, feel like soap operas made by a horny lunatic. Really great stuff.

New 🎃🎬: Bride of Frankenstein which I’d never actually seen. Hard not to laugh at the hermit scene if all you can think of is Mel Brooks but every aspect of the filmmaking is so fabulous. Lanchester steals the film with her 3 minutes of screen time but I really loved Ernest Thesiger’s Pretorius too.

TAXI! reminded me a lot of gritty 70s movies like THE LAST DETAIL.  Like them, it's a downbeat meditation on male anger and alienation.  Cagney's superb as a cab driver with a hair trigger temper.  As for Young, she does the best she can with the underwritten role of Cagney's love interest.

Obscure ok.ru of the day. Long suppressed by producer Frederick Wiseman and almost impossible to see, the legendary Shirley Clarke followed up THE CONNECTION with the incredible THE COOL WORLD. Essential indie viewing. ok.ru/video/106104...

I know everyone expects me to say "Frank Miller's THE SPIRIT" but that's a genuinely good movie, you jerks. So I'm saying HOOK, which is a dumb movie, yes, but when adult Peter finally finds his happy thought, it gets me (as the kids say) "in the feels" every single damn time.

I've posted a ton of comedy, so to keep with the somber  mood set by The Wailing, let's talk about Saint Maud. What an amazing movie, with an all out performance by Morfydd Clark. Twin horrors of of religious zeal and being medically reliant on a stranger co-mingle here wonderfully, horrifcally.

#Watched Hong Kong Godfather (1985) - late Shaws has one of the most insanely gory heroic bloodshed finales I’ve ever seen. I can’t believe this came out BEFORE A Better Tomorrow. Also featuring the most insane destruction of a child since the sleeping bag smash in Prophecy. #filmsky
I know MST3K took the piss out of it, but honestly, Mario Bava's DANGER: DIABOLIK is actually stylish and fun. I wish more modern caper films had such a sense of style.

To understand the coming decades:

OUT
* 1984

IN
* The Veldt (Bradbury)
* Handmaid's Tale (Atwood)
* CHILDREN OF MEN (film, Cuarón)

Looking for a Valentine's Day movie to watch with your special someone tonight? I recommend the light hearted romantic comedy Miracle Mile (1988). This sometimes overlooked gem has a real heart and soul to it, an adorable meet cute at the beginning, and an uplifting ending. Check it out!

Since it’s scary season (the best time of the year), I’m sharing a horror movie resource I use a lot: doesthedogdie.com

I love scary movies but there are certain things I won’t watch. DTDD is a trigger warning resource with hundreds of possible movie triggers listed. 💜

HOLY SHIT. Everyone’s the hero of their own story; the tragedy is that you might be the villain of someone else’s. Poison for the Fairies (1986) is a beautifully dark Spanish film about childhood and magic that feels like a throwback to late-60s filmmaking. Must see
Just watched MERMAID LEGEND (1984). A dream-like slow burn drama about the wife of a fishermen avenging his death gives way to a bloody rampage soaked in arterial spray. Fantastic movie.

US 50’s: Kiss Me Deadly
World* 50’s: Seven Samurai
US 60’s: Faster, Pussycat! Kill! Kill!
World 60’s: Army of Shadows
US 70’s: The Parallax View
World 70’s: Touki Bouki
US 80’s: Repo Man
World* 80’s: Women on the Verge of a Nervous Breakdown

*assuming that the average US moviegoer hasn’t seen these

Never been an AFTER HOURS fan, but return to it every few years, bc that’s what we do sometimes with movies we want to like or figure out. This is about my 4th time and it hit different tonight than ever before. I get it now; I like it.

DEATH MACHINE (1994); endearingly loving tribute to the 80s genre flick, loosing a robotic ALIEN into a BLADE RUNNER-esque megacorp HQ during a DIE HARD-style heist. Brad Dourif delightful as an evil tech nerd incel. Stretches its low budget beautifully across its smoky, steely cyberpunk surface.

YOU WON'T BE ALONE (2022, #film)

*Phenomenal*. A witch comes of age in rural 19th century Macedonia. Think Terrence Malick-flavored folk horror, except he couldn't turn out something this textured. 

It's gorgeous, it's revolting, it's grueling, it's humane, and it's on par with UNDER THE SKIN.

A+

NEWS FROM HOME: So extraordinary in its cross between intimacy and remove, as Akerman's conceit makes each juxtaposition of letter and image resonate with so many absences and possibilities; a film impossibly, piercingly direct in its indirectness. Sublime.

THE POPE’S EXORCIST’S BODYGUARD is mostly fun due to the relaxed charisma of Crowe, who seems to be having a grand old time; and while some of the dialog is quite funny (mostly intentional), the story and execution are mostly retreaded rites. But yeah, I’d watch six more.

On RENFIELD:

I was prepared for horror and comedy. I wasn't so much prepared for plumbing psychological depths and Marvel/MATRIX-style action sequences combined with a police corruption procedural.

This movie includes everything PLUS the kitchen sink, but does it well. I enjoyed it!

📽️ Today's pick is Carol Reed's 1947 British noir film, Odd Man Out. After an armed robbery gone wrong, a wounded IRA leader flees a city crawling with cops in this densely philosophical, gorgeously photographed thriller. On Criterion, Max, Plex, Shout Tv, and Tubi.

Patricia Arquette - 📽️ Lost Highway (David Lynch, 1997)

Surprise: Lynch had somebody play a dual role. The eternal good sport Arquette is a blank foil to all manner of mania in this surreal film. Even those baffled or annoyed by Lost Highway can hopefully give it up for her femme fatale pastiche.

Delighting in one of my true comfort films, Pacific Rim, put through a monochrome filter

Stripped of colour you're left with only contrasts, with details, and you see the film as something different. Harsher and raw in a beautiful way
More Metropolis than monster thriller

*Adored* The Last of Sheila. So funny and sexy, cynical beyond belief. Cast full of people hotter than anyone’s ever been on screen. This features one of the most bananas bits of characterization (the casting too!)  I’ve ever seen, I almost screamed. Costumes by Joel Schumacher!

Just rewatched The Taking of Deborah Logan (2014), definitely one of the better found footage horror films. Great scares and fun offbeat characters, particularly with a fearless performance by Jill Larson as the titular Ms Logan.

I love watching THE CANNONBALL RUN. Burt Reynolds is faking his own laugh from SMOKEY AND THE BANDIT so badly he’s just making this bizarre strangled noise, and it won’t cut away, and you’re like “this was the #6 movie of the year”

I have a soft spot for the chaotic, of-its-time vibes of Gregg Araki films, what can I say. 

If you can handle 90 minutes of Rose McGowan you ALSO get Parker Posey killing someone with a katana which honestly is worth the price of admission alone.

I've got a great film recommendation for spooky season!

It's called "Junkhead" and it has a really dark and claustrophobic post-apocalyptic atmosphere, some absolutely horrifying creatures and really fascinating world-building. This film is an amazing passion project done by a very small team.

I liked Poor Things a lot: it captures the topsy-turviness of Alasdair Gray's brain, his acerbic wit, and his horrors at how cruel human beings can be. Like Frankenstein, both the book & the film ask us who the real monsters are.

Watched Girl Missing (1933) this morning, a well above-average mystery/comedy pre-code. This one has incredible fast-paced dialogue throughout, and it’s genuinely funny stuff. Farrell is just wonderful, this one’s a must for fans of hers. The plot is nonsense but who cares.

Breaking news in two things can be true: IT FOLLOWS is a brilliant, formally ingenious horror movie that absolutely did not cry out for a sequel; and I will be first in line to see what David Robert Mitchell does with a second shot at the premise.

Any film that includes Irene Ware performing an interpretive dance while an actor dressed as Poe recites "The Raven" gets a big thumb's up from me.  Though too silly to really work as a straight-up horror flick, THE RAVEN (1935) is packed with fun moments of absurdity/weirdness like this one.

watch Ravenous (1999) for a film that combines a sharp critique of manifest destiny, a suggestion that cannibalism somehow gives you super powers, deep homoeroticism and one of the wildest tones you've ever seen - all in one really weird movie

Just watched 1990: The Bronx Warriors (1982) on Criterion… thought it’d be a half-assed Warriors ripoff, and it was, but I kinda loved it anyway. It looks better than it has any right to look, it’s really fun throughout (I loved all the weird gangs), and Fred Williamson is magnificent. Good movie!

Putting this here in the hidden opinion vault: Authentic cut of Twixt is my favorite Coppola film. Feels like someone reversed the wiring on a guy maddin homage to PS2 survival horror cut scenes, seems like it should be nothing but archness but it is blisteringly, painfully earnest. Just incredible.

Setting a goal of thirty first watches for #Noirvember. Last night was Cruel Gun Story (1964) which very much lives up to its title. A violent heist thriller with a lot of proto-John Woo gunplay and a criminal protagonist who wants to use his ill-gotten gains to pay for an operation for his sister.

What's a "comfort" horror watch you don't mind playing often? 📽️

I used to watch The Lost Boys when a local station would run it on the weekend.

A+ soundtrack, lots of humor, fantastic cast, tense atmospheric tone that doesn't overwhelm, and the single greatest closing line in cinema, hands down.

No One Asked Me But™ dept:

Goliath (w Billy Bob Thornton, W Hurt) seas 1 + 4 are excellent. 

You can skip directly over 2 + 3.

Love the noir/ 'Vertigo'/ 'Catch a Thief'/ 'Chinatown'/ 'High Noon'/ Kerouac allusions.

All of this explained in huge detail (w spoilers) vissiniti.com/goliath-seas...

I know this is controversial to say but Blackhat (2015) is a really good movie. It's a little arch and silly for a Mann effort but that's because he's at the height of his powers and having fun. It's his Ocean's Eleven

Rewatched THE CONVERSATION (1974). 

A paranoid thriller that examines the thin line between perception and reality. Fifty years later, its themes about surveillance and technology still ring true. It's also got one of the best neo-noir endings ever put to celluloid.

#Shocktober 2023 - Baskin (2015) - my last damn Shocktober log, finally. This surreal, gory Turkish horror flick about a bunch of cops caught in a terrifying nightmarescape had terrific mood and imagery (and goes hard on the gore), but the overlarge, indistinct cast and fuzzy narrative detract.

The Thing. 1982. dir: John Carpenter

There’s a LOT to like about this film: the ensemble cast, Carpenter’s direction, Ennio Morricone’s score, Rob Bottin’s astounding physical effects work, the tense paranoia among the crew, and Dean Cundey’s exceptional cinematography.

putting on DAYS OF THUNDER for the first time in a million years while trying to go to sleep, and i can see why this movie is so big, nicole kidman and tom cruise do a great job playing “smart, beautiful, talented woman” and “charmless twerp who is comically and inexplicably out of his league”

Tonight's watch was The World, The Flesh, and the Devil (1959). A loose remake of this, The Quiet Earth (1985), is a favorite so I finally caught up with the original. Got mad at Hollywood racism all over again for not giving Belafonte a bigger career, he's incredible doing some real heavy lifting.

CLEARCUT (1991); can't believe I'd never had heard of this without Twitter. Graham Greene sickeningly sad & darkly mischievous as Arthur, as much a rage-filled man as a trickster spirit dragging 2 white men on a journey into the abjection of the colonised. A bleakly funny, melancholy, furious gem.

Saw a masterwork from Iran. "The Wasteland", directed by Ahmad Bahrami. 

Deeply indebted to Béla Tarr, shot in gorgeous B&W, with patient long takes. A contemplation on how capitalism has hollowed out the Earth entire. 

Absolutely my favorite film of 2020, if not the decade so far.

saw a BEAUTIFUL 35mm print of this film after a q&a with steven spielberg back in film school. this movie is a cinematic achievement and something the french new wave guys could only dream of (if you really want me to go down a rabbit hole, you can ask me what i mean by this). it's also racist.

#NowWatching Dr. Caligari (1989) - while there are technically “erotic” elements, they feel like a ruse, to lure the audience into a bizarre Brechtian fever dream of body horror, gender fluidity, and sexual insanity accompanied by buck wild practical effects and German expressionism. Wow. #filmsky

TALK TO ME - the latest A24 horror film wow’d me at SXSW 2023 after its Sundance premiere, and is now in theaters. A possession story wrapped in tension, brutality, and pangs of grief, this one got under my skin in all the right ways. 4 / 5 ⭐️ #FilmSky

The Matrix and John Wick are both Keanu Reeves action movies with no explicit supernatural elements yet I would recommend both of them as required viewing for the World of Darkness - Mage and Vampire respectively

Babak Anvari's UNDER THE SHADOW. Deft and restrained examination of war, religion, thwarted ambition, and the way the unseen creeps in through the literal cracks in our facades. Best jinn film ever made. Makes a lot of American horror look ideologically lazy.

First film on catch-up is THE APOLOGY, streaming on Shudder from writer/director @user; essentially a two-handed thriller about the cost of guilt and the need for accountability, the film is taut, tense, and completely riveting. The cast is aces. Don’t let this one slip by.

Just got back from seeing End of Evangelion in theaters. It's one of my fav movies, and I've seen it lots of times on video, but never on the big screen until now.

It's such a unique film that will never be repeated. It's grim, cool, visceral, stylish and unapologetically disarming. A masterpiece.

ENTRAILS OF A BEAUTIFUL WOMAN is fucking insane. Ultra pinky violence and not for everyone. Really gnarly effects in but never saw someone die in a horror film by a woman sitting on a man’s face and suffocating him? Holy shit. Last 20 min is a fucking trip. Loved it.

This will sound like a joke but it’s not: the Milli Vanilli documentary is absolutely fucking riveting. 

Having lived through that era I knew most of the details of the story, but the film is an amazing deep-dive into what seemed like little more than a punchline at the time. Highly recommended.

Lisa Frankenstein is funny, and gory, and a love letter to horror comedies of the 80s- to Little Shop, Beetlejuice, So I Married an Axe Murderer.

It's weird in ways that movies aren't really allowed to be.

Had it been made in '89 you'd have merch in every Hot Topic.

I know we all love the direction of THE QUICK AND THE DEAD, but the script is actually a thing of wonder. "What if a western was just the best bits of other westerns?" and it holds together perfectly.

I love Popcorn (1991)! 🍿 It kind of feels like a Goosebumps episode. There's reggae music, psychedelic dream scenes, inventive zany kills, and it takes place at an old creepy theater! It's such a blast.

Here's my free to watch Halloween movie rec: The Halloween Tree by Hanna-Barbera. with 90's cheesiness, the masterful turn of phrase in narration, and an amazing score, this film introduces all-age audiences to the idea that mortality isn't something to be afraid of. archive.org/details/the-...

THE CONFERENCE (streaming on Netflix) is a solid Swedish slasher film. It’s if PARKS AND RECREATION had a bunch of jerks in it, and those jerks started getting offed in brutal and gruesome ways, one by one. The characters/actors were great, as were the kills. A fun ride!

So many movies coming up that I really wish I could go see without knowing anything before watching. No trailers, no new photos, or posters, or any other marketing. Fincher's The Killer - I don't want to know anything about it before the festival screening. If Miyazaki can do this, so can anyone. 👍🏻📽️

Just rewatched Creed (2015)… I never saw the two Creed sequels, so I suppose I’ll do that next. This film is great because it remembers what made the first Rocky film great — an emphasis on humanity and characters. I love Stallone in this one.

My boss, who loves horror films, told me that she just saw this great pair of films "about this creature a bunch of scientists find in the Arctic." 

Readers, she'd somehow never seen the 1982 or 2011 The Thing. I recommend her horror films all the time, yet I never suggested those two. I failed her

The Last Boy Scout. 1991. dir: Tony Scott.

An absolute TOP TIER 90s adrenaline ride. Fire cracking one-liners from Shane Black, outrageous action set-ups [including the hand puppet Furry Tom], and blistering direction from Tony Scott. The cigarette scene alone is a classic. Recommended.

After this year’s SCROOGED rewatch, I am surer than ever: the monologue at the end is one of the best things Murray ever did. I know at the time it was lambasted (by critics AND one of the screenwriters), but it’s the most cathartically, productively unhinged Scrooge conversion there is.

Still thinking about last night’s viewing, THE LEGEND OF HELL HOUSE (1973). Loved it. For me it worked as an occult heist film: putting together the crew, scoping out the job, dealing with tensions in the gang etc. Apparently the Richard Matheson source novel goes harder, going to have to read it

𝐁𝐋𝐀𝐂𝐊 𝐂𝐇𝐑𝐈𝐒𝐓𝐌𝐀𝐒. WOW.
Where do I start? It TRICKS u into thinking it’s gonna be a generic B horror, then completely subverts u pulling u into Riley’s personal horror. Levels u out w the frat/sorority angle, then finishes u off by turning the metaphorical fight physical. Genius. #filmsky #horrorsky

Watching The Thing from
Another World (1951). I like it quite a bit. It’s on as good as Invasion of the Body Snatchers but it’s got that mood of Cold War anxiety and paranoia. I wonder if it scared audiences or if they started laughing when James Arness’ “intellectual carrot” shows up.  

Filmsky📽️

We've watched all four Invasions of the Body Snatchers  (1956, 1978, 1993, 2007) this month. Something I just now really got: each of these movies features a deep friendship (with varying degrees of romantic undertones) between two academics. 

The X-Files is just a series-long Body Snatchers!

The Goodbye Girl (1977) I have never seen Dreyfuss, let alone any actor, fully embody a role the way Richard does in this. He deserved the Oscar he won for this 100 fold. This was charming & FUNNY solely on his back, & it’s better for it. Saturatingly feel good. A MUST WATCH before u die! #filmsky

I think Lady Bird slightly underwhelmed me upon first release because it didn't magically hit me where I live out of nowhere like Mistress America (the greatest film of all time) did. But I have not stopped thinking about it since 2018. A miracle earworm of a film.

The thrill of seeing a classic horror movie for the first time continues with THE INNOCENTS. Miraculously beautiful chiller from Clayton and Capote. Victorian estates through the lens of Freddie Francis’ B/W anamorphic widescreen photography is just breathtaking.

This weekend's watch. SEX, LIES & VIDEOTAPE (1989).  Haven't seen it in 25 years at least. Forgot how good it is.

Made for a scant $1M, it won the grand prize at Cannes, & Spader won best actor. Brilliantly written. Marvelous characters.

If you haven't watched it in awhile, highest recommendation.

Filled some big movie gaps over the past month — They Live, After Hours, Blade, Blow Out, Spartacus, The Long Goodbye, Poltergeist, Presumed Innocent, The Innocents, Internal Affairs, Treasure of the Sierra Madre — can’t recommend this sort of project enough.

All you clever folks already know this, but Soderbergh's THE LIMEY is a masterpiece. The clever, non-linear editing elevates what could have been a somewhat pedestrian plot and not a single member of the cast sounds a wrong note. One of Terence Stamp's best performances.

#NowWatching The Holdovers (2023) - lovely little “poor little rich boy” drama with terrific performances. Shockingly old fashioned, and I don’t mean that as an insult, or at least I mostly don’t. My wife enjoyed it too, at least to the extent I could get her to pay attention.

this is exactly why she’s brilliant and singular. an accretion of objects, tableaux, people floating through rooms, liminality, all adding up to a *story*. no ponderous plot or didactic message. true cinema.

Watched the original Woman In Black today, it's fantastic. My favourite kind of ghost stories (and films) share a certain bleakness, a shadow that spreads over the characters so that they're doomed from the very beginning. British ghost stories and Japanese ghost stories seem to do this best.

14/31

TETSUO THE IRON MAN (1989) is cyberpunk body horror for fans of Lynch, Cronenberg (either one), and industrial metal music. A mad masterpiece. Efficient as hell at under 70 minutes. The sequels, particularly BODY HAMMER, are also cool pieces of body horror weirdness.

Now on Shudder.

My #Noirvember film #15, Robert Siodmak’s THE SPIRAL STAIRCASE. Mute housemaid is stalked by a serial killer who is terrorising the small town she works in. Incredibly atmospheric, beautifully shot, genuinely suspenseful film that also functions as a proto slasher.

Just rewatched BIG NIGHT. Fantastic, even though made when a film could be about a genuinely “big night” even though Galactus isn’t trying to eat the world and the story is just an exploration of brotherhood, with a lot of great Italian cooking thrown in as “special effects”.

LEAVE HER TO HEAVEN: A Tech-noir-color masterpiece that’s unafraid to go to dark places. Gene Tierney (an absolute smokeshow!) gives a crisp, chilling performance (Oscar nom!). Costume design is impeccable. Cinematography is exquisite (won an Oscar!). I gasped loudly twice.

Dark August (1976) is definitely a hidden gem. It’s full of interesting filmmaking choices that mostly pay off. Lovely cinematography, lived in performances, and a queasy, anxious score—there are many things that make this worth seeing even if the plot line is a bit thin

More?!

11/31

GREEN ROOM (2015) is a horror film, I care not what ye say to the contrary. It's also one of the best films of the past ten years, to my mind. A movie that left me breathless and clutching my seat cushions like a newb. Deeply upsetting because it feels too real in days like these.

Watched Raging Grace (2023), a horror film about the mistreatment of Filipinos in England, it’s very clever and unique, I’d be hard pressed to compare it to anything. Reminded me a bit of Jordan Peele’s approach to horror. Recommended.

The Dunwich Horror (1970) is a very horny yet deeply unsexy movie. Silly and hamfisted—but I did like the psychedelic representation of the monster. “Mind-altering” works just as well to describe encounters with unfathomable being as it does the effects of hallucinogenics

Ed Lachman’s cinematography for EL CONDE is mesmerizing: its exquisite, uncanny abstractions of shadow and light are the film’s main artistic triumph, at times genuinely evocative of Dreyer and Murnau in its visual and spiritual depth. Sumptuous, awe-striking work.
10th or so viewing of MULHOLLAND DRIVE hits as hard as the first. Every single second of this movie is riveting. Exactly my kind of weird.

I rewatched The Fog (1980) yesterday and it finally crept its way into my top 3 Carpenter films. As much a note-perfect classic ghost story as a film ABOUT classic ghost stories, and telling them. a love letter to Arthur Machen, M.R. James, and especially William Hope Hodgson.

#Shocktober 2023 - Exorcist III (1990) - I usually can’t stand exorcism movies because fundamentally they’re all just redoing The Exorcist. Kids in bed, cursing, barfing, power of Christ compels you, blah blah blah. But not this one. Blatty is way too weird for that basic bitch shit. /1 #film sky

My 4th #Noirvember film was FEMALE JUNGLE (1955). Absolute bare bones b-noir about the investigation into an actress’s murder, it still manages to pack some narrative punch & has great camerawork. Lawrence Tierney chews the scenery as lead & it is Jayne Mansfield’s first big screen role

#Watched The Boys Next Door (1985) - goddamn! Think ‘80s spring break teen comedies full of shenanigans, except Spheeris moves the sociopathic nature of the leads from unexamined subtext to explicit text. These guys think they are the heroes of a sex comedy, not the villains of a thriller. #filmsky

I don't want to oversell it, but I really enjoyed this horror-ghost-pandemic period piece, and it has a lot of tricks up its very capacious sleeves, and it looks great. 

The wonky dubbing actually enhances the weirdness. (I didn't even know they dubbed movies anymore.)

This was a dreamy coming-of-age flick with a morbid edge. A teen in love with her 30s stepbrother suspects he’s a serial killer.. and still keeps trying to flirt with him and even blackmail him into sleeping with her? Kind of a genius premise lol. Pretty sweet up until the killer comes into play lol

If you're looking for some seasonal slashery films that *I* think are better than their IMDB scores, these were fun:

Hell Fest (2018)
Scare Package (2019)
The Last Matinee (2020)
Vicious Fun (2020)

Introspective and thoughtful they are not. But still fun.

Legend (1985) Pure dark fantasy 80’s crack right here. The sets were breathtaking, practical effects; the creature puppetry & makeup was astounding. The detail was ORNATE & PRISTINE. The world was a character in itself; it made the ludicrous plot work. I’ve never wanted to fuck Satan more. #filmsky

So, couple additional thoughts on The Killer (2023). First, proof that too much Morrissey will drive a man to murder. But otherwise, superfluous and annoying VO aside, solid decent fun, very much in the vein of the Tony Arzenta-style Eurocrime flicks it emulates. Also, felt very Parker. #filmsky

2010: The Year We Make Contact (1984) has got to be one my favorite movies with the least justification for existing. No, 2001 did not need a sequel but as everything gets worse I find its warmth and humanity a life raft. youtu.be/CmCMIJsYvZM?...

I won't spoil it, but TWILIGHT DINNER has possibly the greatest, grossest opening line of all time.  An impressive film overall.  While the sex scenes are abundant, they manage to be both disturbing and alluring.  Indeed, this is a provocative horror movie about sex, not dissimilar from Cronenberg.

Well acted, well cast, and walking that perfect tonal line between silly and menacing. 

I know Halloween is the time we throw in HOCUS POCUS, but if you don’t mind losing the seasonal flavor, give WARLOCK a watch if you haven’t seen. It’s almost the same movie.

Just saw The Dark Knight again.

Heath Ledger elevated the whole film.

Without him, the movie is good, but his outstanding performance is why I'm up late watching it so many years later.
Watched Tremors on SyFy and it still holds up. A simple premise executed in brilliant B-movie fashion. Imagine trying to get that made today.

the amazing mr x is a twisty, highly-atmospheric horror/thriller from eagle-lion films about a phony spiritualist. it’s pretty great… pretty low budget but the photography is beautiful and the script is tight and clever. shades of the uninvited and nightmare alley….this one is legit. check it out. 📽️

Watch Edward Yang's Yi Yi (a masterpiece!) and try to keep track of how many fucking characters that movie introduces that you're still able to hold perfectly straight in your head. It's gotta be nearly two dozen. A miracle movie.

Saw CORALINE on the big screen today! A fabulously detailed realization of a deeply creepy but fully relatable story. I had the oddest feeling throughout that, having only seen it on small screens before, I had been playing with the dollhouse version of the story, & was now seeing the real thing.

First watch: The Chase (1946, dir: Arthur Ripley). A very unimaginative title for a wildly unpredictable noir. Very glad I didn't read the synopsis before watching this. It starts predictably enough before going a bit ... Shutter Island. Well worth 90 minutes of your time!
📽️
absolute masterpiece. a horrific story about how greed corrupts everything. loved every frame of this quietly devastating film, and I could have sat for another hour.

Palm Springs (2020) really impressed me. It's not the greatest film ever, but it is one of those rare movies that quickly established its tone and expectations before accomplishing exactly what it set out to accomplish in a tight 90 minutes. Plus, it was fun! 📽️

Tonight's Hooptober movie was Requiem, which isn't exactly a horror movie, but is horror-adjacent with its plot about potential possession. Pretty good movie! Really shows how easily Schizophrenia could be believed to be demonic possession.

I watched Michael Caine thriller The Black Windmill (Don Siegel, 1974).

It'd just be an ok, slow-burn spy/actioner, but stylish direction, a great cast filled out w top character actors (see alt text), & excellent location work, incl a hovercraft crossing of the Channel, make it quite a lot of fun.

THE EMPTY MAN, one of the few horror movies to delight me in recent years as it resolutely refused to follow anything close to a pattern, is on HULU. It is quiet and weird, and should not be watched in the background. Check it out.

I will take recommendations for weird (wyrd) horror in replies.

The very best film I saw last year opens tomorrow across the country! A woman in the afterlife revisits herself every 10 years to kill the woman she was to become the woman she must be - while shot like BARBARELLA on mescaline. A work of mad genius I've seen 3 times now. 2024's first MUST SEE film!

🎃🎬1: Barbarian rewatch! Seeing it for the first time knowing NOTHING AT ALL was the right call. Surprise makes it all the more terrifying, fascinating, disorienting, funny. But watching again you can really appreciate how perfectly constructed it is to explore real world fear the way only horror can

Tried watching DARK WATERS a few times before finally making it all the way through today.  Always had trouble getting past how mega derivative it is of Fulci and Argento.  The film hasn't changed but I guess I have as I found myself accepting/enjoying it as a fun tribute to the two Italian masters.

THE EXORCIST hasn’t lost a shred of the sheer power it held over me as a kid. Horrific, vicious animal of a horror film that claws and bites at the soul. Will never lose its place as one of if not THE scariest films ever made
My first horror movie of the season: THIRTEEN WOMEN, a pre-code "slasher" in which horoscopes predict doom for a group of friends. The best part? It's under an hour. Streams on Criterion Channel.

Something Wicked This Way Comes is the best of the dark Disney era, by far. The perfect balancing act between nostalgia for a childhood that is almost over and nostalgia for a childhood that is long past is bittersweet and beautiful. 1/2

#NowWatching Out for Justice (1991) - Flynn’s a real director with a hell of a supporting cast. Still, you can only compensate so much for the gaping charisma hole that is Seagal’s Det. Gino Felino (!!). BUT 3 absolutely classic scenes of Seagal walking into a room and fucking EVERYONE up. #filmsky

Oh hell yes. This movie deserves all the awards. It's a queer comedy wrapped in drama with so many layers of grief and acceptance and love. Incredible performances, and the fight choreography and cinematography are both awesome to boot.  
variety.com/2023/film/aw...

MAY DECEMBER: A perverse, gripping film that resists easy categorization. It employs diamond-sharp formalism and performance to make an already disturbing story into something even darker and sadder and stranger. When you laugh, you may not be able to say quite why. I loved this so much.

Last week, @user recommended POSSESSION (1981), which proved to be on AMC+. What a gorgeous, messed up, delight of a movie! The acting is incredible. 
Not remotely scary, but suffused with feeling.

A befuddling, sickening, intermittently fascinating flick.  For whatever reason, director Lewis Teage and co. decided to make an inspiring underdog story out of this tale of a Philly dude forming his own violent vigilante squad.  Not nearly as good as William Lustig's VIGILANTE, but worth watching.

NO ONE WILL SAVE YOU pits dangers from above against those rooted deep in the soul, following Kaitlyn Dever as she wordlessly fights to survive extraterrestrial horrors while struggling to admit that she has any right to do so.

Terrifying.

Heartbreaking.

This is how it’s done.

🎥 saw CARRIE last night for the first time in a while—after a few years of other horror films good, bad, and whatever

I can’t get over how much I love this one, how rich in empathy it is, how finally sad. De Palma’s style made it seemed snide and detached to me at one point; now I can’t imagine why

If you’ve never watched THREADS, know that 1) you probably should, it’s brutal, crushing and almost terrifyingly realistic, 2) it’s story, about the people who stay, isn’t horror but it has influenced so much horror over the years 3) it was a made for TV movie

THE IRON CLAW is fucking amazing. Riveting, heartbreaking, tragic, and glorious. Hands down one of the best movies I’ve seen all year. It’s like Linklater’s THE WRESTLER. Whether you know the story by heart or are hearing it for the first time, you’ll likely be as blown away as I was.

Watched After Hours the other night. It's an odd film that's kind of hard to describe in one post why it's so good. It's well crafted and plays with tropes and expectations in fun and anxiety-inducing ways. Very recommended for folks that like black comedy & surreal odysseys. Free on Roku🎥

Watching Nope again for the first time since I saw it in the theater. Peele is so good at making a movie that’s better to watch the second time around, so many details laid down early that are easy to miss when you don’t know what they will signify later

Just revisited Miami Vice (2006). It has the same problem I have with most of Mann’s movies (heavy on style, light on substance). But it’s an exceedingly stylish movie, Mann has a hell of an eye. I just wish it wasn’t entirely bereft of thought. But it’s fun and it moves and it looks terrific.

Caught 'Charade' at the @user  

Love this film. Has great humor, a nice mystery & a fantastic cast. 

It's enhanced on the big screen. More than that, Music Box is one of those classic theaters, complete with a live organist, that makes film an emotionally moving experience.

One of the rare remakes that surpasses the original. "Invasion of the Body Snacthers" (1978) is smart, unnerving, and gory. Everything you could want, plus mini-afro Donald Sutherland! You can't help but ask yourself while watching: "How would I fight back?"

9/10 snowballs ⚪️

YAAAAAAAAAS :D

The Good, the Bad, the Weird is impossibly awesome.

I blind-bought the movie not honestly expecting it to be much more than a largely conventional Korean remake of The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly, and ended up being surprised and blown away by its unique vision.

HIGHLY recommended.

15/31

The general consensus is that John Carpenter's PRINCE OF DARKNESS (1987) rules, but I still think that undersells the movie. THE THING is the masterwork of his 'apocalypse trilogy' and IN THE MOUTH OF MADNESS is fast becoming a new favorite. But don't sleep on this one.

NIGHT OF THE HUNTER (1955, Apple, #film)

A strange, grim, oddly-assembled mess until it suddenly congeals into a terrifying tale of man's evil. It’s moving, soul-sick, and occasionally visually stunning. Powerful, riveting work with deeply arcane cinematic sequences.

Hou Yao (1903–1942) was a pioneering Chinese film director, screenwriter, and film theorist. He wrote and directed many films including The Discarded Wife (1924), Romance of the Western Chamber (1927), the first Chinese film shown in Western countries.

Mr. Badii is a middle-aged man intent on committing suicide. Driving in the hills above Tehran, he searches for someone to bury him after his death. He meets an assortment of different characters, but each have reasons to turn down the job.

The 'Einstein Cross' (Q2237+030) looks like five luminous objects in a tight cross formation.

It isn't.

Here's the light curves of 1635 supernovae measured in four colours.  They brighten and fade in a couple of months, but their peak brightness is always about the same -- allowing us to use them as standard candles to measure the acceleration of the expansion of the Universe.

It's Alnitak season! Here is a start for the Horsehead and Flame Nebulae that surround the bottom bright star of Orion's belt. This is 54x300s (4.5h) with a dual narrowband filter. I like the contrast and detail of the nebula regions, need to work on star reduction and internal reflections.

Eta Carinae lies in the center of the Homunculus Nebula. A star on the verge of supernova, it initially went through a "Great Eruption" in the mid 1800s that ejected stellar gases and formed the nebula seen here. It is the only star known to emit natural LASER light, here in the ultraviolet spectrum

Today's space plot: the sun-synchronous orbit is a particularly useful orbit; its height is proportional to (cos inc)^(2/7), the blue line in the plot. See how almost all sats in this region lie close to the line! (points near 400,97 started off near 500,97 and moved left due to atmospheric drag)

Roy Kerr submitted his paper describing the spacetime around a rotating black hole OTD in 1963.

Physicists had been searching for decades for this solution to Einstein's field equations. It describes, at least approximately, every black hole in the Universe. 🧪 🔭

A pleasing evening calculation, prepping for my Introductory Astrophysics class:  

If the Sun is orbiting the Galactic centre at the typical stellar rotation velocity (~225 km/s), how many times has the Sun been around the Galaxy during its ~5 billion year lifetime?

Answer: around 20 times 💃

🔭🧪

This is 6h 30m (78*300s) total exposure on the Wizard Nebula NGC7380, in the northern circumpolar constellation of Cepheus. I captured this data last night at a focal length of 765mm with an OSC cooled astrocam through a dual narrowband filter. 🔭🔭🔭

The Extremely Large Telescope that a European organisation is building in Chili is ready in 2028 and will provide images 16 times sharper than those of Hubble. This telescope is able to take direct images of earth-like planets that orbit other stars.

ESO-website about this project: elt.eso.org

This swoop of red nebulosity is called Barnard's Loop. It's huge. Framing all of it is a challenge. Above it on the left is the reflection nebula M78. Below it is the dark nebula "The Boogeyman." This is only a little over three hours. That was all the clear sky I had. #Astronomy #Astrophotography
Starfleet named the ship Voyager, after a NASA probe that got lost, fell in a black hole, became a god, and tried to destroy Earth. In retrospect, maybe what happened next was inevitable.

Through most of history, there was an unbreakable rule about studying the heavens: You can look, but you can’t touch.
Missions like OSIRIS-REx have broken that rule, changing the way we think about the universe. My latest Invisible Universe column: invisibleuniverse.substack.com/p/bringing-i... 🧪

THE SWORD nebula, a mass of dust & gas, resides in constellation Orion. To the naked eye it appears as the brightest ‘star' in the hunter’s sword. This image released in 2006 is a combination of visible & ultraviolet light from Hubble telescope as well as infrared data from Spitzer Space Telescope

This is 47 Tucanae (NGC104), a globular cluster about 15,000ly from Earth and about 120 ly in diameter. It's the second brightest glob in the sky and has about 10,000 stars. 
Data taken at Obstech, Chile by SWOS team Hanson, Parker, Mazlin, and me.  Processing by me. PWCDK24 with SBIG 16803 camera 🔭

Holy crap, the Euclid commissioning images look absolutely incredible. I can't wait to see what it tells us about dark matter, dark energy, and how galaxies form & evolve. (Euclid will observe galaxies over 1/3 of the full sky, as opposed to JWST, which sees deeper over a much smaller area.)  🧪🔭

My pet theory about black holes is that each one contains its own universe, and our own universe is the interior of a black hole in another universe. There’s speculation about this (“white holes”), but obviously it’s untestable and doesn’t explain where the first universe came from. But I like it.

From Evan Lewis: A bright flash of high-energy gamma rays heralds the merger of two dense neutron stars. What’s left behind? For the first time, authors have detected a follow-up radio burst which may hint at the nature of the merger’s remnant. 🔭🧪

Just went outside to look at Saturn. My 'scopes are packed up but that's OK. Just knowing those photons my eye is detecting left that planet an hour ago, after traveling over an hour from the Sun and getting reflected by molecules in Saturn's cloud tops... pretty cool.

This is the NGC-5427 galaxy, seen by Hubble and processed by me! 🔭

This observation, which was made in February 2022, is part of a program (#16691) that is meant to observe supernovae in galaxies pretty much close to us. 

I used these filters: F606W (B), F606W+F814W (G), F814W (R).

The Voyager 1 mission was supposed to last 5 years. And like a lot of these things, NASA planned for it outlasting its initial function, and had the ingenuity to make that happen, but 45 years is a really good run

This is how space debris (probably something like a spent rocket stage) looks like in a long exposure. It's slowly fading in and out as it tumbles through space and reflecting sunlight (either from the sun directly or via Moon reflection). 🔭🧪

These are fairly crummy photos, but they are just from a regular zoom lens plus a teleconverter. It’s shocking to me that you can see Jupiter that way. I don’t think I’m hallucinating the cloud bands in one, and in the other you can see three of Jupiter’s moons in orbit. #astrophotography

International Space Station slipping across the Moon's face as it flies at 8 kilometers per second, gently kissing Tycho crater. That crater is 53 miles wide, so while the station almost looks like it's orbiting the moon, it's actually 1000x closer to us. Bedtime space tweet, photo: Andrew McCarthy)

another weekend night spent imaging our gassy neighbors!

this time processed using infrared light as the luminance channel to make a false color image.

the bright dot is jupiters moon Io, which reflects a lot of infrared light apparently! 🧪 🔭

The "red sky paradox" (if most Earth-sized planets orbit red dwarf stars, why don't we?) is slowly but surely evaporating away, just like the atmosphere of the red-dwarf-Earths. Most of these "potentially habitable" worlds are likely barren, atmosphere-free rocks, similar to the Moon.

Well, I'm never quite fully satisfied with my work on Saturn, but here's probably my best shot with last night's data during the opposition (when Earth and Saturn are closest). 

This took about 16000 x 15 ms video frames over 4 minutes, and integrated the best 50% in "lucky imaging." More below🔭🧪

Just thinking about how wild it is that we understand, in detail, the fusion of <10% of the Universe's baryons into helium minutes after the big bang under physical conditions completely different from stellar nucleosynthesis (lower densities, higher T, abundant neutrons). Cosmology is awesome. 🧪🔭

This is NGC 2392, a tiny planetary nebula often referred to as the Eskimo or the Clown Face. I shot it for four hours last night and only ended up with half an hour of useable images. Still it shows a fair amount of detail for something so small. #Astronomy #Astrophotography

In distant space outside our galaxy is... something big. Known as the Great Attractor, it's the gravitational centre of the Laniakea Supercluster, millions of times as massive as the Milky Way, and we can't see it. The galaxy itself is in the way! But gravitational waves aren't blocked by matter.

6 (SIX!) planets orbiting a nearby star all dance in sync. This is pretty remarkable, yet also inevitable.

Fascinating: The Mars Express orbiter has detected enough water ice buried beneath the Red Planet's equator to cover the entire planet.

Astronomers assume that a large part of Mars was covered with water billions of years ago. It is possible that simple life forms occurred on Mars at that time.

Can you tell your Hayabusa2 from your OSIRIS-REx? 🛰️ The JAXA & NASA missions both returned an asteroid sample to Earth. But sample return is crazy difficult, and there's no established road to success. The 2 spacecraft therefore used very different tech. Here are 7 differences between the pair!

There's an upper limit to how much mass non-rotating neutron stars can have, the TOV limit (once thought to be ~3 solar masses, before collapsing into a black hole).

New paper states max mass: 2.25 M_Sun & radius: 11.9 km. 

Implies GW experiments have detected lightest black holes already. 🔭🧪

🔭 Using a Star Analyser 100 with my eVscope to look at Messier 57, the Ring Nebula, to get a spectrum of the entire field of view (including stars). The Ring (left, middle) is a planetary nebula. Center is blue-green light from doubly ionized Oxygen & right of that is red light from ionized hydrogen

S4717, a star orbiting the Sagittarius A (Sgr A) black hole in the center of our Milky Way, travels 15,000 miles per second, making it the fastest star in the universe.

The #Hubble images used in this animated science visualization present a full rotation of #Jupiter. It's not a real-time movie, but snapshots of the planet, taken in January, have been photo-mapped onto a sphere, and the model is then rotated in animation. 🔭 🧪

A nice reintroduction to deep sky imaging last night: this year's look at the Andromeda Galaxy and two of its satellite galaxies (M32 and NGC 205).

Helium on Earth is mostly product of alpha decay of uranium & thorium & offspring.

An image taken with a 8.2" telescope. It's a 17m exposure, 17 arcmin on a side, limiting mag V ~20, 4 arcsec pixels. It doesn't look like much. But the telescope is on New Horizons out in the Kuiper belt. It has the darkest sky ever seen. Great for the cosmic optical background of the Universe.

The supermassive black hole at the center of our galaxy is named Sagittarius A* (pronounced Sagittarius A-star). Ever wonder how it got its name?

Because the compact radio source (the black hole) was "exciting", it was marked with a *, like excited atoms. 🔭🧪

ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2003ANS....

Astronomy fact: Aldebaran (Alpha Tauri) is, on average, the fourteenth brightest star in the night sky. Located close to (but not part of) the Hyades cluster. 210,000-420,000 years ago it was the brightest star in the sky (apart from the Sun). Photo by me. 🔭 🧪 #astrophotography

I use kitchen blender settings to categorize the galaxies. The Milky Way is a stir and M87 is a puree. M82 looks like it was just ice cream on puree but then someone put in some root beer and it blasted out all over the counter. 🔭

🛰️
 Astronomers have detected Euclid's Falcon 9 second stage rocket; per orbit derived by Bill Gray, it's already left the Earth-Moon system and entered a 0.964 x 1.072 AU x 0.8 deg solar orbit. Euclid itself, per JPL Horizons data, is currently 860,000 km from Earth, crawling more slowly out to L2.

This is Neptune in true color. Some fun facts about Neptune: it's made mostly of hydrogen, with a lot of helium, methane, ammonia, and water mixed in. It has the fastest winds in the solar system, and it rains diamonds. Also, no homophobes live there.

From last night asteroids (363) Padua and (481) Emita. The streaks in the image show 90 minutes of their motion. They will be even closer together tonight and closest on Monday, December 18th, when they are less than two arc minutes apart. 🔭

Ariel, moon of Uranus - January 24th, 1986 from NASA's Voyager 2 spacecraft 🔭
This view is close to true color and brightness as the human eye would see it (yep, Ariel is gray...)

This view was made from a composite of green, clear, and violet frames, and calibrated against published spectral data.

Rubin Observatory will measure the ghostly glow of innumerable stars stripped from their home galaxies and left to wander vast intergalactic space. Such "intracluster light" will help us understand the elusive dark matter known to pervade galaxy clusters rubinobservatory.org/news/fossil-...  🔭

M42, the Orion Nebula, is the nearest stellar nursery to Earth. New stars are formed here in the hot gasses of the nebula, and even some small star systems are being born! At 1500 light years away, it is far away, but close enough to appear as a smudge in the sky to the naked eye.

“supermassive black holes at the centers of galaxies feed on normal matter, allowing the black holes to generate magnetic fields that fire matter back out into the void. These winds collide with intergalactic material, which, in turn, affects the growth of new galaxies.” 🔭

Based on data sent from Odysseus back to Intuitive Machines on Earth, IM believes the spacecraft caught a foot on a rock and is currently leaning against a lunar rock. However it is pulling in solar power and has a 100% charge and they are ready to deploy scientific tools. 🧪🔭🌙

🔭This is NGC 1269/1291 a cool barred spiral about 33 mly. It has an interesting inner bar and an outer rings. Lots of little galaxies scattered around the image. Data taken at Obstech, Chile by Hanson, Parker, Mazlin and me. PWCDK24 with SBIG16803 chip on L600 mount.

Voyager 1 captured the first evidence of an extraterrestrial volcanic eruption OTD in 1979. The plume in this image was discovered by astronomer Linda A. Morabito, working as a JPL engineer at the time, and announced a few days later. 🔭 🧪 👩‍🔬

Our sun is a member of a low density star cloud known as the Local Fluff which is currently tumbling on the forward swell of a density wave known as the Orion Spur. From a distance our star cloud probably looks like a big sparkly pillow. 🔭

A metal-poor star in the Milky Way’s halo may have formed from the remains of the very first stars to flicker into existence, opening a window into the properties of our universe’s original stars. aasnova.org/2024/02/26/h... 🔭🧪

Fun fact: there are only 9,096 stars in the night sky visible without a telescope. Less than 10,000 even in the most perfectly remote location.

Split between north and south hemispheres, the most perfectly clear night sky you've ever seen had about 4,500 stars visible.

It's really doing my head in as to how JWST papers are reporting that there are galaxies, interacting galaxies, relatively 'soon' after the Big Bang. Like, how did they have time to build up like this?!

The Great Orion Nebula is a challenging object to get the correct exposure. I used several applications of GeneralizedHyperbolicStretch in PixInsight to tease out the surrounding dust while keeping the brighter parts from blowing out.  Captured this back in Feb ’23. It’s about 10 hrs of data.

I just went out with the binocs and found Uranus in about 15 seconds. 😁 I looked up the star patterns first and spotted them in the sky pretty easily, and there it was. Looked like a star, but unfortunately I couldn't detect a green color. Certainly could with my 'scope but I'm too lazy tonight. 🔭

Clouds can destroy some exoplanet signals, but they also impart signals in phase curves in reflected light.

Using five Kepler hot Jupiters, we find: 
– 2/5 have asymmetric cloud distributions
– clouds are compatible with enstatite
– atmospheric variability is a mirage

In orbit around the Milky Way are dozens of satellite galaxies, each containing millions or even billions of stars. In their wakes, tidal forces draw out streamers of gas and stars, some of which are ancient. Just imagine the view any planets in these streams might have in their night skies.

🔭 See the dot at the center of this image? That's the planet Neptune. Just to the left of it is Neptune's largest moon, Triton. They are currently about 2.7 billion miles from Earth. I photographed them just now with my 4.5-inch Unistellar eVscope.

Two rather poignant Roman tombstones now on display in the museum at Corbridge Roman town. On the left, the tombstone of Vellibia Ertola, who 'lived most happily for four years and six days'. On the right, that of five-year-old Ahteha, daughter of Nobilis.

The Valley of Fires (Carrizozo Volcanic Field) formed only 5,200 years ago when lava spilled out of Little Black Peak and Broken Back Crater (cinder cones/vents) covering the landscape with these black pāhoehoe lava flows. It feels like a combination of Hawaiian terrain with Southwestern flora. 🌿

Egyptians believed that azurite is a stone of mystery and psychic powers, used to pierce the veils of life's greatest mysteries. The priests used azurite-based paints to draw the eye of Horus on their foreheads to raise their consciousness to the level of communing with gods.

The gatehouse of St Bartholomew-the-Great, West Smithfield. Built 1595, above a C13th arch (formerly the west door to a then-bigger church) the Tudor front was bricked over but revealed after zeppelin bomb damage in 1916.

Olesko Castle is the only preserved fortress of the times of Kyivan Rus.
The castle was built on a hill in the middle of marshland.
In 1629, Polish King Jan III Sobieski was born here, and Mykhailo Khmil, the father of Bohdan Khmelnytsky, served here.

The world's oldest condom, made of pig intestines in 1640 CE, discovered in Lund, Sweden. The owners manual advised men to dip it in unboiled milk before use to prevent diseases when engaging with prostitutes. It’s washable and reusable. On display at Tirolean County Museum in Austria.

Santuario Madonna della Corona is a sanctuary in the Italian Alps near Lake Garda. It sits on a sheer cliffside, offering stunning views and housing a revered Black Madonna statue. Pilgrims visit for its tranquil atmosphere and scenic pilgrimage path, drawn by its rich history and natural beauty.

On February 17, 1600, philosopher, cosmologist, and unrepentant heretic Giordano Bruno was burned at the stake in the Campo de' Fiori in Rome. A notary sketched the execution as it happened. In 1889, a statue of Bruno by Ettore Ferrari was unveiled in the Campo, where it stands today #earlymodern

Evidence of Sasanian minting:
1) Seal of moneyer, with scales, hammer and dies.
2) Die for reverse of drachm of Khusro II, regnal year 11 (600/01), YZ (Yazd) mint.
3) Die for reverse of drachm of Khusro II, regnal year 15 (604/5), AW (Hormizd-Ardashir) mint.
Dies supposedly found in tomb near Mosul.

Picking up the Irish Archaeology Alphabet with County Louth 🏺 

Ireland's smallest county packs a big punch when it comes to heritage. It has the finest high cross in the country at Monasterboice, as well as one of the best castles at Castleroche. The Proleek Dolmen isn't bad either!

Aruch Cathedral/Surp Grigor
Originally a large domed basilica. According to inscriptions and Sebeos, the church and adjoining residence were built by Grigor Mamikonian and his wife Hełine between 661 and 682 CE. It is one of the few early Armenian churches to preserve fragments of frescoes.

Thamugadi in Algeria was designed to be home to 10,000 retired legionaries, but soon outgrew its original allocation.Because of the remarkable state of preservation of the ruins & the unique example of Roman urban planning with clearly delineated grids, it was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site

Detail of Jonas Moore's 1658 #map shows the ancient course of the Old Well Stream winding from R to L through the peat #fens N of Cambridge, with ribbon development along its silty banks; once the course of the R Ouse - & now cut by 2 parallel mid17thC canals (1/2)

The Iron Crown of Lombardy was made in the Middle Ages. Legend maintains its central band was beaten from a nail of the True Cross. It was used in the coronation of the first Holy Roman Emperor Charlemagne on Christmas day 800 and was worn again in 1805 by Napoleon when he was crowned King of Italy.

Another movie night, another #MuseumBum 👍🏛️🍑🎥. Have you noticed the figurine on the bedside table in Cruel Intentions? It's Breton Wrestlers (1929) by Malvina Hoffman. Her work can be found in The Met in NYC and The Art Institute of Chicago.

New coin: Ionia, Phocaea.
RPC III, 1942 rpc.ashmus.ox.ac.uk/coins/3/1942
I visited Phocaea (Foça, Turkey) in 2023. Phocaea (or Phokaia) was the northernmost of the Ionian cities. It was the mother city of several Greek colonies (Massilia, Emporion, Ampurias & Elea) and a member of the Delian League.

#OnThisDay in AD 138, Hadrian adopted Antoninus Pius as his son and successor and ordered him to adopt 7-year-old Lucius Verus and 16-year-old Marcus Aurelius to secure that succession for another generation. This political act is depicted on the Parthian Monument from Ephesus.

The Cascajal Block is an Olmec artifact from roughly 900 BCE that could represent the oldest known piece of Mesoaemerican writing.

Some glyphs are clearly pineapples, bugs, torches, etc. but otherwise this hasn't been deciphered yet

In 752 CE, a woman from Shimōsa province in Japan named Anahobe no Akome gave birth to quadruplets, 2 boys and 2 girls. Is this possibly the earliest reliable record of quadruplets? Are there other premodern examples? Wikipedia only goes back to 1750?!
