Great to see that the Swedish #lidar covering the Birka Viking period settlement on Bjorko island, Lake Malaren is now available. Birka was an important trading settlement and port between c. 750 and 975 AD. There are around 3,000 graves in three main cemeteries #archaeology

While Greek and Roman statuary is often seen as originating the 'naturalistic' tradition in Europe, both the idealized statues of the Greek archaic period and the more naturalistic Hellenistic style have precedents in Egyptian art

Always trying to convince myself that there are relict sedimentary fabrics in the ~1.7 byo Yavapai Schist of central Arizona (still a sedimentologist in my soul), but other than some convincingly preserved banded iron formation, I think it’s mainly metamorphic foliation, as seen in these outcrops. 🧪

Pictures of  the excavation of the Frome hoard, discovered by metal detectorist Dave Crisp in 2010. The hoard, made of 52,503 Roman bronze and silver coins gathered in a huge 🏺, weight 160 kg : it is the second largest Roman coin hoard yet found in Britain.

Coins are dated from 253 to 305 AD.
The Pantheon Dome in Rome, made of concrete, was built between 25 and 27 BCE, and still standing without props or any other means of support.

The Gundestrop Cauldron (200-300 AD) depicts a horned figure in a meditative pose, holding a torque in one hand and a serpent in the other. He is sometimes identified as the Gaulish god Cernunnos or as a Lord of the Animals.

Super interesting question from a student!! She's researching care and disability in the MSA/MP and there's classic examples from Neandertal sites like Shanidar, but we're failing to find comparable examples from contemporary Homo sapiens (MSA/early LSA or UP Europe). Any suggestions? 🏺

A Roman tile from London with a tale to tell... scratched into its surface are the words "for the last 13 days Austalis has been wandering off on his own every day" - a way for one annoyed worker to try & get a shirking colleague in trouble, 1800-1900 years ago?

Your annual reminder that although it is claimed, today probably wasn’t the day that Vesuvius erupted in AD 79 burying the landscape in volcanic debris and covering the Roman cities of Pompeii and Herculaneum as well as other settlements and rural villas in the area.

The block wheel cart has been used in Ireland since the Bronze Age. This pair of block wheels made from 3 pieces of alder, from a bog at Doogarymore, Co. Roscommon, were part of a 2 wheel cart dated to 
the Late Bronze Age/Early Iron Age. 🏺

This frieze from Fulton County, Georgia shows "Medicine's battle with Death." Note the rod with two snakes. Many mistake it for the Rod of Asclepius (the traditional symbol of healing). However, this is the Caduceus, symbol of commerce. A common error in the USA.

Koi Krylgan Kala is an archeological site from Karakalpakstan Republic. Built by Iranian people from the kingdom of Khwarezm (a sister civilization of a perhaps more famous Sogdia) in 4 cent BCE. Koi Krylgan Kala was both a fortress, a settlement, and a Zoroastrian temple.

Lovely 50cm resolution French #lidar detail of the French 1813-14 fortifications on the Bayonette ridge north of Vera in the Pyrenees. This new lidar shows a much more extensive system of trenches & redoubts than previously recognized. They were built to slow down Wellingtons advance into France

One of the rarer pottery types: a 15th/16th century CE ceramic grenade. It would have contained a black-powder load and was thrown by hand, flings or catapults after lighting a slow burning fuse. Virgilkapelle, Vienna. #archaeology🏺

ClassicsTober23 Phaedra, a victim of the gods, was cursed by Aphrodite to fall in love with her stepson Hippolytus. Why? Because he devoted himself to Artemis & not love 🤷🏻‍♂️. It ends in shame & death for Phaedra, & Hippolytus mangled under a chariot. No wonder she looks haunted

Under our scope, plastic samples should glow bright orange. This first sample, on limpets from a hydrothermal vent 1600m deep, does not appear to contain microplastics. 

This is the shells-attached sample. The other one is basically an orange field of nothing.

These wall paintings are from Coombes church, W Sussex. They were painted by monks from the nearby wealthy Lewes Priory in the C12th. They are regarded as masterpieces of ecclesiastic art from this period. Given the available colour palette, they are colloquially known as ‘egg & bacon’ murals
📷me

Les Catacombes de Paris ossurary contains remains of more than 6 million people. Tunnels are originally from mining Lutetian limestone (Paris stone) orig formed in Eocene epoch's Lutetian age (47.8 - 41.2 Ma). Stone quarried here used to build Louvre, Les Invalides, & Arc de Triomphe.

Opening up the Whitehorse Hill cist on Dartmoor..

God, how I love this stuff! 1750-1600 BC. The burial of the cremated remains (possibly a young female) can be pinpointed to Aug-Sept because purple moor grasses were laid on the base stone. And a bear pelt laid on top

#Archaeology

St Peter's in Melverley has been a site of Christian worship for 1000 years. In 1401, the original Church was burnt down by Owain Glyndwr's armies. By 1406, it was rebuilt using local oak. The vastness of history rises from the wood. It's such a special place 💚

This mosque in Aleppo started as a Hittite temple some 3100 years ago. Still has Hittite inscriptions carved in its stones, and is one of the oldest religious building in use!

(Not quite continuous: it fell in disrepair for a century or two. But what are a few hundred years for a Hittite temple!)

This amazing photo of the Lion Gate at Mycenae has been thought to be of Schliemann (top right) for decades. It’s now thought to be an 1890 photo of aristocratic German tourists with the then German ambassador to Greece. Fascinating full story here: dainst.blog/people-at-the-…
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Advent Calendar of Mortality History - Door 24 💀🎄: ‘Behold the ende’ reads the message which encircles the skull on this gold mourning ring. The skull warns of what we will all become. The bezel features another  inscription - ‘rather death than fals fayth’. Late 16th cent. (V&A) 🗃️ #earlymodern

The famous monumental statue of the Mexica (Aztec) goddess Coatilcue was buried by the Spanish invaders near the site of the destroyed Templo Mayor. Rediscovered in 1790, Antonio León y Gama made these illustrations of it for his 1792 book on the object, before it was reburied.

♟️ Look at this beautiful 12th century chess set from Iran. The shah (king) is represented as a throne; the vizier (the equivalent of the queen) is a smaller throne

❓Can you guess which one is the elephant (bishop)?

More in Through the Mirror of Chess: ideasroadshow.com/chess/

🏺🗃️🏛️ #EduSKy #history

A 16th century German 'oath skull' (a human skull on which defendants swore their oath in Vehmic courts) engraved with the 'magical' Roman 'Sator square', mysterious palindromic word-squares found across the Roman world, comprising the words SATOR, AREPO, TENET, OPERA, ROTAS

#EpigraphyTuesday; an ex voto to Asclepius and Hygieia by Publius Flaccinius Felix, known from another inscription as a decurion of the collegium fabrum at Sarmizegetusa. Late 2nd/early 3rd century CE. Muzeul de Arheologie Sarmizegetusa.

🏺 AncientBluesky #RomanArchaeology #Romania
The “nude goddess” of Babylon (1stC BCE-1stC CE) figurine has ruby eyes. Where did the likely come from? Myanmar. Evidence for long distance trade in antiquity is all around us.

'King Tutankhamun's "golden mask" in Harry Burton's original photo, taken in 1925. The garland of cornflowers and olive leaves were on the coffin during his funeral. Burton's glass plates are kept at the Griffin Institute, Oxford.'

Stonehenge origin mystery solved as experts find evidence megalith 'first stood in Wales'

Extensive research found a dismantled stone circle in West Wales that was moved to the current site of Stonehenge thousands of years ago.

The forum and Byzantine fort in Madauros, Algeria. It became a Roman colony for veterans in the 1st century. It was the birthplace in 124AD of the Numidian Apuleius who wrote the only Latin Roman novel, "The Golden Ass", to survive intact. St Augustine of Hippo also studied there.

Ancientbluesky 🏺

This torc was found at Snettisham in 1965 & has wire ring terminals overcast with molten metal to create a thicker terminal ring - special because sprue (metal filling channel which molten metal was poured into mould through) hasn't been cut off, & so torc is unfinished...or is it?

#Archaeology

This is the Ashmolean Parchment, one of the oldest known love spells. Written in Coptic around 600 CE likely from Egypt, it binds Phello, son of Maure, to seek out and satisfy the desires of the caster, also listed in the text. Who cast it? Apapolo, son of Nooe.

Queer Pining: As old as time itself

Museum30, day 13, storage. These large storage dolia are part of the Roman garum and salted fish factory of ancinet Barcino, preserved under the streets of Plaça del Rei, Gothic Barcelona. Fantastic interpretation and tourist presentation of the archaeology site by the Museu D'Historia 🏺📜🏛

Hunter-gatherer societies on the Iberian Peninsula were making sophisticated baskets with decorative geometric patterns 9,500 years ago, more than 2,000 years earlier than previously thought, researchers in Spain have reported.

A Roman votive altar dedicated by Lucius Valerius Monteius to Cautes, a companion of Mithras, later reused in the city wall of medieval Barcelona; well, once their gods aren't important anymore, altars are just convenient blocks of worked stone...

Things I absolutely love about the cover of The Borgia Dove: the keys and sun in the rondels over Giulia's head are the keys and sun from the ceiling of the Borgia Apartments in the Vatican! @user has beautifully echoed Pinturicchio's work and it's gorgeous!

Incidentally, correct me if I'm wrong, but as far as I know, the "12th-century" "castle" that Trump is extremely proud of owning in Aberdeen is actually a *19th-century* *house*, incorporating portions of an 18th-century house, built on a 14th-century foundation.

#ClassicsTober23 day 27 Pygmalion is another impossibility in #Pompeii. But he carved his statue out of ivory, so here's a small ivory statue found in the house at I.8.5 of the Hindu goddess Lakshmi (MANN inv. 149425). Trade route keepsake or Indian resident in Pompeii? 🏺🏛️

Remarkably well-preserved 4th-century Byzantine mosaic from ruins of a villa in Mariamin, Syria, depicts women musicians playing organ, double flute, lyre, forked cymbals, and an eight-bowl xylophone-ish instrument. Two young boys pose as cherubs at far left

A little Roman child's shoe, delicately made from leather - this would have been a fairly expensive piece of footwear. It was found in the fort of Vindolanda (Britain), & may have once been owned by one of the fort commander's children 🏺AncientBlueSky

Both central pillars of Building B at Göbekli Tepe (10th/9th millennium BCE) have reliefs of jumping foxes. Fox is well represented in the bone assemblage of the site. Other pillars show loincloths made from fox pelt - a hint at their importance to Neolithic people. #Archaeology 🏺

The beautiful shrine of St Amphibalus at St Albans. If you look carefully, you can see a tiny man in a mask among the heads, added when the stonework was restored in 2020. Such a wonderful reflection of the ongoing life of the cathedral.

Wanapum flow of Columbia River Basalt entered a lake here, just south of I-84, a bit east of The Dalles on Rte 97. The transition from pillow basalt to columnar jointing ~indicates the lakes surface.⚒️ GeoHols for scale in left photo.

In March 1872 the wooden foundations of Newcastle's Roman bridge were excavated.

John Collingwood Bruce, father of Hadrian's Wall study, was there. He salvaged many timbers & turned one into the boards of his own copy of "The Roman Wall"! 🏺

This 1,700-year-old blue glass bowl, a little more than 8 inches in diameter, was found buried next to a woman at the center of a cemetery at the Roman settlement of Emona in present-day Ljubljana in Slovenia.  Its Greek inscription reads: "Drink to live forever, for many years!"

In 1938, 17 brass and copper heads and the upper half of a brass figure (dated to the 14th-15th century CE) were found by accident during house building works at Ife in Nigeria. The realism and sophisticated craftsmanship of the objects challenged Western conceptions of African art.

In Roman Londinium, as now, the Thames was used for dumping building rubble & y’day there was a lot on the foreshore. These are Roman roof tiles. The lower piece is the tegula, a plain flat tile with a raised edge. This was laid flat on a roof & covered with a curved tile (above) called an imbrex.

Longmen Grottoes in Henan Province, China. Over 2,300 caves filled with over 100,000 statues of all sizes. 4th-10th-centuries CE

This is a UNESCO-recognized World Heritage site, representing the pinnacle of Chinese stone carving art.

Internationally recognisable, the Hamsa (meaning '5') has its origins in ancient Mesopotamia. Often used as a protective amulet or as an apotropaic mark it's been popularised by a wide range of ethnic & religious groups. This one came from Tripoli in Libya, N Africa. #FolkVent 4

#RuinoftheDay - The Arch of Augustus at Fano (Italy), a city gate in the form of a triumphal arch with three vaults which served as the entrance to the city of Colonia Julia Fanestris by the Via Flaminia. The inscription on the frieze dates the arch to AD 9.

This relief, part of the Sebasteion at Aphrodisias is thought to depict a personification of the Roman emperor crowned by a personification of the Roman people (or the Senate or both!). The corona civica was awarded for saving the life of a citizen.

Inky paw print on a sherd from Thebes, Egypt, recording  Maieuris paid their taxes at around 94 CE. I've flipped the image so the paw print is facing up & the text is inverted. My guess is this was a cat, it's a cat kinda thing to do no? But I'm not an expert. 6.5x6.4cm
#MuseumWork #RomanEgypt #Cats

As I'm dipping into Norwegian legal history today, I'm reminded of how runestones could function as legal monuments. One example of this is the Nørstebø stone from the late eleventh century, a border marker which also carries a written confirmation of the agreement by which the land was divided.

Canyon DeChelly National Monument, Arizona USA. Chinle Wash is the stream in the canyon. Chinle is a Navajo (Diné) word meaning “it flows out”. The cliffs are Permian DeChelly Sandstone, deposited in a sand sea in western Pangaea, with a caprock of Shinarump Member of the Triassic Chinle Formation.

At first glance you might think that this alabaster tomb might be from Westminster Abbey but this remarkable monument to Alice Chaucer is at St. Mary, Ewelme in Oxfordshire. She was the grandaughter of Geoffrey Chaucer. Note the stone cadaver behind the tracery at the bottom.

Around 2400 BCE, the ruler of the Sumerian city of Lagash, Enmetena, had the details of the battle against the neighbouring city, Umma, recorded a stone ("Enmetena cone"). The claim for war reparations is the earliest known record of compound interest. 
arxiv.org/ftp/arxiv/pa...

It’s #ChariotTuesday: here’s a mid-C8thBCE vase depicting a war chariot & warriors with distorted figure of 8 shields. Given the common theory that chariots were not used in war in this period & given the Bronze Age shields, query whether this represents a myth
🏛️BM 📷me 🏺 AncientBlueSky

A bit of spectacular Yorkshire vernacular at Clapham cum Newby in the form of the old Manor House dated to around 1640. The double storied porch has a lovely decorative lintel over the door with spiral stops to the hoodmould - a style which was an influence on the Arts and Crafts movement.

Look closely at the spare decoration on this 6th c. BCE black-figure 'lip cup'. Yes, that's Hercules battling the Nemean lion. But what about the lettering below the figures? Want to try to decipher it? You can't. It's gibberish. A comment on drunkenness? 😆🍷 #GettyVilla  1/

Direct archaeological evidence for the presence of cereals in Early Neolithic settlements sometimes comes in interesting forms. Imprints of an einkorn and an emmer wheat grain in sherds of the Linear Pottery Culture (5500-4800 BCE).  Historisches Museum Regensburg. #Archaeology 🏺

I felt rather unmotivated to post geology recently, but bsky is bringing my motivation back :)

This is the east coast of the Calamita on Elba, Italy. The sea breeze might make it appear chilly, but these rocks are actually migmatites intruded by leucogranites - estimated T > 700 °C *yikes*

Wild that the statue of the tetrarchs that Venice took from Constantinople in 1204 still has a 100 years before it’s been in its new home as long as its old. Probably a product of the way I learned European history (Romans, static noises, high Middle Ages - cc @user) but still!

Refits are the coolest. 

This is a large portion of a salt glazed stoneware crock made by the company TO Goodwin, located in the Hartford, CT area - likely produced in the mid 1800s. Found over three field seasons. There are about 20 other fragments. #Archaeology 🧪🏺

Thonis-Heraklion was founded in C8thBCE at the Nile’s mouth. Before Alexandria’s creation, it was the major port of the delta. Succeeding earthquakes & floods saw the city slip under the waves in C2ndBCE eventually disappearing c.900CE. It has been excavated since 1933
📷Franck Goddio🏺 AncientBlueSky

This late Cisalpine Celtic gravestone from the necropolis of Cerrione near Biella in the Piedmont, dating to the first half of the 1st century BC, bears the inscription iouiku | uipios 'Iouiku son of Uip(i)os', written in two dextroverse lines running downward on the stela. 1/8

Was in Nantwich today, and uncle pointed out this gargoyle of a woman with her hand in a jar being carried off by the devil. Apparently the stonemasons caught their landlady stealing from them, and added this to the church in her honour. Still there nearly 700 years later. That’ll teach her.

Micklegate Bar, York. Built in the C14th and incorporating a C12th gate. The ceremonial entrance for Kings and Queens into York - made in parts from Roman stone. Held the heads of ‘traitors’ until the C18th.

'Damnatio Memoriae', Egyptian-style! When the 'heretical' pharaoh Akhenaten died, all memory of his reign was meant to be erased. Here, the royal cartouches - Akhenaten's names - have been defaced. At Amarna, over a ton of these statues were found smashed to bits. 

1352-1336 BCE. #BrooklynMuseum 🏺

The 11c St Sophia Cathedral in Kjiv inspired by Ag Sophia in Constantinople; much of the work was done by craftsmen from that city. Examples of the striking mosaics in the #Byzantine style: The Annunciation (the 2 figures are on adjacent columns); Church Fathers; the Eucharist; the High Priest Aaron

Agrigento today is a beautiful place - a hill town with gelateria, baroque churches & its famous temples, but it was also home to the tyrant Phalaris who roasted victims alive in a bronze bull. It’s said the contraption was made to amplify the screams of his victims
📷me 🏺 AncientBlueSky

Ancient British coins of the Catevellauni. They show British & Roman influence. Horses feature a lot as status objects, as do coins marked VER (for Verlamio, the main oppidum). Others show more overt Roman influence with AUGUSTUS inscriptions & classical heads
🏛️Verulanium Museum
📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

#MosaicMonday - Mosaic depicting two lions devouring a boar. Found in the ancient city of Thysdrus (modern-day El Jem). Dated to the 2nd century AD. 
El Jem Museum, Tunisia.

"The Tooth Worm as Hell's Demon,” 18th century. Carved from ivory, the two halves open up to reveal a scene about the infernal torments of a tooth worms which people believed caused cavities in the past. Complete with mini skulls, hellfire, and naked people wielding clubs.

In the Welsh legend of Peredur (the Welsh Sir Percival), Peredur battles a man in rusty armour who rises from under a stone slab lying outdoors. As the bodies of ancient British pagans often rested in megalithic tombs, this implies the man was some ancient undead.
#WyrdWednesday

Why is Jesus depicted as having long hair & beard?

In the oldest depictions, early 3rd century Rome, he's drawn as a typically middle-class Roman: clean-shaven with short, neatly cut hair, wearing a 'toga virilis', the national costume of Roman citizens.

This map was published in 1852, resurveyed in 1906-1909 & again 1930-1952. It was reprinted with amendments in 1974.

The map shows Middle & Upper Coal Measures in grey, with various sandstones in orange & mustard. The blue is Lower Magnesian Limestone (now the Cadeby Formation).

Chepstow Castle, Monmouthshire is the oldest surviving post-Roman stone fortification in Britain. Located above cliffs on the River Wye, construction began in 1067 under the instruction of the Norman Lord William FitzOsbern. 

It was the southernmost of a chain of castles built in the Welsh Marches.

Replica Bronze Age alderwood anthropomorphic figures at UCD Centre for Experimental Archaeology & Material Culture, an experiment tracing their deterioration over time, and comparing with originals found in peat bogs. Shows were not exposed to weather for more than months, before deposition in bogs.

We need to start addressing the alarming lack of Orkney archaeology on this platform, so here's the scant but extraordinary remains of the early 12th C kirk of St. Nicholas in Orphir. Built by Earl Håkon to atone for murdering Magnus, on his return from pilgrimage in the Holy Land.  🏺🗃️Ancientbluesky

Holy crap, they found Silplhium in Turkey.

It was thought to be extinct since the Roman Empire and was used as food, medicine and contraception. 👀

I miss Rome. 🥺 That was the first time I’d seen the Sistine Chapel ceiling and the Last Judgement since they cleaned it— and I legit cried. 

It was also the first time I’d been to the crypt of St. Peter’s since JP II was buried. While I have BIG PROBLEMS with JP II, his tomb is lovely.

The cathedral of St Mary, Limerick, is also home to the only complete set of misericords to survive from medieval Ireland. These beautifully carved hinged oak seats, dating from the 15th century, were designed to give support to clergy who had to stand during long services.

I heard our garden described yesterday as being 'like Ninfa but without the ruins' and I do not think I have ever been as flattered. 
Ninfa is an Italian garden built in the ruins of a medieval village and often touted as the world's most romantic garden.

Ferruccio Mengaroni’s colossal sculpture of Medusa, modelled on his own face. He was convinced that the piece would win him huge fame at the Monza Biennale of 1925. During installation, it fell, crushing him to death. Medusa survived intact. #WyrdWednesday

I found this German helmet on the Somme in the early 1980s when I was 17. It was lying at the bottom of a mine crater at Carnoy, in the former no man’s land, where a farmer must have flung it after his plough dragged it up. The craters are long-since filled in.

A Roman metal luggage-tag which once belonged to Julius Candidus, a soldier in the Legio XX - his name & unit have been punched into the metal. It would have been attached to his belongings in camp or on the baggage-train to try & ensure that he held on to them - for his sake, I hope it worked!

C19th & C20th beadwork bags displayed on gold silk. Vast quantities of beads were imported into England during the Medieval period initially to embellish embroidery. The fashion reached its highpoint in C17th, a downturn in C18th, & rose in popularity in C19th. #colourchallenge #gold #chertseymuseum

Dendera Temple, Egypt. Relief depicts Cleopatra & her son with Julius Caesar Caesarion.
After Cleopatra’s suicide, Caesarion fled but Octavian called for his return. Arius Didymus reportedly said, ‘Too many Caesars are not good.’ Octavian had Caesarion (aged 17) killed in 30 BC.

🏺Ancientbluesky

Rivers and lakes have always had a special place in beliefs and ritual. During the Bronze Age of southern Germany, in particular swords were frequently deposited  in rivers. Swords found in the Danube and its tributaries, Historisches Museum Regensburg. #archaeology 🏺

Fra Angelico, The mocking of Christ with the Virgin and St Dominic. Fresco (1438). Cell 7 . San Marco. Christ holds the Globe and Cane, symbols of the power offered him in contempt by his tormentors. Hands slapping him and faces spitting at him.

Beautiful pottery, exquisite jewellery and stunning frescoes, but nothing thrilled me quite as much as discovering that the ancient Minoans made votive offerings of clay rhinoceros beetles at the open-air sanctuary of Piskokephalo on Crete.

1650-1500 BC, Heraklion Archaeological Museum

Today’s date, 17th October, was recorded in a charcoal inscription in the House of the Garden in Pompeii that reads ‘XVI K Nov’ – 16 days before the Kalends of November. 
It is unclear what is written below but it may be: ‘IN OLEARIA/PROMA SUMSERUNT’ – they took it in the olive oil storeroom

#ClassicsTober23 16: Circe

A powerhouse woman from myth with exceptional skills in pharmaka, Circe looms large in Homer’s Odyssey. This tale sets in motion a reception of Circe that endures even now. This painting by Waterhouse is superb: her power so clear.

🏺ClassicsBluesky

This bronze head has traditionally been associated with M Agrippa, but this is now thought questionable. Originally, the sockets would have had eyeballs (perhaps like the Meroe Augustus), but their absences lends a compelling sense sinister foreboding to the piece. 

🏛️📷NY Met 🏺 AncientBlueSky

The 'Venus of Willendorf' was carved from limestone & tinted with red ochre by an unknown artist around 25,000 years ago.

Found at a Paleolithic dig site in Austria in 1908, it resembles other "Venus" figures from same period: it emphasizes aspects of female fertility.

But why?

A 4,500-year-old Egyptian dress was painstakingly reassembled from approximately 7,000 beads found in an undisturbed tomb in Giza, Egypt. The dress is thought to have belonged to a female contemporary of King Khufu (2589-2566 BC).

A 16th century depiction of Sir John Winter 'the Britched Woodwose' or 'Tamed Wodewose' in the parish church of Odd Eaton. Although legend fabulously and falsely claims he was knighted by King Henry VIII, modern scholars believe he may have been a genuine feral child or wild man.

Chartres cathedral, constructed within a generation after a fire in 1194, is rightly famous for its stained glass windows. But after visiting today I think the exterior carvings deserve equal adulation - truly incredible sources for material culture around 1200. #medievalsky

This magnificent Gallic flagon dates to c450 BCE. It’s exquisitely cast & regarded as a masterpiece of early Celtic art. If you look closely at the spout, there’s even a little joke at work as there’s a bronze duck who will ride the flow of beer or wine as it’s poured out

🏛️BM 📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

This is the oldest preserved human brain 🧠 at 2600 yrs old, found in Heslington, Yorkshire, UK in 2008.

The original owner was hanged, decapitated & the head immediately buried in mud.  Presumed ritual murder/sacrifice from the British Iron Age.

It's shrunk to 20% its original volume.

Ancient DNA is amazing, Part 4,982. DNA of 400-yr-old cow bones in Hispaniola and Mexico hints that the first cowboys in the Americas were enslaved Africans. Analysis backs up historians' belief that classic cowboy stuff like herding from horseback and using lassos were created by Africans, too.

This floor is entirely flat, a remarkable 17th-century optical illusion of Florence Cathedral's marble tiles. When seen from above, the intricate geometry creates the illusion of a giant abyss that people might stumble into. The red/white/black and green marble floor took 160 years to complete.

What you could buy on the streets of Rome in 1582: Need a turtle? Watermelon? Wax flowers? Plates? A devotional image? A tooth pulled? [Ambrogio Brambilla, Ritrato de quelli che vano vendendo et lavorando per Roma from Speculum Romanae Magnificentiae (British Museum)]

Let's talk about the 'Ain Ghazal' statues, ~9000 years old, found in Jordan, and just deeply, deeply disturbing to look at.

Some mysteries here we don't have answers to:
1. Why do some have 2 heads?
2. Why were they buried?
3. How could a loving god permit them to be?

A torc from the Erstfeld Treasure, showing animals that merge into each other to form fanciful creatures. The treasure was deposited in a rock crevice in a valley leading to the Gotthard Pass, probably as votive offerings of a group of travellers It consists of 4 golden torcs and 3 golden arm rings.

Today's historical anthro art picks up from yesterday's post: Dolon, the Trojan hero who disguises himself as a wolf to spy on the enemy Greek camp, is caught and interrogated by the Greeks Odysseus and Diomedes. Early 5th century BCE pottery fragment now in the Getty Museum in LA (inv. 90.AE.35.40)

The trireme, a marvel of military ship building. Here are 2 ancient reliefs, the 1st from Rhodes carved into a cliff face (📷Saffron Blaze), the 2nd from Athens (📷Acropolis 🏛️). The other 2 pics are the ‘Olympias’, a modern reconstruction which an inexperienced crew got to 17kph! 
🏺 AncientBlueSky

A gable within a gable in Frankwell, Shrewsbury. Possibly C15th. The principals of the cruck frame are split from a single tree. The beam that ties them together is arch braced - and check out the trenches (cut-outs) where the purlins once fitted.

Sitting Bull’s war bonnet and war shirt from c.1875, in the Royal Ontario Museum. Such a privilege to see them, but also unsettling to see these objects of sacred power fossilised in a museum case. (Sitting Bull apparently gifted the war bonnet and sold the shirt, but even so.) 🗃️

Managed to get to the only hillfort I haven't visited before in our local area at Crowthers Coppice just north of Welshpool. Impressive earthwork defences in the woodland along the footpath and a lidar view for comparison. The site of a huge hoard of late Bronze Age metalwork found in 1862.

Did you guess it? I am a 16th-century brass oyster gauge crafted in the form of an oyster with a purpose as unique as my design. So, next time you enjoy an oyster, think of the intricate history and efforts that have gone into preserving these sea treasures for centuries!

Fascinating news: researchers who last year announced evidence that footprints in White Sands, N.M., were 19-23k yrs old return w/ two new lines of evidence to back claim that humans were in the Americas at the height of the last ice age, thousands of years before generally accepted arrival time.

On my recent trip to Scotland I made a special visit to the Meffan Museum in Forfar to see this lovely little bronze bust of Minerva. She was discovered at the site of a Roman fort at Cardean and dates from the early 80s AD. I particularly love the very grumpy looking Gorgon on her armour!

The colossal bronze statue of Hercules that stands in the Vatican Museums. The 13-foot statue was unearthed in Rome in 1864, under a slab inscribed 'FCS' - Fulgor Conditum Summanium, indicating that the statue had been ritually buried after being struck by lightning.

#FrescoFriday - The eponymous panel from Casa dell'Amore Punito, Pompeii (VII.2.23): discovered 5 August 1844. Cupid is brought to Venus, seemingly to be chastised for firing his darts willy-nilly. Naughty boy! 🏺ClassicsBluesky

Image: National Archaeological Museum, Naples (9257)

Fort Rock, a tuff ring about a mile in diameter, north of the town of the same name in central Oregon. Wave action in the pluvial lake here in the Pleistocene eroded it into the amphitheater shape we see today.⚒️

Saw this in Berlin in an excellent exhibition on miniatures and polychromy. It is a miniature shrine with statues of Hermes and two nymphs on a base from Tanagra in Boiotia (c.400-350 BC)! 

The roof is missing, but it would have finished off this Polly(theism) Pocket set.

📸 One of the most autumny pictures I've taken - Bran Castle in Transylvania, built in the late 14th century and once a Saxon frontier fortress to counter the expansionist Ottomans. In recent times, it's become regarded as 'Dracula's Castle'. It's not. Vampires don't exist 🍂🍁.

Cthonic Demeter of Arcadia was represented as being snake-haired, which creates a faint-but visible line between early agricultural feminine deities and the destruction of matriarchal cults by the Hellenes as represented as metaphor in the story of Medusa

A 'pilgrim mark' at St John the Baptist Hornton, Oxon. Quite how they know its a such a mark is not explained. I suspect it's the old crusader story - invented 200 years ago and still doing the rounds. Myth made fact through repeated statements.

Fragments of Georgian-era glass. Prunts, finials, bottle stoppers & the aqua blue neck of an apothecary bottle. Full of imperfections, bubbles, wonkiness & all manner of irregularities as the glass emerges from the furnace. The Georgian period became famous for lead glass for its drinking vessels.

This is a life-sized Etruscan bronze replica of a sheep’s liver. It was used by haruspices who specialised in divination by examining the entrails, particularly the livers, of animals. The markings on the liver would help interpret their finds. 
🕰️C2ndBCE
🏛️📷Palazzo Farnese, Piacenza
🏺 AncientBlueSky

#MemorialMonday: Monument in Cernobbio, Italy, commemorating the main stages of the unification of Italy: the three Wars of Independence, the Taking of Rome and the First World War. The monument was inaugurated in 1923 and was created by the Milanese sculptor Angelo Galli.

Late 17th C token found by me on the Thames, these give so much information. Minted in lieu of money when there was a shortage of coins in circulation, they could be exchanged for goods. This one shows the name Thomas Skelton, a Mealman (someone who deals in grain) based on Shear Lane #mudlark

TIL that the Egyptian city of Faiyum was called by another name by Greeks during the Pharaonic Era: "Crocodile City," because of it being the center of Sobek (crocodile god) worship. The Romans called it "Crocodilopolis."

that one time an East African girl was accused of appropriating South Asian culture for wearing a nose-chain style piercing; a type of jewelry also traditionally worn in East Africa, actually for longer than in South Asia, and the likely origin being pre-Islamic Arabia

#RuinoftheDay -The Mithraeum House in Augusta Emerita (Mérida, Spain), built around three collonaded courts with an interior garden and a central pool. The quality of the floor mosaics is very high, the most fascinating one being the cosmological mosaic that once covered the floor of the triclinium.

The faces of Palmyra. These are tomb portraits of Palmyran elites dating from c50-270CE. The sculptural style, as well as that of clothes and hair, show a natural mix of Roman & Syrian influences. But look at those eyes - they’re looking at you from depths of the past
🏛️BM
📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

This 6th century mosaic in Sant’Apollinare Nuovo, Ravenna shows the first ever depiction of Father Christmas (or Pater Navitivitas as the late Romans would have called him). The triple depiction is intended to show the “Three Ages of Santa” - youth, adult, old man as described by Tertullian.

This depiction of praetorians comes from the Arch of Claudius celebrating his invasion of Britain. The arch has not survived, but this panel suggests the artistry used to enhance the sense of military glory…

🏛 The Louvre
📸 Christophe Jacquand
#ReliefWednesday
🏺ClassicsBluesky

It’s Friday so it’s time for frescoes! This bowl of figs is from the Villa Poppaea in what was once Oplontis, a small settlement destroyed in the Vesuvius eruption. It’s a lovely still life; the plump succulent figs look good enough to pluck off the wall

🕰️C1st BCE
📷 open source
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The Cult of Mithras, a Roman mystery religion, was popular among the Roman army from the 1st-4th century CE and left traces all over the empire. Relief with a very expressive Mithras and the bull, KHM Vienna. #Archaeology 🏺

One of my death historian favourites from Westminster- the #c18th Nightingale Monument by Roubiliac. Joseph Nightingale tries in vain to protect his wife as death emerges from the locked vault. Death is partially shrouded and stares up at the pair clutching a hefty dart.

A series of camels carved into desert rock faces in north-western Saudi Arabia are actually prehistoric, dating from 7,000-8,000 years ago - before either the Pyramids of Giza or Stonehenge were built.

1900 year old mourning ring! The hologram is thought to depict Titus Carvilius Gemello, who died aged 18. it was found in his mother's tomb - a tender reminder of her love for her son. Found in the Grottaferrata necropolis near Rome in the year 2000.

This is the Cyrus cylinder. It documents the capture of Babylon by Cyrus & highlights his enlightened treatment of Babylonians (cf Assyrian conduct). It’s been called the first human rights charter but that’s daft. It does portray a different, more tolerant way of ruling tho

🏛️BM📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

I love this book. Though written in the 70s, its scholarship holds up. Horse collars, invented in China, reached Europe by the 900s and revolutionized agriculture.

The very first picture *anywhere* of a horse pulling a plough comes from the 11th C Bayeux Tapestry!

#medievalsky

Why did I come to Sicily? In a metaphor: the Duomo of Siracusa. The time-worn columns of a 5th Century Doric temple to Athena still stand in what is both allegedly the second Christian church after Antioch and a former mosque, which is now a wood-roofed basilica with a rococo altar and facade.

The magnificent Tewkesbury Abbey, founded in 1092.
Within its walls you can find the tombs of Despensers, Nevilles, Beauchamps, and even the Duke of Clarence who was reported to have been drowned in a butt of Malmsey wine.
The Wars of the Roses loom large here 🗃️

This is statue of Saint Bartholomew, an early Christian martyr who was allegedly skinned alive. Notice: that's not a robe that he’s holding. It's his dissected skin. This stunning statute is by the Italian sculptor Marco d’Agrate, c.1562.

Here is the headstone of Marcus Cocceius Nonnus, pictured as a charioteer with a whip in one hand and a victory palm in the other. 

He was 6. 

A little boy who liked the races. No different from little boys today with their favourite football teams 

🏛️BM
📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The Broken Menhir, Jersey. A Late Neolithic sentinel placed amid coastal woodland 5000 years ago. Fallen and inundated by dunes in antiquity.  Rediscovered and raised out of the sand last century. #StandingStoneSunday 🏺

This is an incredible relic from Ancient Rome. The bronze doors of the Lateran Cathedral (left) were originally the doors of the Senate house (right). They date to the reign of Domitian (81-96CE) & were moved to their current location in 1660 by Pope Alexander VII

📷mine 
🏺 ClassicsBlueSky

#FraFriday Detail of the Cortona Altarpiece Annunciation.  I love how the words exchanged go left to right and then right to left (not unique to Fra Angelico but so subtly done).  I love the use of architectural elements separating human/divine space in this and many other traditional Annunciations

Reminder to new users here:

The difference between Mycenaean and Minoan art is essentially a matter of fluidity and naturalism. Minoan Art has loose, organic lines and forms; Mycenaean art is more symmetrical and abstract. Nothing illustrates the difference better than comparing their octopus jars.

The Phrygian cap was associated in Rome with easterners but later became the headgear of freed slaves (but more pointy). Drawing on this, in the C18th it became associated with Liberty. Later, Peyo gave the Smurfs the same cap to represent their communitarian & free lifestyle. 
🏺AncientBlueSky

In its original context, the Prima Porta Augustus was painted. Archaeologists can often determine the base colours from small paint deposits left behind which leaves open suggestions for the top layers of colour. Here ‘Rome in 3D’ offer an example of a potential colour scheme.

🏺AncientBluesky
These are scoria bricks in Middlesbrough. They are made from the slag... the remnants of the iron industry. They are pretty much indestructible.

This reticulated beaker has an inscription in glass letters: FAVENTIBVS. It’s an expression of good luck (perhaps short for "Faventibus ventis" – with favourable winds). The ‘cage’ of the beaker is also glass. These items are the pinnacle of Roman glass craft

🏛️📷Kunsthistoriches 
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The tuff cliffs at Bandelier National Monument were deposited by volcanic eruptions over a million years ago then eroded by water and some of these layers eroded pockets like Swiss cheese. These ‘cavates’ were then used by the Ancestral Pueblo peoples as dwellings after their further excavations.🌿

Was good to be reminded (Bettany Hughes tv doc) of the solid silver Mummy case with falcon head, from 885 BC of Shoshenq II. 
Silver was more precious than gold.
It was discovered in an intact tomb. If only we could see the many similar items taken from other tombs, mostly in antiquity 😭#Egyptology

Changing the bedsheets one-handed is just showing off, Jason! 👍🏛️🍑 #MuseumBums

A marble relief from 200AD Luni, showing Jason nicking the golden fleece while Medea distracts the dragon. This is from National Roman Museum of Palazzo Altemps.

Thanks @user for sharing 😁

This is one of my favorite coins, a post-reform Umayyad dirham of AH 80 (699/700 CE) minted in Marw. I like the sinuous early post-reform style of its Arabic script, and the fact that the mint name is still written in Middle Persian (MLW) on the obverse below لا اله الا الله وحده لا شرك له.

A self-reminder that Corleck Head exists: a three-faced stone idol from Corleck Hill, County Cavan, Ireland. About 2000 years old; unearthed some time in 19 century, but kept as a farm gate decoration for decades (which de-contextualized the artifact). Related to the so-called "Celtic head cult"

One of the outrageously important runic inscriptions on the Viking-style Galloway Hoard, deposited c.900 in what is now SW Scotland. The kicker is that the runes are ANGLIAN, not ON, likely made by someone from A-S Northumbria.

Images created from photo I took at Treasure Trove in Scotland viewing.
Neanderthals were morning people, a new study suggests. And some humans today who like getting up early might credit genes they inherited from their Neanderthal ancestors.

#FindsFriday
What's he got in his pocket today? 
It's a 3500 year old Middle Bronze Age Arrowhead manufactured from a Neolithic blade already then 2500 years old.

In the colossal cathedral sized water tank beneath the Hagia Sophia in Istanbul, several “heads of Medusa” have been used as pillar bases for the past 1.500 years. Believed to be taken from some other Roman ruin. the heads are upside down or sideways to inhibit the power of the Gorgon Medusa's gaze

An 8th century spearhead turned into one of the most important regalia and relics of the Holy Roman Empire: the Holy Lance. Said to be the lance with which Longinus pierced the body of Christ, it lead the way into numerous battles as a token of victory. Imperial Treasury, Vienna.🏺

This stunning gold hairnet (3rd c. BCE) from Tarentum features Medusa at the crown. It reminds me so much of what the so-called 'Sappho' of #Pompeii has in her hair, that now I really wish we had a view of the top. #ClassicsTober23 day 2. 🏺

Socks with sandals? Quite fashionable in Ancient Egypt!
This sock (c. 4th-5th century CE) with a divided toe was designed to be worn with thonged sandals. It was found in an Egyptian burial site, I'm happy that someone went to the hereafter with warm tootsies. 
📷 National Museums of Scotland.

News coming in that there are newly discovered electoral graffiti from Pompeii in house IX.10.1 in support of Aulus Rustius Verus, a candidate for aedile, in a lararium. The house also had a bakery. Aediles + bakers= a very important business relationship! pompeiisites.org/en/comunicat... #epigraphy

The Golden Hat of Berlin is one of 4 found in Europe, all from the Bronze Age, and is also the best preserved hat among them.  These hats are actually astronomical calendars with which the seasons were calculated for the benefit of agricultural production. 🏺🏺🏺

10th century BC, Germany.

#OnThisDay in 9 BC, the Ara Pacis Augustae (Altar of Augustan Peace) in Rome is consecrated by Augustus. It had been commissioned by the Roman Senate four years earlier in celebration of Augustus' return to Rome after three years in Hispania and Gaul.

Also in today's adventures, Rufford Old Hall, near Ormskirk. A 500-year old Tudor house in superb condition, the extension of which is believed to be one of the first three brick buildings in Lancashire. Note the squirrel topiary 🐿️

It is extremely rare for 17th c. battlefield fortifications to survive, but here at the Thirty Years War site of Nordlingen (1634) we have the well preserved Imperial fortifications on the Allbuch hill which resisted multiple Swedish assaults, resulting in their defeat. These need protection! #lidar

The landscapes of central and northern Germany were once full of megalithic graves. Many have been destroyed to produce material for road construction in the 19th century. Lübbensteine, near Helmstedt in Lower Saxony, Germany, ca. 3500 BCE. #Archaeology🏺
Sometimes I think about how furry art is older than agriculture. This is the Lion-man. It’s 30 to 40 thousand years old, carved out of mammoth ivory and is the oldest known statue. Period.

Fibulae are the prehistoric equivalent to safety pins. In some cases fairly elaborate safety pins. Two fibulae from the early Iron Age cemetery at Hallstatt, Austria, ca. 800-400 BCE. NHM Vienna. #Archaeology 🏺
A murmuration of Roman bricks in the nave at St. Martin's, Canterbury - these walls facilitated worship by Queen Bertha of Kent and St. Augustine in the C6th. King Æthelberht was baptised here.

This is An Grainan of Aileach, 6th century ring fort. I was really lucky to grow up near this incredible structure. It offers stunning views of Inishowen. Most ring forts have been completely destroyed, and the faint reminders left on the land are called Fairy Forts. It's bad luck to remove them.

malacca is a really concentrated hit of history: 16th century portuguese church (francis xavier's home base) got taken over by the dutch and used as a dutch reformed one, then in the 18th c the dutch let it go to ruin and built a new one... that the british soon took over and reconsecrated anglican

Southampton Castle’s thirteenth century outer bailey wall. Back then, there would have been an earth bank and a ditch in front of it.

The royal castle would have been situated on a motte behind this wall. The tower block, seen here, stands on the motte, marking the site of the castle.

These crescent-shaped clay objects have been interpreted as fire dogs due to traces of burning, „moon idols“, and depictions of the sun boat due to their shape. But what if all options would be true? Objects can have multilayered symbolism and meaning. GNM Nürnberg. 1200-800 BCE. #archaeology 🏺

The Countdown to St. Patrick's Day.  This is the Giant's Causeway in County Antrim.   It's an area with 40,000 interlocking basalt columns, created over 50-million years ago. This is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.   The album cover for Led Zeppelin's "Houses of the Holy” was photographed here.

Did you know that ancient Egypt had porn? (kinda) Turin Erotic Papyrus, ca 1150 BC, found at Deir el Medina (Luxor), shows 12 vignettes, the women are beautiful+ the men have facial hair+ bellies with enormous penises (sound familiar?). This was NOT normal for ancient Egypt.

if you can get to nyc before March 3 and haven't yet seen the Africa & Byzantium show, you should go. here are details of a famous 13th c mosaic icon I'll never see again bc it's usually in the monastery of St Catherine in Sinai--completely made of little tesserae except where it's been restored.

Besides their role as tools or weapons, axes always had social or spiritual meaning - as markers of status or ritual paraphernalia. 
Gold double axe, one of 19 from the Arkalochori Cave, Crete, 1700-1600 BCE. Museum Heraklion. #Archaeology 🏺

This 2nd c. BCE Etruscan cinerary chest is a favorite of mine, due to the opulence of the figure of Vel Umrana, son of Arnth, on the lid. Wearing banqueting wreaths, he hold a patera (offering dish), and leans on tasseled cushions. Distinct pigment remains! #polychromy. 🏺 1/

British Museum
📸 my own

#AncientSiteSunday the tomb of Amyntas, c350BCE in ancient Lycia (now Fetiye, Turkey). We know the name of the owner as the exterior is inscribed “Amyntas, son of Hermagios.” Although this was a Persian satrapy pre-dating Alexander, Greek influence is clear
📷Alexey Komanov
🏺 AncientBlueSky

A genuine treat to see the recently discovered shrine with its protective snakes in Region IX in Pompeii. 
An offering of burnt figs & dates had been left on the altar, overlooked by the painted figure of the ‘genius paterfamilias’ who holds a cornucopia and patera.

Ancient Romans inscribed tombstones for their dogs. One of them reads ‘I am in tears, while carrying you to your last resting place as much as I rejoiced when bringing you home with my own hands fifteen years ago.’

Stacked Roman amphora fragments at Monte Testaccio in #Rome, essentially an ancient dumping ground for unwanted vessels (reusing not being a notable phenomenon) - these are just a small number of up to 53 MILLION vessels abandoned here in antiquity!

"I do not believe in these slanted eyes!" 🙄
this is what the BM curator of the Egyptian dept said to the seller when he refused to buy this supposed head of Akhenaten in 1927.

But the seller, I.E. Gejou, placed a photo of it in his albums of objects sold (no buyer's name). 

Anybody recognise it?

Can you guess what this charming artifact is? These wreath-crowned Greek lovers on their cushioned kline are actually a lamp-filler. The reservoir was kept filled with oil, to top off small lamps. See the hidden parts in the next tweet … 🏺 1/

#BritishMuseum 
📸 my own

Popped into Southwark Cathedral yesterday afternoon to see this beautifully curated mini-mudlarking exhibition. Loved the finds linked to Tudor Christmas feasts, and a newly discovered 13thC pilgrim badge found on the Thames Foreshore from the shrine of Our Lady in Le Puy, in the south of France.

This little blue faience monkey, ca. 1352-1336 BCE, once sported tiny gold earrings, representing a household pet. Ancient Egyptians enjoyed monkeys for their playful behavior, but they were expensive to import, and owning one indicated wealth and status.  

El Amarna, Egypt (Brooklyn Museum).

Belt buckles and bracelets made from Spondylus shells, a species living in the Mediterranean / Black Sea in depths around 15m, were between the favorite ornaments of the Linear Pottery Culture in Central Europe between 5500-4800 BCE. Historisches Museum Regensburg. #Archaeology 🏺

Twelve Days of Mortality History Christmas - Day Twelve🎄⚰️:  Mysterious miniature pine coffins with tiny figures inside. Only 8 coffins now survive of 17 discovered on Arthur’s Seat, Edinburgh in 1836. The purpose of the coffins is unknown (National Museum of Scotland) 🗃️

More Northern Isles archaeology. The Stones of Stenness, part of the Heart of Neolithic Orkney World Heritage property. Over 5,000 years old, and remains of what may be the oldest stone circle in Britain. 🏺#AncientBlueSky #UNESCO

This beautiful marble statue is of Isis. Originally, an Egyptian goddess, she became increasingly popular in Rome from the C2ndBCE onwards. Her integration into Roman culture culminated in her being one of the patron deities of the Flavian dynasty. 
🕰️c100-150CE
🏛️📷 Kunsthistoriches
🏺 AncientBlueSky

It's the #DayOfDionysos here at Erotic Mythology!

Exquisite gold offering bowl engraved with the drinking contest between Bacchus & Hercules. We know this story from art but no written myth has survived.

Patera of Rennes, Roman bowl, ca 210 CE. Today in the Bibliothèque Nationale de France, Paris

When I was recreating Vindolanda Roman fort in a gameworld a few years back, I wanted to give it life & flavor. This is the "High Street" of the vicus (town) outside the fort's west gate, at its peak ~AD235. All of these buildings are known from archaeological digs 1969-2012.

Jesus is looking very Roman, very Theodosian in fact, on this 5th cent. sarcophagus in the cathedral of #Ravenna. The sarcophagus is believed to hold the relics of St. Barbatianus, the spiritual guide of the empress Galla Placidia.

📷🇮🇹 flic.kr/p/2pgt7Hd

Death gets shredded on #MementoMoriMonday. Death, Hans Leinberger, boxwood, 1520s.
The inscription on the scroll reads SVM/QVOD/ERIS/FUI/Q’D/ES/HOC/E/E’N/OIS/HOMO/ECCLE; [Translation] I am what you will be. I was what you are: For every man is this so.
art.thewalters.org/detail/27282...

There is a LOT of Odysseus in #Pompeii, most of it war & women. But then there's a small fragment, from house at VIII.2.1 of Eurykleia washing his feet when he returns to Ithaca. It's a pivotal moment in the story, but visually such a simple, kind gesture. #ClassicsTober23 day 20 🏺

This is an agate seal stone found in the Warrior Tomb nr the Mycenaean palace at Pylos. It dates from c.1450BCE & depicts 2 warriors fighting over a slain man. It is strikingly reminiscent of scenes in Homer where warriors fight to save the body of a fallen comrade
📷Cincinnati Uni
🏺 ClassicsBlueSky

Any metalwork surviving from classical times is a rarity given how much was melted down, but this survivor really is spectacular. It’s a silver bust of Serapis. Standing only 6” high, its detail and workmanship is all the more remarkable. A masterpiece. 

🏛️📷NY Met
🕰️2nd C CE
🏺 AncientBlueSky

About 80 % of students thought this was a swanky straw hat from the 1950s. Truth is that it’s the oldest Roman grasswork "Panama hat" extant, from Fayum in Egypt. In 2017 they radiocarbon dated it to 420-568 CE. Turns out the “Dark Ages” was really more about cool shade (Pitt Rivers Museum, UK) 🎩

The Mosaic of Bacchus, discovered in 2009 in Saint-Laurent-d'Agny near Lyon, is thought to be the oldest known representation of the god of wine on a mosaic in Gaul. It was created around AD 10-14 and decorated the triclinium floor of a Roman domus. Bacchus is crowned with ivy and holding a thyrsus.

This week's #MythologyMonday theme is mirrors. Aphrodite is often depicted with a mirror, so much so that a stylised mirror became the symbol of the planet Venus and of the element copper in alchemy. Copper is associated with Aphrodite through the island of Cyprus, one of her major cult centres.
1/?

New coin: Hadrian Tetrassarion of Bizya, Thrace.
rpc.ashmus.ox.ac.uk/coins/3/1942
I visited Bizya (Vize, Turkey) in April 2023. Substantial remains of fortifications still rise, and a Roman theatre was excavated. Most of the coin types minted for Hadrian show Bizya’s fortifications on the reverse.

Standing outside the mouth of Arma dello Stefanin, July 2022. This site houses a long sequence of Upper Paleolithic, Mesolithic and Neolithic levels. Originally excavated in the 50s and 60s and then again in the 80s, my team and I have been working there for the past two years 🏺🧪

The Leaning Tower of Pisa was never straight.
        It was constructed in the 12th Century.When construction on the second story started, due to the unstable ground it was built on, the tower started to lean.

Some clay pipe bowl loveliness for you this morning. Getting harder to find complete clay pipes in the Thames, they *do* wash up but more likely you’ll find just the bowl or perhaps a partial stem with a makers’ name. This is a beauty; 19thC pipe bowl in acorn design with a tiny acorn at the heel.

A skull shaped watch which opens to reveal the dial within. The back of the skull is engraved with a familiar image - two skeletons standing alongside a large, snake-riddled skull (hourglass & crossed scythes appear too). c.1600 (Sold at Christies in 2017) #earlymodern 🗃️

Where did the mylonite in previous post come from? Right about here (-8.53623, 125.90753) where it occurs on the north side of the south-dipping Laclo Fault. The hanging wall rocks are boudinaged limestones in a thick apparent dextral shear zone. I interpret this as a folded detachment fault ⚒️

I'm usually ambivalent re white-guilt takes on "Golden Age" art, but. . . .

for a both mocking & authenticity-hungering exploiter class: white peasant fun borne by an enslaved Black person, on SE Asian shell & W African gold.

nautilus cup, Cornelis Bellekin, Amsterdam c 1700. #earlymodern #art

This fragment of a 1st c. CE frescoed wall depicts a windblown muse holding a tragic theater mask, and is likely to depict Melpomene, the muse of Tragedy. These floating figures are found throughout the homes of Pompeii and Herculaneum. 🏺 

#GettyVilla #archaeology

📸 my own

🏺 #GrindingStones appear to have been an important tool for #mobile herders 7,000 years ago.

So important, they apparently carried them along - to grind animal bones, plants and pigments:

theconversation.com/stone-age-he... via @user

#OnThisDay in AD 62, Pompeii was severely damaged by an earthquake. The event is commemorated in a bas-relief decorating the lararium of the House of Lucius Caecilius Iucundus. The relief shows damage to Pompeii's Temple of Jupiter, in which Iucundus is thought to have died.

Proof that writer’s block is nothing new ~ this Pompeiian woman with gold-threaded hair engages us as she muses over her wax tablet. She’d soon have plenty to write about, so let’s hope she survived (Pompeii, 55-79AD, unearthed 1760, National Archaeological Museum of Naples)

War as a metaphor for love was common. Sappho & Catullus both used it. Here we see this realised in mosaic. On the left Venus & beside her on the same floor mosaic, little Cupids fight as gladiators with an umpire, even ending in the gory death of one. 

🏛️ Bignor Roman Villa
📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The reliquary of the jaw of St. Anthony of Padua (Basilica del Santo, Padua) is crazy enough as it is, but in the modern times they added two light sources, to illuminate it better. The lights reflect from a glass sphere that holds the jaw, looking like ghostly eyes.

(See the 3d photo for no-eyes)

The beakhead ornament at St. Mary's  Iffley shows birds and beasts with beaks clasping the moulding of the arch. Beakheads are a classic detail of Romanesque architecture thought to originate in Henry I's reign. They are thought to represent the vices threatening our lives.

Blue Glass Beads; from late Bronze Age (3300-1200 BC). Found in 1885 when a farmer ploughed up a cremation urn at Kongehøj in Denmark.

Recent analysis shows they were made in Mesopotamia, 3100 years ago. Evidence of long-distance trade connections. 
📷 : National Museum of Denmark

#archaeohistories

Would love to recreate: 2nd cent  BCE outdoor dining room w/ huge mosaic wall featuring "vines, lotus leaves, tridents, trumpets, helmets & mythological marine creatures; mosaic scene was painstakingly created using mother of pearl, shells, corals, shards of precious glass & flecks of marble."

This 1st c. CE Gallo-Roman vessel is known as an aryballos, or (checks notes) unguentarium, which would have held perfume. Colorful enamel inlays form tendrils, rosettes, and birds. The handle terminates in two elephant heads, now missing their trunks. 🐘 #GettyVilla #Gaul

📸 my own

This magnificent, huge scallop shell mosaic was found in Roman St Albans & now resides in its Verulanium Museum. It’s an absolute gem, perfectly executed. 

Or is it? Have a look at the bottom right corner where one ‘wave’ is different. Mistake or mosaicist’s signature or joke?
📷me 🏺 AncientBlueSky

🗃️Long walk through muddy fields and leafy lanes to the magnificent Middle Littleton Tithe Barn this morning. The barn was built in around 1270, to store crops, grain & produce paid by the local people as tithes, or taxes, to the local landowner.

A Roman tombstone dedicated to a soldier named Gaius Lovesius Cadarus, who died at the age of 25 after 8 years service with the Roman Army in the Legio XX Valeria Victrix. He was originally from Emerita in Spain, but died while garrisoned in Chester (Britain).

This is the so-called Coppergate Helmet, named after its find location in York. It’s Anglo-Saxon & one of only 6 helmets surviving from that period. If you look at the nose guard, it has wonderful fantastical animals weaving through the decoration

🏛️Yorkshire Museum
📷Museum Trust
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The Manhattan schist formed from mud deposited on the ocean floor & scraped in continental collisions during the formation of Pangaea over 450 - 300 million years ago. These collisions, part of the Taconic Orogeny, created a mt chain taller than the Himalayas, & squeezed and baked the…👇🏼
🧪 geology ⚒️

Archaeology in our own village shortly ! That low grassy mound in the centre of the photo is the remains of a Bronze Age ring ditch burial mound that has been largely ploughed down. The whole field is being stripped and excavated ahead of new housing.

I can assure you there is no replica Pompeii and my photos are very much the original thing being excavated before my eyes. As for the phalluses - many were removed long before the Victorian period in the mid 18th century and some were covered up until the 1960s. But plenty remain on show

This magnificent miniature masterpiece is an early C3rdBCE tetradrachm of Lysimachos of Thrace. It depicts Alexander with the divine horns of Ammon & on the reverse Athena holding a tiny winged Nike (victory). This side also has the logo “of King Lysimachos.”
🏛️📷Chicago Art Institute
🏺 AncientBlueSky

This ceramic spoon from Mitchelstown, Co. Cork is a rare find in the archaeology of Bronze Age Ireland. It was found in a pit close to a burnt mound and is radiocarbon dated to 1916–1696 BC. 
#Life_in_Bronze_Age_Ireland
#Archaeology

#ClassicsTober23 Odysseus. Here he is, tied to the mast, to experience the Sirens without the consequential death. It’s a bit worn & a bit rustic but I love this relief. It’s C2nd BCE Etruscan so also demonstrates how far the tales of Odysseus had spread outside the Greek world

ClassicsBlueSky

This remarkable fragment of a 2nd c. CE Roman #mosaic from Carthage is featured in the exhibition ‘African & Byzantium’ at the #MetMuseum. In curator’s comments, it’s described as ‘men preparing a feast for a festival.’ To be exact, these are slaves, from all over the empire. 🏺 1/

This Byzantine ‘green man’ type figure dates from the C5th-6th. The tonality & plasticity of the skin is a testament to the vision & skill of the mosaicist. 

Also he looks like a member of Creedence Clearwater Revival. Yes, yes he does see a bad moon rising

🏛️📷 Great Palace Mosaic Museum, Istanbul

Noticed that Civil War fortifications show up in the DC gov LIDAR data. Of course they do—but it's something else to *see* the earthworks. This is Fort De Russy in upper Rock Creek Park. (Hillshaded 2022 1m DTM via DC Open Data.)

What a delightful little thing. This terracotta pig, whose back is decorated with small gobbets of coloured glass, is a child’s rattle. It’s Roman and dates to the 2nd century CE. So, if you’re ever feeling low, just think of this; it’ll stick a smile on your mush. 

🏛️📷NY Met
🏺 AncientBlueSky

A tiny skeleton rests inside the bezel of this gimmel ring and is revealed when the ring divides in two. The ring is believed to mark the engagement of Sten Sture the Younger. It is inscribed 'That god united, no one should separate' 16th cent 🗃️ #earlymodern

Evidence for long-distance trade in the Irish Neolithic. These beautiful green jadeite axes from Donegal & Westmeath are c. 6000 years old. Studies have shown that the stone they are fashioned out of comes from a series of Neolithic quarries located high in the Italian alps

Sometimes it's the simplest little pots that inspire people the most: This tiny Roman BB2 Black Burnished cooking pot was found in a child's grave at Godmanchester. Was it a toy or could it have contained a last meal, lovingly prepared for the afterlife? #archaeology ⚱️

#RuinoftheDay - The late 1st century AD bouleuterion of Iasos in Caria (southwestern Turkey). Its capacity was around 950 spectators. In Hadrian's time, the scaenae frons (the stage facade) was embellished with rich sculptural decoration depicting Dionysiac scenes.
Bronze Age sewing was carried out with bone needles. These slender-headed needles with rounded or flat-topped heads are from the Late Bronze Age Hillfort at Dún Aonghasa, Inis Mór, Co. Galway. 🏺

Most 3rd-4th c. CE Roman gold glass depicted Christian scenes. However, this piece depicts the god Oceanus reclining in the sea, indicated by wavy lines in pale-blue pigment. He holds a jar from which water pours; above are the legs of horses that probably pulled a chariot over the waves. #MetMuseum

On my recent trip to Northumberland, I stumbled across the amazing Heavenfield. Site of a 7th-century battle between the Welsh and Northumbrians, a wooden cross was erected here (with a recycled Roman altar base, now in the chapel) which was said to have miraculous properties. The view is amazing!

This big eyed terracotta figurine from Hacılar dating to mid-6th millenium BC is in my opinion one of the most exciting things at the Museum. With a smaller, long-tailed figurine on her lap, it has been interpreted as "goddess suckling a leopard".
And she sits on a seat.🏺

A multi-tool for the well-prepared Roman on their travels - containing a spoon, & a three-pronged fork, spatula, pick, spike, & knife, which all folded into the main body. It is quite like a Swiss Army knife in form (if more culinary in nature), & was made about 1700 years ago.

JC Bruce loved & supported Hadrian's Wall archaeology. But 19th C ethics were different. In 1876 they found 13,000+ Roman coins in a well. This lovely bronze eagle? He had over 500 of them melted down to make it.

Note: We may find that terrible, but that doesn't make us objectively right either. 🏺

Late Bronze Age baby-feeding vessels from Vösendorf, Austria, about 3000 years old. They have a tiny hole in the tail, so you can fill them with milk, and then an infant would hold it, and suck the milk through the tail. Ingenious and so pretty! Photo: Enver-Hirsch / Wien Museum
Most people assume Roman temples were plain gray stone, but the reality is they were often tastefully lit with Mardi Gras colors.

Santa Maria del Fiore, Florence.  More commonly known as the Duomo. The largest dome built in 1000 years

Thought I'd be obsessed with that dome, but really I can't get over the fit and finish of the tri-color marble cladding. Its patterns are visually perfect. Impossible to overstate the skill here

This is an amazing piece of beautiful Roman glass (C3rdCE), somewhat incongruously decorated with the blood & slaughter of the arena. 

We see a retiarius, Pulcher, fighting a secutor, Auriga. The other side shows a leopard in a venatio, or wild beast hunt

🏛️📷 Trier’s Landesmuseum
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Edfu temple (lucky to have visited with my dear brother): a Hellenistic temple built between 237-57BCE. It is near complete & shows how important continued Egyptian forms were to Ptolemaic architecture. Also, a direct influence on the 1836 Temple Works building, Leeds (last pic)

🏺 AncientBlueSky

A Roman helmet found nr St Albans from C1stCE. The helmet is stamped “PAPIRI” to indicate the owner, Papirus or Papirius. 

You have to suppose nothing good happened to this poor chap for him to have lost his helmet (possibly part of Boudicca’s uprising?)
🏛️Verulanium Museum
📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Coffins from Deir el-Balah, Gaza, 13c BC. It's a mix of Egyptian form (Egypt ruled that land at this time) and local Canaanite aesthetic substrate (the coffin on the left is very reminiscent of Arabic pre-Islamic idols). A lovely case of syncretic interaction of cultures

Among my all-time favorite finds from Roman Britain. The Rudge Cup, found down in Wiltshire, is a representation of Hadrian's Wall, with several of its forts listed in order!

A Wall souvenir dating to the 2nd C. Was the Wall a tourist attraction? 🏺

Tonight's goodnight image. Escomb Church, County Durham, England. The 2nd oldest intact building in all the British Isles. Built ~AD670 from stones pilfered from the nearby Roman fort at Binchester, including a complete arch and a stone marked by the Sixth Legion.

The vibe inside is amazing. 🏺

So #TIL that many of the windows in Orvieto, Italy's 14th C Cathedral are paned not in glass but in thin-sliced alabaster! The idea was to let in light but keep out some of the roasting heat.

MedievalSky

Such a rare survival - the nave ceiling at Peterborough Cathedral dates from the C13th - it has been overpainted twice but the original design remains - and original paint from the C13th has been found beneath the nail heads...

Many people come to the National Museum of Scotland to see the ~800 year old Lewis chess pieces. But I was curious: what do these little bits of walrus/whale ivory see in return?

Turns out it’s a medieval wooden harp from the Scottish highlands, plus a lovely 16th century painted wood ceiling 🏺🧪♟️

This depicts the Palace of Theodoric the Great in Ravenna. It’s in Sant’Apollinare Nuovo. Although Theodoric did much to beautify Ravenna, his image & his advisers’ were removed from this mosaic when the Byzantines took back control. If you zoom, you can still see ghostly hands on columns though…

First coin post on BlueSky!

A drahm of Wahram VI, better known as Wahram Chobin “Wooden”, general & nobleman, who usurped the Sasanian crown for a brief moment in 590-91 CE. He later took refuge at the Göktürk court & was later assassinated by agents of Khosrow II. 

Minted in Gayy (now Isfahan)

This is the Lycurgus Cup. It’s made from dichroic glass, meaning it changes colour depending on the direction the light comes from (red from behind, green from front). It shows Lycurgus’ death. He tried to kidnap Ambrosia who was turned into a vine which then strangled him
🏛️📷BM 🏺 AncientBlueSky

Journeying deep into Siberia's trackless taiga in 2019, archaeologists found forts built by hunter-gatherers 8,000 years ago. It's evidence that farming wasn't a prerequisite for complex societies – or monumental architecture – from a surprising part of the world.

Greek statue pfp account: Look at this marvellous Roman structure. Surely they were aware of the great legacy of Western Civilisation they were founding with their superior art

Ancient Romans: oh look old Pigfuckerus Maximus built something with his loot, I'm so drawing a cock and balls on it lol
An armed owl! 🦉 Ancient Greek Attic red figure pottery dated 5th-early 4th century BCE. (The owl was a symbol of Athens & the goddess Athena). The Louvre (CA2192).

OMG AncientBluesky, I just got to see the tombstone of Q. Artulus! He was a 4YO boy who died working in the Spanish mines ~2K yrs ago, commemorated with a life-size sandstone relief statue, inc. name & age. This sums up so many of the contradictions of the Roman world. I think he's a collegium kid.

A Roman lead curse tablet, which directed divine ill-will towards a man named Antonius, who is also seemingly pictured on the tablet, & named twice. The curse was laid on him about 1600-1700 years ago - but the curse sadly does not tell us what he had done... (I'd love to know!)

I still marvel at Roman engineering. Here, a lead water reservoir with a beaded decoration is fed by a single pipe and then two pipes, that can be adjusted using the valves, supply water to different parts of the Villa Arianna, Stabiae.
They thought of everything. 🏺

Chiricahua Nat. Monument (SE Arizona) has fabulous landscapes, views, and especially stone spires, pinnacles, and balanced and tumbled rocks, shaped by erosion after a cataclysmic volcanic eruption 27 million years ago covered the area with ash that hardened into rock. Gets remarkably few visitors!

Love the old and new writing formats in this 4thC mosaic of Jesus and apostles. Jesus holds a #scroll while Paul holds a #codex - perhaps representing the Old and New Testaments? Anyway, it’s an early representation of the codex in Roman Christianity. Basilica of Sant’Lorenzo, Milan #bookhistory

A terracotta Roman flask, decorated with an image of two gladiators in combat (although it looks like the murmillo gladiator on the left might be on the verge of victory, to me) - it was made about 1800 years ago, probably a mass-produced item for gladiatorial fans.

One of the earliest surviving images of Pan, this bronze  statuette (5th century BC) depicts the god with goat's head and legs but a human torso. His raised hand shields his eyes from the sun while his alert eyes suggest he is watching over a flock of sheep or goats.

#TombTuesday - The Lion Tomb in the Qasr al-Bint Nabatean Necropolis of Hegra (now in Saudi Arabia). The tomb is decorated with a carving of two felines on either side of a rosette. The name of the stonemason who carved the tomb, Aftah, is engraved above the left feline.

Are we doing FrescoFriday here on BlueSky? Well, regardless, enjoy these details from the gorgeous Garden of Livia - a beautiful Nymphaeum decorated in stunning technique to resemble a lush garden filled with birds. Now at the Palazzo Massimo museum in Rome!

(Pics are mine)

Did you know that “curiosity” is not actually inherent to the story of Pandora as it exists in the ancient stories? It just became an easy way to blame a woman for something that was divinely ordained. #classicstober23

Though I have yet to visit it, the porta nigra in modern Trier is one of my favourite Roman sites. It’s a wonderful (substantial) ruin and this early 20th century postcard of the gate makes it look even better. I love the sightseers at the bottom in their very best clothes. 

🏺 AncientBlueSky

Popped out to this late 18th/early 19th century farmhouse with the conservation officer today near Trewern, Powys. Built of hand made brick made from clay in the fields at the back ‘brick field’. They want to demolish it. It was a time capsule inside 🙂

This is believed to be the oldest depiction of Jesus.

The 'Alexamenos graffito' (singular of graffiti), is trolling a Roman soldier named Alexamenos, apparently a Christian, likely around 200 AD.

The Crosby Garrett Helmet is an extremely fine Roman cavalry helmet dating from the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD. It consists of a face mask and a headpiece in the shape of a Phrygian cap topped by a griffin. It was discovered by a metal detectorist in Cumbria (UK) in 2010.

The Great Barn at Harmondsworth is the largest standing medieval timber-framed structure in Britain.

Built in 1425, it's regarded as an outstanding example of medieval carpentry, built with the same skill and attention to detail as palaces.

No, this isn’t the earliest known representation of Sam the Eagle from the Muppets. 

It’s a bronze griffin’s head which once adorned the rim of a cauldron given as a votive offering (probably to Zeus) at Olympia. 

It’s late C7th BCE and - to be frank - is fantastic.

Good news this morning. I weighed myself and I was 188 pounds. It matters to me because I weighed 250 pounds in high school, 275 pounds after cancer, and 230 pounds 10 years ago. Been 190 for last 5 years.

The media loves to focus on quick success weight loss stories. "Lose 10 pounds in 2 months!" Kind of stuff. Good for those people. I'm here to be the person that isn't afraid to show what it's like to take 1 full year to lose 9 pounds (maybe 10 if I'm lucky).

Progress at your own pace is healthy!

re back injury: if u lift weights and take a break bc of covid, take waaaay longer to recover before returning than you think you need; im positive the reason my back got messed up is bc i started lifting again too soon. there is likely still a ton of inflammation in ur bod even though u feel normal

I think this was the first week since I separated my AC joint last summer that I've managed to wrestle for 5.5 hours (over 3 days).

Feels damn good to have my endurance back, and my shoulder is staying strong.

7 months ago I couldn't stand up without pain. I held on to walls while walking & I couldn't pick my son up from his bath. 

3 months of physical therapy & 4 months of strength training later & I'm building a gym in my garage with dreams of power lifting.

The guys who just took the top 3 in the Vuelta a Espana could crank 350+ Watts of power for 6 hours every day for three straight weeks, which is a level of strength and endurance beyond superhuman, and any one of them would get mashed like an ant 1-on-1 against a backup high-school basketball girl.

Not to be that guy, but quitting my white collar office job where I drove to work and felt dead when I got home for a job on a college campus where I bike to work and there’s a big world class gym and pool, has actually been great for my health and I’ve lost around 10lbs in only a month and half.

#ZigHealthTip If you're trying to lose weight (or gain it), do your best to weigh yourself daily. Why? Because your bodyweight can fluctuate by as much as FIFTEEN POUNDS from water retention. Daily weigh-ins allow you to extract averages. Log the weight, plot it on a graph and watch the trend.
